The temple floor tilts, uneven with fallen tile and drowned silt. Bioluminescent moss lights the Baron's coral crown, and he clangs his sword once against a broken pillar, a challenge older than the tide. Guards herd back at the sound, giving him the whole plaza.

Somewhere deep, a drowned bell answers the Baron's low roar, and guards herd forward from the dark, spines bright with anticipation. He waves them back firmly, wanting this fight entirely for himself. His trident dips once, patient and proud, before closing the distance.

Frost of deep, ancient cold clings to broken pillars in the sunken hall. The Baron's webbed fingers flex slow around his trident as he studies your stance. He steps once, twice, closing ground faster than his bulk should allow, current bending around his charge.

The current carries a torn net past your knee, drifting slow through the dark water. The Baron watches it pass, almost bored, before his gaze snaps back to you. His spear rises, catching the last bioluminescent glow, and the real fight finally starts now.

Green light pulses slow along the Baron's spine as he surfaces from the fountain's dark mouth, water sheeting off his shoulders. He points his trident once toward the guards flanking him, and they fall silently back. His attention narrows completely to you before the strike comes.

Bubbles rise thick from a crack in the flagstones, marking where the Baron's broad tail lashed hard against stone moments before. He watches you note the damage, smiling with too many teeth, and lunges before you can adjust. The current tightens like a closing fist.

The plaza's twelve pillars hum with old magic as the Baron strides unhurried through their pale light. His trident drags cold sparks off ancient stone, and he studies you a long moment, patient and curious. He has clearly done this exact fight before, many times.

A school of pale, glowing fish scatters through the ruined archway behind the advancing Baron, betraying nothing useful. He barely notices their passage, gaze fixed only on you, spear tip tracing slow circles through the drowned and heavy dark pressing close around you.

Somewhere a stone door grinds shut completely, sealing this drowned hall off from retreat. The Baron's wide grin widens further at the sound. He rolls his broad shoulders once, trident held low, and steps forward into the real fight that has been building since you entered.

The fountain hisses as something large and unseen breaks its black surface behind the advancing Baron, though he does not turn to look. His whole attention narrows completely to you instead, spear point drifting closer through the cold water pressing in around you both.

A statue's broken stone arm litters the plaza floor, a genuine tripping hazard hidden in the churned murk. The Baron steps over it easily, trident angled low, closing distance with unhurried confidence. Behind him, guards watch in silence, giving their leader this fight.

The Baron's laugh comes out warped as strange, distorted bubbles, low through the crushing underwater dark. He shakes his old, worn spear once, testing its familiar balance, then drives forward with sudden speed that closes the gap faster than his bulk should allow.

The temple's second hall lies drowned beneath drifting silt, and it is here the squid has made its den. Arms coil among the fallen columns like living rope, patient and unhurried, while one pale eye tracks the last flickering lamp. You step forward, blade drawn, knowing the current will not wait.

Bioluminescent motes drift through the flooded nave, catching on broken stone and stray current, and for a moment the ruin looks almost beautiful. Then a shadow moves against the glow, huge and deliberate, and eight arms unfold from the rubble at once. You brace against the pillar as the water itself seems to lean toward the squid's hunger.

The beak clicks twice in the crushing dark, a hard dry sound utterly wrong for a place this far underwater. You raise your shield instinctively, though you cannot yet see what you're guarding against. Then the ink comes, black and sudden, swallowing the lamp whole, and something enormous begins to move inside the cloud toward you.

A tentacle drops from the shattered ceiling without warning, thick as your whole arm and lined edge to edge with hungry suckers. It wraps the fallen statue first, testing its weight, before turning slowly, certainly, toward the warmth of your own body pressed flat against the drowned stone wall.

You feel the current shift wrong before you understand why, curling backward against the natural flow of the flooded hall. Too late you realize the squid has been breathing the water toward itself all along, drawing you closer one patient pull at a time while its great pale eye never once leaves the swinging lamp.

The squid's mantle flares crimson then fades to bruised purple, a warning written in living color across its whole vast body. You do not need to understand the language of it to know exactly what comes next. Two arms brace against the broken pillars while a third reaches, unhurried, certain of exactly where you stand.

Ink hangs in heavy ribbons through the drowned corridor long after the initial cloud should have thinned, and somewhere inside that lingering dark the squid is still moving, slow and huge and utterly silent despite its enormous size. You press your back to the cold stone and wait for the water to give you an answer.

The lamp gutters once in the crushing current, and in that half second of total dark the squid closes the last ten feet between you without a single ripple to give it away. When the light finally sputters back to life, its beak is already reaching for the space where you had been standing only a breath before.

Eight arms unfold from the rubble at once, and the whole ruined temple floor seems to come alive beneath your boots. You back slowly toward the altar, blade raised high, current dragging at your knees. There is nowhere left to retreat to now but straight through the middle of the waiting squid itself.

A sucker the size of a coin closes hard on your ankle, and the entire leg goes cold and strangely numb in an instant. You hack at the gripping arm twice before it finally releases, leaving behind a raw red ring of broken skin. Somewhere close in the dark, the beak clicks once in patient answer.

Roll for initiative: something vast has been floating silent above the flooded vault this whole time, patient and utterly unseen in the crushing gloom. Now your lamp finally catches its huge golden eye, and the long waiting between you is well and truly over. Eight arms begin, slowly, to unfold.

The current drags cold and sudden across your legs, and you know an arm has found purchase somewhere close behind you well before you ever feel the first sucker settle firmly into your skin. You spin fast, blade up, but the black water gives away nothing of where the next strike will come from.

Its eye tracks the swinging lamp rather than you, and for one long moment you allow yourself to believe you might slip past entirely unseen. Then the current shifts hard against the flooded stone, and every arm scattered through the ruined room turns slowly, deliberately, your way instead.

The beak snaps shut a foot from your face, and the sound echoes wrong and metallic through the flooded stone chamber around you. You stumble back hard against a broken column, blade raised, heart hammering loud against your ribs. Somewhere in the churning dark, four more arms begin rising from the silt.

You feel suckers find your shoulder first, and the tentacle behind them goes rigid at once, ready to haul you straight under the black water. You drive your blade hard into the gripping arm before it can tighten its hold fully, and for a moment the whole creature seems to hesitate around you.

Something huge shifts the current near the drowned altar, and every candle flame still burning in the chamber bends the same direction all at once, as though the water itself were bowing to something larger. You turn slowly toward the dark, already certain of exactly what you are about to find waiting.

The beak grinds slow against your raised shield boss, patient and testing, entirely unhurried by your rising panic in the freezing water. Behind it, two more arms are already curling wide through the murk, working together to flank you completely before you even notice they have moved.

A sucker mark blooms angry red across your forearm where the crushing grip finally let go only a moment ago. You have maybe a single ragged breath left before the next arm finds you again somewhere in the churning, freezing dark water pressing hard against every side of the ruined hall.

The temple floor tilts hard beneath you as the squid drags its full immense bulk over the broken archway toward you, ancient stone cracking loudly under the sheer weight of it. You brace low, weapon raised high, refusing outright to run from something this patient and this hungry.

Its arms find the rusted lamp chain and yank down hard without warning, and the light swings wild across the entire flooded hall, throwing huge shifting shadows that hide far more arms than you could ever hope to properly count in the crushing dark surrounding you.

A tentacle threads slowly through the shattered window frame and taps once against the ancient altar stone, testing its considerable weight before curling fully around it and hauling the whole heavy thing free with barely any visible effort at all, stone grinding loud against stone as it goes.

You hear the beak before you ever actually see it, a single dry click swallowed fast by the crushing weight of water pressing hard against your ears. Then the solid dark directly in front of you simply, silently, begins to move, closer with every passing heartbeat you spend frozen in place.

The squid rises slowly up past the broken ceiling, arms trailing behind it like drowned rope through the endless gloom, and for one long terrible moment the whole ruined chamber seems far too small to properly hold its enormous, waterlogged bulk as it settles into the flooded nave.

Bioluminescent spots race bright along its rippling mantle, a clear warning flare an instant before all its arms strike at once, faster than anything that size should ever reasonably be able to move through the crushing weight of underwater current bearing down on the drowned hall.

The ink cloud finally thins just enough to reveal one huge unblinking eye, fixed steady on your guttering torch, and you slowly realize with a cold certainty that it has been silently watching you far longer than you ever actually knew, patient as the drowned stone around it.

A tentacle wraps tight around the last standing pillar, and the whole ancient structure groans loudly under its crushing weight, dust and loosened grit sifting slowly down through water gone suddenly murky, cold, and utterly unreadable around you, hiding whatever comes next.

You feel the grip before you even feel the pain, cold rubbery bands closing hard around your calf and dragging you off balance toward the waiting black water pooling somewhere unseen below the broken temple floor beneath your feet, the beak already clicking somewhere close.

Its arms spread wide across the entire sunken nave, blocking every last remaining path back toward the stair, and you understand at once, with total clarity, that there is only one way left now to fight this whole encounter out to its bitter, dripping end.

The squid's eye rolls slowly toward the swinging lamp, and its whole huge body follows a heartbeat later, arms trailing thick silt and broken stone in a long wake across the entire flooded floor of the drowned temple hall, closing the distance fast.

You brace hard against the pulling current as a tentacle sweeps low through the murk, hunting patiently for your footing among the loose scattered stones covering the whole drowned temple floor beneath the crushing dark water, close enough now to touch.

Its beak rasps hard against your raised blade twice in quick succession, testing the edge carefully before finally committing its considerable full weight behind a third strike aimed straight for your unguarded, exposed chest, water surging with the force of it.

The squid's mantle pulses once, bright gold against the surrounding endless gloom, and every arm scattered through the flooded chamber tightens at exactly the same instant, as though the whole vast creature had simply exhaled all at once, readying itself to strike.

The passage floods knee deep before you've gone ten steps, cold climbing fast toward your chest. Somewhere above, bioluminescent moss lights a broken archway in dim blue, and beneath it a webbed hand traces slow circles. The current answers instantly, curling tight around your ankles like it already knows where you'll stumble.

You descend past drowned pillars carved with waves that seem to move in the failing light. The wavebender stands rooted at the altar ahead, crown dripping, arms loose at her sides while the water itself gathers around her shoulders. She has not moved, and yet somehow every step you take brings her closer, patient as the tide.

Bubbles stream past your mask in a slow silver column as pressure builds behind your eyes. Ahead, a shape glides against the current instead of with it, unhurried, watching you struggle to keep your footing on silt gone suddenly treacherous. The whole drowned hall seems to lean in, holding its breath along with you.

A low groan of stone rolls through the flooded corridor, shaking loose dust from a ceiling that has held for centuries. Something shifts near the coral altar, calm and utterly deliberate. When the wavebender finally turns to face you, the current bends first, rushing ahead of her like an eager, loyal thing.

The last of your torchlight gutters against the crushing dark, and for a moment you're certain it will die completely. A ripple answers from somewhere close, slow and precise. Then blue light blooms along a crowned silhouette, and you understand the wavebender decided how this fight would go long before you arrived.

Cold water floods your boots as the temple floor gives slightly, unstable after centuries beneath the sea. Somewhere close a current hums with plain, unmistakable intent, curling around broken columns like it's searching for a way in. The crowned shape watching from the dark has not blinked once since you entered.

You round the last pillar and the water goes suddenly, unnervingly still. Then it moves all at once, curling toward a figure waiting calmly at the center of the flooded chamber, crown catching what little light remains. The wavebender lets the silence stretch, patient as only something ancient can afford to be.

The drowned stairs spiral down into colder, darker water than anything above. Faint blue light pulses at the bottom, rhythmic as a heartbeat, and something waits there that has clearly been waiting a very long time. Every step down tightens the current around your legs, gentle at first, then insistent.

Pressure aches behind your eyes as the temple deepens, the dark pressing in from every side like a held breath. A pale shape glides along the vaulted ceiling, trailing threads of light that never quite touch the water below. It watches you the way a tide studies a shrinking shore, unhurried and certain.

A shaft of pale light finds the coral altar first, cutting a narrow path through the murk. The wavebender stands within it, current wrapped tight around one wrist like a coiled rope she could release at any moment. She watches your approach as though it were already written somewhere in the drowned stone.

The corridor floods faster than you expected, cold biting hard through every seam of your gear. Ahead, a shape glides calmly against the current instead of with it, patient while you struggle to keep pace. Somewhere behind her, drowned banners drift overhead, torn and long since colorless in the gloom.

You feel the current before you see it, curling gently around your calves like a leash tightening by degrees. At the far end of the flooded hall, the wavebender waits with arms loose, letting the water obey every slow breath she takes. The whole temple seems to hold its breath along with her.

Coral crunches underfoot as you edge past a broken statue face down in centuries of silt. The water thickens around you, resisting each step like it has a will of its own. Ahead, a crowned shape turns to face you, patient and utterly unafraid, current already spooling from her open fingers.

The temple opens suddenly into a flooded chamber ringed with empty coral thrones, save for one. The wavebender rises from it unhurried, current gathering around her shoulders like a held breath finally released. Somewhere above, the last daylight fails completely, and the blue glow becomes the only light left.

Your light catches a crown half buried in drifting silt, then the still face beneath it. Eyes open slowly, deliberately, as though waking has always been a choice rather than a necessity. The wavebender rises without hurry, current already curling tight at her feet like a loyal, waiting animal.

A drowned bell hangs silent overhead, coral crusted and forgotten by everyone but the water. Beneath it waits the wavebender, arms open in something almost like welcome, current already rising to meet your very first step forward. The whole hall seems built around this one moment of arrival.

The passage floods your lungs with pressure and biting cold long before you reach the altar. Ahead, blue light traces a crowned silhouette against the crushing dark, unmoving in a way that somehow unsettles more than motion would. You realize she has simply been waiting for you to notice her first.

You step over a fallen spear, rusted through and abandoned in the silt. Whoever carried it met the wavebender before you did, and did not carry it out again. The current around you tightens now, patient and unhurried, as though it has all the time in the world to finish what it started.

The temple's deepest hall glows faint and blue, lit by something quietly, patiently alive. At its heart stands the wavebender, current curling around her shoulders like an old and loyal pet finally returning home. She watches you cross the threshold without so much as raising a hand.

A ripple crosses the vaulted ceiling above you, wrong in a way you can't quite name, far too deliberate to be current alone. You look down just as the water beneath your feet tightens its grip. The wavebender has already decided, calmly and completely, exactly where you're meant to stand.

The corridor bends toward a soft blue glow that seems to breathe in and out with the current. Something large moves just beyond the light, unhurried, testing the water before it commits. When it finally steps forward, the tide bends first, and only afterward does the wavebender follow.

You feel the pressure change before the current even does, a warning your body understands before your mind catches up. A slow, deliberate pull tugs hard at your boots, testing your balance. Ahead, the wavebender watches calmly, letting the temple itself soften you before she moves at all.

The last doorway opens onto black water lit by slow, drifting motes of light like dying stars. At its center a crowned shape turns toward you, current gathering at her fingertips like a breath about to be released all at once. The whole drowned world seems to lean toward that single held moment.

A current coils lazily around a fallen sword, dragging it slowly across the silt floor like a curious hand testing a toy. You look up sharply, heart hammering against your ribs. The wavebender is already closer than she was only moments ago, and you never once saw her actually move an inch.

The altar chamber hums with a low, ancient song that only the water itself seems to remember clearly. The wavebender waits at its center, crown dripping steady light, current already coiling tight for whatever comes first. You get the sense she has performed this exact moment countless times before.

You duck beneath a drowned lintel and feel the water tighten sharply around your legs, testing. Ahead, a pale figure raises one webbed hand, unhurried, and the whole temple seems to lean in closer to watch what happens next. Somewhere behind her, coral thrones sit empty and waiting, patient as she is.

Bioluminescence flickers along a fallen archway, tracing a narrow, deliberate path through the gloom. At its end waits the wavebender, current curling around her shoulders like a cloak she wears without any visible effort. She has clearly been standing there far longer than the light suggests.

The floor drops away into a flooded shaft, lit faintly by veins of living coral along the walls. At its bottom the wavebender stands rooted, current spiraling upward around her like smoke made of living, breathing water. Every fish in sight has already fled the chamber ahead of you.

The dark presses close as the corridor bends steadily, relentlessly downward, swallowing your torchlight a little more with every turn. A pulse of blue answers from somewhere below, rhythmic and patient. Something ancient rises slowly from the drowned floor, entirely unhurried, to finally meet you.

A soundless wave rolls through the flooded hall, bending the water sideways hard enough to stagger your footing. You brace against the pull with both hands on cold stone. Ahead, something crowned and infinitely patient simply waits for you to lose your balance completely.

Your torch throws long, wavering shadows across drowned statues lining the ancient hall. One of them blinks, slow and deliberate, and you nearly drop the torch entirely. The wavebender steps free of the stone itself, current uncoiling from her wrist like a rope that has always been waiting to be used.

Silt clouds the passage ahead, stirred by something large moving just out of sight. When it finally settles, the wavebender stands revealed at the altar, crown glinting faintly, current coiled tight like a spring beside her, ready to release at the smallest provocation from you.
12: The descent into the sunken temple takes you past columns wrapped in pale, glowing moss, the only light in a world gone completely black beyond your reach. Cold water presses at your chest with every step deeper, and the silence has a texture to it, thick and expectant, like the whole drowned building is holding its breath and waiting for you to make the first mistake. Then the water shudders, and a shape the size of a fallen tree cuts across the flooded hall ahead, jaws already opening. You plant your feet in the silt, raise your weapon, and meet the giant shark's first rush with everything you have, feeling the impact rattle up through your arms and into your chest.

You have fought in cold water before, but nothing prepares you for the moment the shark's shadow finally swallows the last of the light above the drowned altar. Its bulk fills the corridor completely, blocking any hope of retreat, and for one long breath you simply stand there, weapon raised, waiting to see which way it will finally turn. It chooses the direct approach, surging forward with a speed that seems impossible for something so large. You throw yourself sideways into the current, boots dragging against slick, ancient tile, and answer its passing rush with a strike aimed for the softer flesh just behind its jaw.

The temple corridor narrows here, forcing anything that hunts inside it into a single straight line, and you use that fact deliberately, backing into the tightest stretch you can find. When the giant shark finally comes for you, it has no room left to circle or feint, only to charge straight down the throat of the passage toward where you stand waiting. You brace against a fallen column, time your breath to the rhythm of the current, and meet the charge head on rather than dodge it. The impact staggers you hard, driving you back a full step, but your blade finds purchase in that same instant, and steaming blood clouds the water between you both.

Bioluminescent fungus lights the drowned hall in soft, shifting patches of blue and green, throwing strange shadows across broken statues that once guarded this place. You move carefully between them, weapon ready, aware that the giant shark could be resting in any one of the deeper pools of darkness between the pale light. It reveals itself with a sudden surge from behind a collapsed pillar, jaws already closing on the space you occupied a heartbeat earlier. You are already moving, twisting hard against the drag of the current, and your counterstrike lands solid as its momentum carries it past you into the open water beyond.

The current here runs strange, curling in slow spirals around broken pillars that have not felt open air in centuries, stirring silt into pale, drifting clouds. You watch the water closely, reading its subtle motion for any sign of the giant shark's approach before your own eyes can catch the shape itself. The telltale shudder comes at last, and you brace hard against a nearby column just as the enormous shadow finally breaks from the gloom. Its rush is fast and sure, but you have already committed to your stance, and you meet the charge with steel raised, feeling the shock of impact travel straight through your bones.

Deeper into the drowned temple, the water grows colder still, pressing at your ribs with a weight that feels almost physical, almost deliberate. Somewhere ahead, past a curtain of drifting silt, something vast stirs the murk, and you know without needing to see it clearly that the giant shark has already caught your scent. You do not wait for it to choose the moment of attack. Instead you push forward through the cold current, weapon leading the way, and when the shark finally breaks from cover with jaws wide, you are already committed to the strike, meeting its rush before it can fully close the gap between you.

A dozen scars mark the broken columns of this hall, souvenirs of hunts that ended badly for whatever came before you, scraped deep into ancient stone by countless failed strikes. You study them for only a moment before turning your attention back to the drowned corridor ahead, weapon ready, refusing to become the next set of marks on the wall. The giant shark announces itself with a slam against the far wall, testing the water for your exact position with practiced patience. You use that single moment of noise to reposition fast, closing the distance while its attention is elsewhere, and strike hard from the blind side it never expected you to reach.

The sunken altar sits at the heart of the drowned hall, surrounded by broken statues worn smooth by centuries of slow current, and something about the stillness here feels deliberately wrong. You approach carefully anyway, weapon ready, watching the shifting shadows between the pale glow of scattered moss for any sign of movement. The giant shark erupts from behind the altar itself, closer than you thought possible, jaws already wide and closing fast. You throw yourself hard to the side, feeling the rush of displaced water drag at your armor, and answer its passing charge with a fast, desperate strike of your own.

Cold water floods the corridor ahead of you in slow, deliberate waves, each one a fraction colder than the last, marking the giant shark's approach long before you can see its shape in the gloom. You brace against a broken pillar, steady your breathing, and wait with real patience for the moment it finally commits. When it comes, the charge is sudden and overwhelming, closing the distance faster than you expected despite your preparation. You meet it anyway, driving your weapon forward into the rush of teeth and muscle, and the impact staggers you both, sending clouds of silt billowing through the water around you.

The drowned hall stretches long and dark ahead of you, its ceiling lost somewhere above in shadow, its floor buried under centuries of silt and broken stone. You move carefully, weapon ready, listening past the constant pressure of the water for any sign of the giant shark's approach through the gloom. It finds you first anyway, surging from a collapsed archway with a speed that closes the gap before you can fully react. You twist hard against the current, feeling your boots skid on the slick tile beneath you, and drive your weapon up into its exposed flank as it passes close enough to touch.

Somewhere past the broken archway, the water itself seems to hold still, an unnatural calm you have learned to distrust more than any obvious threat. You raise your weapon and wait, feet planted firm in the silt, watching the darkness for the shape you know is already circling somewhere just out of sight. The stillness breaks all at once, and the giant shark surges forward with jaws wide, closing the distance in a single powerful burst. You meet the charge head on, bracing hard against the impact, and your blade finds its mark just as the current threatens to sweep you both away entirely.

You press on regardless, weapon ready, aware that the giant shark somewhere ahead has likely already heard every step you have taken through this drowned ruin. The Sahuagin Baron holds court in a drowned plaza where twelve broken statues stand like a leaderless guard, weed sulking green from their hollow eyes. The second branch of the temple opens into a drowned nave where the ceiling has long since given way, and pale surface light filters down through drifting silt like something almost sacred. Then the squid comes on all at once, arms spreading wide to block every remaining path back toward the stair. You plant your feet against the current and swing as the first tentacle closes the distance, suckers already flexing to grip whatever they touch. The beak clicks somewhere behind the reaching arm, patient, certain, entirely unhurried by the fight it has clearly already decided it will win.

You feel the pull before you understand it, a slow steady tug against your whole body that has nothing to do with any natural current in the drowned hall. By the time you realize an arm has coiled around the fallen statue behind you, using it as an anchor point, it is already reaching for you directly, unhurried and utterly certain. The beak clicks twice in the crushing dark, dry and hungry, close enough now that you can feel the vibration of it through the water pressing against your ears. You strike blind toward the sound and connect with something solid, and the whole chamber seems to shudder in answer. Every arm in the flooded nave tightens at once, as though the creature itself had simply inhaled in surprise.

Roll for initiative: something vast has been floating above the drowned vault this whole time, patient and unseen behind the drifting curtain of silt and bioluminescent motes. Now your lamp catches its huge golden eye at last, and the long stillness between you finally breaks. Eight arms begin, slowly, deliberately, to unfold from the rubble where they had been coiled and waiting. You do not wait for the first strike. You drive forward under the swinging lamp light, blade angled for the beak, and the squid answers by flaring its whole mantle crimson, a warning it clearly expects you to heed. You do not, and the water around you both goes suddenly, violently still before the real fight begins in earnest.

Bioluminescent spots race bright along the squid's rippling mantle a heartbeat before all eight arms strike together, faster than anything that size should reasonably move through crushing water. You throw yourself sideways, feeling the rush of displaced current shove past where your chest had been only a moment earlier, close enough to steal the breath from your lungs. The near miss buys you exactly one heartbeat, and you use it well, driving your blade hard into the nearest reaching arm before it can recoil. The squid's mantle darkens to bruised purple, and for a moment every remaining tentacle in the flooded hall goes rigid, as if the whole creature were reconsidering just how easy this meal was actually going to be.

The lamp gutters once in the current, and in that half second of total dark the squid closes the last ten feet between you without a single ripple to betray it. When the flame sputters back to life, its beak is already reaching for the space where you had been standing, and you barely twist clear in time. You feel suckers brush your shoulder as the arm overshoots, and you drive your blade hard along its length before it can recover and grip properly. Ink sprays thick through the water, mixing with something darker, and the squid recoils with a violence that rocks loose stone free from the drowned ceiling above you both.

The temple floor tilts hard beneath your boots as the squid drags its full immense bulk over the broken archway, ancient stone cracking loudly under the sheer weight bearing down on it. You brace low against a fallen pillar, weapon raised, and refuse to give ground even as the current surges hard against your legs, urging you back toward the stair you have already decided not to take. Its arms find the rusted lamp chain first and yank down without warning, and the light swings wild across the whole flooded hall, throwing huge shifting shadows that hide far more reaching arms than you could ever hope to properly count. You fight by feel as much as sight now, striking at every shadow that moves wrong against the drowned stone walls around you.

A tentacle threads slowly through the shattered window frame high above the nave and taps once against the ancient altar stone, testing its considerable weight with an almost curious patience. Then it curls fully around the stone and hauls the whole heavy thing free, grinding loud against the flooded floor as it drags the makeshift barrier out of its own way entirely. You watch, blade ready, as the squid clears the last obstacle between itself and you with barely any visible effort at all. The message is clear enough without words: nothing in this drowned hall is heavy enough to slow it down, and nothing short of your own blade will convince it otherwise before this fight is finally settled.

The squid rises slowly up past the broken ceiling, arms trailing behind it like drowned rope through the endless gloom, and for one long terrible moment the whole ruined chamber seems far too small to properly contain its enormous, waterlogged bulk. Bioluminescent spots flare along its mantle, lighting the drowned nave in flickering gold and violet for just long enough to show you exactly how many arms you are actually facing. You count eight, then stop counting, because counting will not help you here. Instead you plant your feet against the pulling current and wait for the first one to commit, blade angled low, breath held tight in your chest as the vast shape settles fully into the flooded hall around you both.

The ink cloud thins just enough to reveal one huge unblinking eye, fixed steady on your guttering torch, and you realize with a cold certainty that it has been silently watching you far longer than you ever actually knew. Somewhere behind that patient gaze, eight arms are already repositioning, quiet and unhurried, closing the last of the distance between hunger and its meal. You strike first, driving hard for the beak hidden beneath that watching eye, and the water around you both erupts into sudden violent motion. Arms lash out in every direction at once, and you fight blind through ink and current alike, trusting your blade more than your eyes in the crushing dark that swallows the drowned nave whole.

A tentacle wraps tight around the last standing pillar in the flooded hall, and the whole ancient structure groans loudly under the crushing weight bearing down on it. Dust and loosened grit sift slowly through water gone suddenly murky and cold, and you realize the squid is not merely hunting you now but methodically clearing the room of anything that might offer you cover or escape. You duck behind a smaller block of fallen masonry as the pillar finally gives way entirely, crashing down in a cloud of silt and broken stone. The squid pauses, considering, and in that brief stillness you finally see the shape of its full body settled low against the drowned altar, patient, massive, and utterly unbothered by the ruin it has just caused.

The current drags cold and sudden across your legs, and you know an arm has found purchase somewhere close behind you well before you ever feel the first sucker settle firmly into your skin. You spin fast, blade already rising, but the black water gives away nothing useful about where the next strike will actually come from in this drowned, crushing dark. Then the beak snaps shut a foot from your face, and the sound echoes wrong through the whole flooded chamber, sharp and dry against the endless pressure of water on every side. You stumble back hard against broken stone, heart hammering, and realize the squid has been herding you toward the altar this entire time, patient and utterly deliberate about it.

Its eye tracks the swinging lamp rather than you, and for one long moment you allow yourself to believe you might slip past entirely unseen through the drowned nave. Then the current shifts hard against the flooded stone floor, and every arm scattered through the ruined room turns slowly, deliberately, in your direction instead, as if the whole creature had simply changed its mind about which target mattered more. You do not wait to find out if that belief was ever true. You move fast for the nearest cover, a half collapsed archway thick with drifting silt, and press yourself flat against stone while eight arms sweep the water behind you, searching with a patience that feels far more unsettling than any sudden violent lunge would have been.

Something huge shifts the current near the drowned altar, and every candle flame still somehow burning in the ruined chamber bends the same direction all at once, as though the water itself were bowing low to something far larger than you. You turn slowly toward the dark, already certain, with a cold and settling dread, of exactly what you are about to find waiting there for you. The squid meets your gaze without hurry, one huge golden eye catching the last of the flickering light before the ink comes, sudden and total, and swallows the drowned nave whole. You raise your blade into the blinding dark and wait for the first arm to close the remaining distance between you.

Cold suckers drag slow along your back as a passing arm carries by, missing you by mere inches in the churning water, and you spin fast to face whatever black shape it emerged from a moment earlier. The squid's mantle pulses once, bright gold against the surrounding gloom, and every remaining arm in the flooded chamber tightens at exactly the same instant, as though the whole vast creature had simply exhaled. You do not wait for the exhale to become a strike. You drive forward under the nearest reaching tentacle, blade angled hard for the pale underside where the suckers thin out near the mantle itself, and the water around you both erupts into a sudden churning violence that rattles loose stone free from every drowned wall in the ruined hall.

A single sucker print, wet and perfect, marks the cold stone where an arm just released its grip on a fallen pillar a moment ago, and you know already it is reaching for something else nearby instead of retreating. You track the ripple of displaced water back toward the drowned altar, blade ready, refusing to let the dark hide where it has actually gone. Then the current surges hard and sudden against your legs, and you understand the squid has simply repositioned itself directly behind you while your attention was fixed elsewhere. You spin fast, driving your blade up in a wide arc, and connect with something solid and rubbery an instant before the beak can close on anything more vital than empty water.

The beak flashes pale in the crushing dark, snapping shut hard on empty water exactly where your leg had been only a heartbeat earlier, close enough that you feel the pressure wave of it against your skin. You do not wait around to see if it decides to try again immediately, driving forward instead while the squid's attention is still fixed on the space you have already left behind. Your blade finds the beak's soft edge this time, and the whole vast creature recoils hard, mantle flaring bruised purple through the drifting ink. For one long moment every arm in the drowned hall goes still, as if reconsidering, before the current shifts again and the fight resumes in earnest, faster and far angrier than before.

A tentacle rises slow from the black water like some drowned ceremonial banner, streaming thick ink and silt behind it as it climbs toward the last flickering lamp. Beneath it two pale ancient eyes finally turn together, fixed on the small warm shape moving carefully across the flooded temple floor. You do not run. Running only tells a hunting thing exactly where to look next, so instead you press yourself into the shadow of a broken column and wait, blade ready, while the vast shape settles closer through the drifting silt and gathering dark, patient as the stone around you.

The squid hunts by touch as much as sight, arms fanned wide across the flooded nave, feeling patiently for warmth in every crevice and shadow. You hold your breath and press flat against the cold stone wall, praying the current carries your scent somewhere other than toward the reaching arms sweeping slow across the drowned floor. Then one arm brushes the very edge of your boot, and you know the moment for hiding has passed entirely. You strike hard and fast before it can tighten its grip, and the whole vast creature recoils with a violence that sends broken stone tumbling loose from the ceiling above you both.

Four arms rise together from the silt at once, dripping black ink, reaching for the last dry ledge in the drowned hall where you have taken refuge. You plant your feet firm against the current and swing before they can close the remaining gap, blade catching the nearest tentacle just below its ring of grasping suckers. The squid recoils, mantle flaring an angry crimson, and for one long moment the whole flooded chamber seems to hold its breath along with you. Then the beak clicks twice in quick succession, patient and testing, and you understand the fight has only just properly begun.

You feel suckers find your shoulder first, cold and sudden, and the tentacle behind them goes rigid at once, ready to haul you straight under the black water pooling somewhere below the broken temple floor. You drive your blade hard into the gripping arm before it can tighten its hold fully, and the whole creature shudders in answer through the crushing current. Ink sprays thick where the blade connects, clouding the water instantly, and you lose sight of everything but vague shifting shapes moving through the murk around you. You fight by feel now, trusting the weight and resistance of your blade more than any sight the ruined temple still allows you.

The beak grinds slow against your raised shield boss, patient, testing, entirely unhurried by your rising panic in the freezing water. Behind it, two more arms are already curling wide through the murk, working together in eerie coordination to flank you completely before you even notice they have moved position. You break away hard, driving for the nearest broken pillar, and manage to put stone between yourself and one grasping arm just as it closes on empty water. The squid's huge eye tracks you the whole way, patient and utterly unbothered by the momentary setback of losing its intended grip.

Something huge shifts the current near the drowned altar stone, and every candle flame still somehow burning in the ruined chamber bends the same direction all at once, as though the water itself were bowing low to something far larger than anything else in the flooded hall. You turn slowly toward the gathering dark, already certain of exactly what waits there. The squid meets your gaze without any hurry at all, one huge golden eye catching the last of the flickering lamplight before the ink comes, sudden and total, swallowing the drowned nave whole. You raise your blade into the blinding black and brace for the first arm to finally close the distance.

A sucker the size of a coin closes hard on your ankle, and the whole leg goes cold and strangely numb in an instant, the cold spreading fast up through your knee. You hack at the gripping arm twice before it finally releases, leaving behind a raw red ring of broken skin and a fresh cloud of blood mixing slow into the drifting ink. The squid recoils from the wound, mantle darkening to bruised purple, and for a moment you think it might actually retreat into the deeper dark. Instead it simply repositions, patient as ever, and you realize this fight will only end when one of you can no longer keep fighting it at all.

The lamp gutters hard in the pulling current, and in that half second of total dark the squid closes what little distance remained between you without so much as a single ripple to give it away. When the flame finally sputters back to unsteady life, its beak is already reaching for the space where you had been standing only a breath before. You throw yourself sideways into the murk, rolling hard across broken stone, and come up already swinging at whatever shape the dark suggests is closest. Your blade connects with something solid and rubbery, and the whole vast creature shudders, mantle flaring bright gold in sudden, furious answer.

Its arms find the rusted lamp chain first and yank down hard without any warning at all, and the light swings wild across the whole flooded hall, throwing huge shifting shadows that hide far more reaching arms than you could ever hope to properly count in the crushing underwater dark. You fight by feel as much as sight now, striking at every shadow that moves wrong against the drowned stone walls, trusting your blade far more than your eyes in a room this thoroughly claimed by ink and current and the vast patient hunger circling somewhere close within it.

The temple floor tilts hard beneath your boots as the squid drags its full immense bulk over a broken archway toward the last dry ledge, ancient stone cracking loudly under the sheer crushing weight of it. You brace low against a fallen pillar, weapon raised high, and refuse outright to give any further ground. The current surges hard against your legs in answer, urging you back toward the stair you have already decided you will not take. Instead you plant your feet and wait, blade ready, for the vast shape to finally commit itself fully to the reach it has been patiently building toward this whole time.

A single sucker print, wet and perfect, marks the cold stone where an arm just released its grip on a fallen pillar only a moment ago. You track the ripple of displaced water back toward the drowned altar, blade ready, refusing to let the gathering dark hide exactly where the creature has actually gone. Then the current surges again, hard and sudden against your legs, and you understand at once that the squid has simply repositioned itself directly behind you while your attention stayed fixed elsewhere. You spin fast, driving your blade up in a wide desperate arc, and connect solidly with something far more vital than empty water this time.

Its arms spread wide across the entire sunken nave, blocking every last remaining path back toward the stair, and you understand at once, with total clarity, that there is only one way left now to settle this whole encounter. You grip your blade tighter and step forward instead of back. The squid's eye rolls slowly toward you, and its whole huge body follows a heartbeat later, arms trailing thick silt and broken stone in a long wake across the entire flooded floor. You meet the first reaching tentacle with a hard downward cut, and the fight finally, properly begins.

You brace hard against the pulling current as a tentacle sweeps low through the murk, hunting patiently for your footing among the loose scattered stones covering the whole drowned temple floor. It finds purchase on a fallen block instead of your leg, and you use the half second it costs the squid to drive in close. Your blade opens a long shallow line along the arm before it can properly recover, and dark blood threads out into the current alongside the last of the drifting ink. The squid answers with a furious tightening of every remaining limb, and the whole flooded chamber seems to close in around you both.

The beak rasps hard against your raised blade twice in quick succession, testing the edge carefully before finally committing its considerable full weight behind a third strike. You catch it on your shield instead of your chest, and the impact rattles all the way up through your bracing arm. The squid's mantle pulses once more, bright gold against the surrounding gloom, and you recognize the pattern now, the same flare that comes right before it commits fully to a strike. You move first this time, driving hard for the beak while it is still gathering itself to attack.

The corridor floods slowly at first, water creeping up past your knees like it's testing whether you'll turn back. Bioluminescent moss lines the cracked walls, throwing soft blue light across drowned murals of a court long since silenced by the sea. Somewhere ahead, a current shifts with unmistakable purpose, curling around broken columns like a hand feeling for a grip. You press forward anyway, torch guttering against the pressure. At the end of the hall, a crowned figure waits at the altar, webbed fingers laced together, patient as only something ancient can afford to be. The wavebender does not turn to greet you. She simply lets the water do it for her, tightening around your ankles like a leash finally pulled taut.

Cold settles into your bones long before the corridor opens into the flooded chamber proper. Drowned banners drift overhead, torn and colorless, stirred by a current that seems to move against every natural rule you know. Fish scatter past you in a panicked shoal, fleeing something you have not yet seen, and the silence that follows feels heavier than the water itself. Then she rises from behind a broken coral throne, unhurried, crown catching the last of your fading torchlight. The wavebender studies you the way a tide studies a shrinking shore, patient and utterly certain of the outcome. Around her wrist, current coils like a rope she has not yet chosen to release, and you understand that choice is coming very soon.

The temple's deepest hall glows faint and blue, lit by veins of coral that seem to breathe with each slow pulse of light. You wade forward against a resistance the water shouldn't have, boots dragging through silt gone thick with centuries of silence. Somewhere close, a low groan of stone answers your footsteps, deliberate in a way that stone alone never manages. At the center of it all stands the wavebender, arms loose at her sides while current gathers around her shoulders like a held breath. She has been waiting long before you arrived, and the whole drowned chamber seems arranged entirely around this one moment of your arrival. When she finally turns to face you, the water turns with her, obedient and eager.

You duck beneath a drowned archway choked with black kelp, feeling the temperature drop another degree with every step deeper. Pressure builds behind your eyes, insistent and strange, as though the sea itself is leaning in to listen. Ahead, a pale shape glides along the vaulted ceiling, trailing threads of light that never quite touch the water below. It watches you the way a predator watches something it has already decided to take. When you finally reach the flooded altar chamber, the wavebender is already waiting, webbed hand raised in something that might be greeting or might be warning. The current curls tight around her fingers, patient and coiled, ready for whatever you choose to do next in this quiet, drowned place.

The stairs spiral down into darker water than anything you've crossed so far, each step colder than the last. Faint blue light pulses steadily from somewhere below, rhythmic as a heartbeat you can almost feel through the stone beneath your palms. Whatever waits at the bottom has clearly been waiting there a very long time, patient in a way only ancient things manage. When you finally reach the flooded floor, the wavebender stands rooted at its center, current spiraling upward around her like smoke made of living water. She does not rush to meet you. Instead she lets the temple do the work first, softening your footing, tightening the current, making certain the ground itself favors her long before the fight even begins.

A soundless wave rolls through the corridor ahead of you, bending the water sideways hard enough to stagger your footing against cold stone. You brace with both hands, torch nearly guttering out completely in the sudden turbulence. Something crowned and utterly patient waits just beyond the disturbance, content to let the temple itself do the softening for her. When the water finally stills, you see her clearly for the first time: the wavebender, arms loose, current curling obediently around her wrist like a loyal animal returning home. She studies your approach without a trace of urgency, as though this exact moment has already played out countless times before, in exactly this order, with exactly this outcome.

Silt clouds the passage thickly, stirred by something large moving just beyond the reach of your failing torchlight. You press forward carefully, feeling the current tighten around your legs with each step, testing your balance the way water tests every weakness it finds. When the silt finally settles, the wavebender stands revealed at the coral altar, crown glinting faintly in the gloom. Current coils at her side like a spring wound tight, ready to release at the smallest provocation from you. She has not moved since the silt began to clear, and yet somehow the distance between you feels smaller than it should. The whole drowned chamber seems to hold its breath, waiting to see which of you moves first.

Bioluminescence flickers weakly along a fallen archway, tracing a narrow and deliberate path through water gone thick with centuries of silt. At its end waits the wavebender, current curling around her shoulders like a cloak she wears without any visible effort at all. She has clearly been standing in that exact spot far longer than the fragile light suggests. You approach slowly, boots dragging against resistance that shouldn't exist in ordinary water. When you're close enough to see her eyes clearly, she finally speaks without words, simply raising one webbed hand, and the current answers instantly, curling tight around your ankles like it has always known exactly where you would eventually stand.

The floor drops away into a flooded shaft you didn't expect, lit faintly by veins of living coral running along the cracked walls. At its bottom the wavebender stands rooted, current spiraling upward around her like smoke made of breathing, living water. Every fish in sight fled the chamber long before you arrived, leaving only silence and that steady, patient glow. You descend carefully, feeling the pressure build behind your eyes with every step. She watches without moving, letting you close the distance yourself, as though the outcome of this fight was decided the moment you first entered her drowned and ancient temple. The current tightens gently around your calves, testing, patient, entirely unhurried.

The dark presses close as the corridor bends steadily downward, swallowing your dwindling torchlight a little more with every careful turn. A pulse of blue answers from somewhere below, rhythmic and unmistakably deliberate, nothing like the random flicker of drifting bioluminescence. Something ancient rises slowly from the drowned floor ahead, entirely unhurried, to finally meet you. The wavebender emerges from settling silt, webbed hand already tracing slow circles through the current. Water bends instantly to her will, curling around broken columns like it remembers every order she has ever given it. You brace yourself against the pull, understanding now that this fight was never going to happen on your terms.

Your torch throws long, wavering shadows across drowned statues lining the ancient hall, each one worn smooth by centuries underwater. One of them blinks, slow and deliberate, and you very nearly drop the torch entirely in surprise. The wavebender steps free of the stone itself, current uncoiling from her wrist like a rope that has always been waiting to be used against someone exactly like you. She crosses the flooded hall without hurry, crown dripping faint light onto the silt below. The water parts ahead of her and closes behind, obedient in a way that feels less like magic and more like old, practiced habit. You realize, watching her approach, that she has done this exact thing more times than you could ever count.

You feel the current before you actually see it, curling gently around your calves like a leash tightening by careful degrees. At the far end of the flooded hall, the wavebender waits with arms loose at her sides, letting the water obey every slow breath she takes without visible effort. The whole ancient temple seems to hold its breath along with her, waiting for you to close the last of the distance. Coral crunches underfoot as you edge closer, past a broken statue lying face down in centuries of silt. She turns to face you fully now, patient and utterly unafraid, current already spooling from her open fingers like thread pulled from a loom that never stops turning, not even for a moment.

The temple opens suddenly into a flooded chamber ringed with empty coral thrones, save for the one still occupied. The wavebender rises from it unhurried, current gathering around her shoulders like a held breath finally, deliberately released. Somewhere above, the last trace of daylight fails completely, and the faint blue glow becomes the only light left in the entire drowned world. You step into the chamber carefully, feeling the water resist you at every turn like it has a will entirely its own. She studies you from the throne a moment longer before rising fully, crown catching what little light remains. The current tightens around your ankles, patient, testing, already certain of exactly how this will go.

Your light catches a crown half buried in drifting silt, then the still, pale face beneath it, eyes closed as though merely resting. They open slowly, deliberately, as though waking has always been a choice rather than any kind of necessity for something this ancient. The wavebender rises without hurry, current already curling tight at her feet like a loyal animal that has been patiently waiting for her to finally stir. You back away instinctively, torch trembling in your grip. She does not follow immediately, letting the current do that work instead, curling around broken stone toward you with slow, deliberate purpose. The whole chamber seems to lean in closer, eager to watch exactly what happens next between the two of you.

A drowned bell hangs silent overhead, coral crusted and forgotten by everyone but the patient water itself. Beneath it waits the wavebender, arms open in something almost like welcome, current already rising to meet your very first careful step forward into the chamber. The whole hall seems deliberately built around this one moment of arrival, every drowned stone angled just so. You hesitate at the threshold, feeling the pressure change before the current even truly does. A slow, deliberate pull tugs hard at your boots, testing your balance the way water tests every weakness it finds in stone or in flesh. The wavebender watches calmly, letting the temple soften you first before she moves at all.

The passage floods your lungs with pressure and biting cold long before you finally reach the coral altar at its end. Ahead, blue light traces a crowned silhouette against the crushing dark, entirely unmoving in a way that somehow unsettles far more than motion ever would. You realize slowly that she has simply been waiting this whole time for you to notice her first. When recognition finally passes between you, the wavebender raises one webbed hand and the current answers instantly, curling tight around broken columns like it has always known exactly where this moment was heading. You grip your weapon tighter, understanding now that patience like hers is its own kind of terrible weapon.

You step carefully over a fallen spear, rusted clean through and abandoned in the ancient silt. Whoever carried it met the wavebender before you did, and clearly did not carry it back out of this drowned place again. The current around you tightens now, patient and unhurried, as though it has all the time in the world left to finish exactly what it started here centuries ago. Ahead, the wavebender waits at the coral altar, crown dripping steady blue light onto the silt below her bare feet. She does not acknowledge the spear, or you, not yet. She simply lets the current do the announcing, curling tighter around your ankles with every careful step you take toward her.

The temple's deepest hall glows faint blue, lit by something quietly, patiently alive within the coral itself. At its heart stands the wavebender, current curling around her shoulders like an old and loyal pet finally, happily returning home after a very long absence. She watches you cross the flooded threshold without so much as raising a single hand in greeting or warning. You approach slowly, boots dragging against silt that seems reluctant to let you pass at all. When you're close enough to see her eyes clearly in the blue light, she finally moves, current snapping tight around your legs like a trap that has been patiently set for longer than you can possibly imagine.

A ripple crosses the vaulted ceiling above you, wrong in a way you can't quite name, far too deliberate to be ordinary current alone. You look down just as the water beneath your feet tightens its grip without warning. The wavebender has already decided, calmly and completely, exactly where you're meant to stand for whatever comes next in this drowned and ancient place. She emerges from the gloom ahead, crown catching faint light, current coiling tight around one wrist like a weapon she has not yet chosen to draw. You brace yourself against the pull, understanding that this fight will happen entirely on her terms, in her temple, in water that answers only to her.

The corridor bends toward a soft blue glow that seems to breathe in and out along with the current itself. Something large moves just beyond the light, unhurried, testing the water carefully before it finally commits to anything at all. When it steps forward at last, the tide bends first, rushing ahead of her like an eager and loyal thing, and only afterward does the wavebender herself follow. You raise your weapon, feeling the current tighten around your calves in response. She studies you for a long moment, crown dripping, before finally speaking through the water itself rather than her mouth: a slow, deliberate pull that says, clearer than words, that she has already chosen how this ends.

The last doorway opens onto black water lit by slow, drifting motes of light like dying stars scattered across the dark. At its center a crowned shape turns toward you, current gathering at her fingertips like a breath about to be released all at once into the surrounding water. The whole drowned world seems to lean toward that single held moment, waiting. You step through the doorway carefully, feeling pressure build behind your eyes with every stride. The wavebender watches your approach without moving, patient as only something this old can afford to be, letting the current test your balance long before she ever needs to test your strength directly herself.

A current coils lazily around a fallen sword, dragging it slowly across the silt floor like a curious hand testing an old, forgotten toy. You look up sharply, heart hammering hard against your ribs in the sudden quiet. The wavebender is already closer than she was only moments ago, and you never once saw her actually move so much as an inch in the drowned gloom. She studies you calmly from across the chamber, crown catching the last of your failing torchlight. Current spools from her open fingers like thread pulled from a loom that never once stops turning, not even for a moment. You realize, too late perhaps, that the fight already began the instant you first stepped through her doorway.

The altar chamber hums with a low, ancient song that only the water itself seems to remember clearly after all these centuries. The wavebender waits at its center, crown dripping steady light onto silt gone thick with age, current already coiling tight for whatever comes first between the two of you. You get the distinct sense she has performed this exact moment more times than memory could ever hold. You approach across the flooded floor, boots dragging against resistance that shouldn't exist in ordinary water like this. She raises one hand slowly, and the ancient song shifts pitch, current answering her gesture like it has been trained to for longer than your own people have even existed.

You duck beneath a drowned lintel and feel the water tighten sharply around your legs, testing your resolve before you've even fully entered the chamber. Ahead, a pale figure raises one webbed hand, unhurried, and the whole ancient temple seems to lean in closer to watch exactly what happens next between the two of you. Coral thrones sit empty behind her, patient as she is, waiting for nothing in particular anymore. The wavebender lowers her hand slowly, and the current surges forward to meet you, cold and insistent against your legs. You steady yourself against the pull, gripping your weapon tighter as the drowned hall grows suddenly, unmistakably quiet around the both of you.

Bioluminescence flickers along a fallen archway, tracing a narrow and deliberate path through gloom gone thick with silt disturbed by something unseen. At its end waits the wavebender, current curling around her shoulders like a cloak worn without any visible effort at all on her part. She has clearly stood in that exact spot far longer than the fragile, flickering light suggests to you now. You approach across broken stone, feeling pressure ache steadily behind your eyes with every careful step forward. When you're finally close enough to see her clearly, she raises one hand and the current answers at once, curling tight and cold around your ankles like a trap sprung after a very long and patient wait.

The dark presses close as the corridor bends steadily, relentlessly downward, swallowing your torchlight a little more with every single turn you take. A pulse of blue answers from somewhere below, rhythmic and patient in a way that ordinary current never quite manages on its own. Something ancient rises slowly from the drowned floor ahead of you, entirely unhurried, to finally meet you here. The wavebender steps forward from the settling silt, webbed hand already tracing slow, deliberate circles through the surrounding current. Water bends instantly to her will, curling tight around broken columns like it remembers every single order she has ever given it across the centuries.

A soundless wave rolls through the flooded hall ahead of you, bending the water sideways hard enough to stagger your footing against the cold, unforgiving stone. You brace with both hands, torch nearly guttering out completely in the sudden turbulence that follows. Something crowned and infinitely patient waits just beyond the disturbance, content to let the ancient temple do the softening work entirely for her. When the water finally stills around you, you see her clearly for the very first time: the wavebender, arms loose, current curling obediently around her wrist like a loyal animal that has finally, happily returned home to her side.

Cold water floods your boots as the ancient floor gives slightly beneath your weight, unstable after so many centuries spent beneath the crushing sea. Somewhere close a current hums with plain, unmistakable intent, curling around broken columns like it's searching methodically for a way inside your defenses. The crowned shape watching from the surrounding dark has not blinked once since you first entered this drowned place. You press forward anyway, torch trembling slightly in your grip. The wavebender finally steps into full view, current already spooling from her fingers, patient and utterly certain of exactly how this particular fight is going to end for you.

Pressure aches steadily behind your eyes as the ancient temple deepens around you, dark pressing in from every side like something almost alive and breathing. A pale shape glides along the vaulted ceiling above, trailing threads of light that never quite touch the black water waiting below. It watches you the way a patient tide studies a slowly shrinking shore, unhurried and utterly certain. When you finally reach the flooded altar chamber, the wavebender is already there waiting, crown catching the last of your failing torchlight. Current curls tight around her wrist, coiled and ready, while she studies your approach as though every single step had already been written somewhere long before you arrived.

Coral crunches underfoot as you edge carefully past a broken statue lying face down in centuries of undisturbed silt. The water thickens around you with every step, resisting like it has a will entirely its own, testing your resolve before the real fight even begins. Ahead, a crowned shape finally turns to face you directly, patient and utterly unafraid of whatever weapon you carry. Current already spools from her open fingers like thread pulled from a loom that never once stops turning for anyone. The wavebender studies you a long moment before finally speaking through the water itself, a slow pull at your ankles that says, clearer than words ever could, exactly how patient she has learned to be.

You press through a curtain of dense kelp into open, black water thick with silt and the memory of centuries. A low hum vibrates through the current itself, unmistakably deliberate, nothing like the ordinary shifting tides you've felt before in shallower places. Ahead, webbed hands part the water like curtains being drawn back slowly, revealing a patient crown beneath. The wavebender steps forward into the dim blue light, current curling obediently around her shoulders like a cloak she has worn for longer than memory holds. You raise your weapon, feeling the pressure build steadily behind your eyes as the ancient chamber grows quiet around the both of you, waiting.

The corridor floods faster than you ever expected, cold biting hard through every seam of your worn gear as you push deeper. Ahead, a shape glides calmly against the current instead of with it, patient while you struggle openly to keep pace with her unhurried movement. Somewhere behind her, drowned banners drift overhead, torn and long since colorless in the crushing dark. She finally turns to face you fully, crown dripping faint light onto silt gone thick with age and neglect. The wavebender raises one hand slowly, and the current answers at once, curling tight and cold around your legs like a trap that has been patiently, carefully set for you alone.
13: The corridor drops away into black water, and the current pulls you forward before your torch even gutters out. Somewhere ahead something enormous shifts against stone, a groan like a hull under strain, and bioluminescent moss along the drowned columns flares blue in answer. This is the deepest hall of the temple, the one the maps refused to draw, and the dragon turtle that guards it has not seen sunlight in a thousand years. It rises from the silt in sections, shell first, wide as a longship and crusted with barnacles and old gold leaf that has not worn off. Two eyes the size of shields fix on you, and the water itself seems to thicken with its attention. Roll for initiative; a dragon turtle this old does not need to be fast to be first. You go in low, blade angled for the seam where throat meets shell, because the plates up top will turn anything short of a critical hit. The first exchange is a test: it wants to see what you are made of before it wastes its breath weapon. Answer honestly. Answer with steel. By the third exchange the shell's rhythm becomes plain: it favors its right flank when it turns, dragging that hind leg a beat slower than the rest, and a sharp eye can use that lag to slip past the jaw. The steam breath, when it finally comes, boils the water into a blinding curtain for a two count. Ride it out behind the column, then push in while the ancient creature is still recovering its wind.

Bioluminescent lichen lines the sunken hall in cold blue veins, enough light to fight by and just enough to see the shape rising from the collapsed roof: a shell wider than the room, golden scutes dulled by silt, a jaw that could close a portcullis. The dragon turtle has slept here since before the temple drowned, and your torch smoke curling upward through water is the rudest thing to happen to it in years. It does not charge. It settles instead, forelimbs planting into the seabed, and a sound builds in its chest like a kettle nearing boil. That is the tell for the breath weapon, a gout of superheated steam that will cook anyone caught in the open. Scatter behind the fallen columns; the stone is old but it still blocks scalding water. When the steam passes, close the distance fast. Its underside near the joints is the only soft place on that whole armored bulk, and a well aimed strike there does real damage while the rest of your hits glance off shell like coins off a countertop. The second gout of steam comes sooner than the first, a sign it is growing irritated rather than merely cautious, and the fog it leaves behind stings even from a distance. Push through anyway. A dragon turtle that keeps venting breath weapon back to back is a dragon turtle running low on the reserve that fuels it, and that reserve, once spent, takes long minutes underwater to rebuild.

The current changes before you see anything, a slow displaced surge of water pushing back against you, and that is how you know the dragon turtle has turned to face the party. Its shell scrapes the temple roof, shedding a rain of loosened stone, and for a moment the whole ruined hall rumbles like it might come down on everyone at once. Fifteen feet of ancient reptile does not need to be quick to be dangerous. It closes the gap in two great strokes of its legs, jaw snapping shut a handspan from where a fighter stood a heartbeat earlier, and the shove of displaced water alone nearly knocks the party off their feet. Brace against the nearest column and get your dice ready. The fight settles into a rhythm: dodge the jaw, wait out the steam breath, dart in when its head turns to track someone else. Every hit that lands on the shell is wasted effort; every hit that finds the throat or the tendon behind a foreleg counts double. Aim carefully. This is not a fight you win by swinging harder. Somewhere in the chaos a shove of displaced current knocks the party's smallest member sideways into a fallen column, and for a heartbeat it looks like the fight might end there. It does not. Recovering fast, everyone regroups behind the stone, waits out the next gout of steam, and returns to the plan: strike narrow, strike the seams, and let the shell take every blow meant to miss.

Something ancient wakes in the deepest chamber of the sunken temple, and the whole drowned hall seems to hold its breath with you. The dragon turtle uncurls from where it has rested for a millennium, shell scraping free of silt in a slow grinding whoosh, and its eyes catch the bioluminescent glow like twin captured moons. It does not roar at first. It simply rises, water displaced in a widening ring, until its head is level with the broken altar at the room's center. Then the roar comes, a physical thing that rattles your teeth and sends a shockwave of bubbles boiling off its jaw. Initiative is rolled in that instant, and the fight begins whether you are ready or not. Its favored trick is patience: let you spend your best attacks on the shell, let the current carry your strength away, and wait for an opening its steam breath can exploit. Do not give it that opening. Strike the seams, not the plates, and keep one eye on the gout of scalding water always building in its throat. Twice more the gout of scalding water rolls through the chamber, and twice more the party answers with narrow strikes to the throat while its guard is wide. By the third exchange the ancient thing is finally slowing, breath ragged, shell dragging low against the silt. That slowdown is the whole fight in miniature: wear down patience with patience, until even a thousand year old guardian runs out of both.

Cold water presses in from every side as you cross the last submerged threshold, and the temple opens into a vast collapsed nave where a single shaft of surface light somehow still finds its way down. In the middle of that pale column rests the dragon turtle, so still you could mistake it for a boulder until one golden eye opens. It has guarded this place since before the roof caved in, and the coins scattered across the floor, the columns of gold leaf still fixed to fallen stone, all of it belongs to a hoard it has never once had to defend. Today it will. Draw steel and step into the light; the current carries the sound of your approach straight to it. The turtle uncoils in a single motion, shell first, then the long neck, then a jaw wide enough to swallow a shield whole. Its breath weapon vents first, a boiling gout that turns the water white, and only after that does it close for the bite. Survive the steam. The bite is the easier problem. Somewhere past the second exchange the dragon turtle changes tactics, dragging its bulk sideways to pin someone against a fallen column instead of simply biting. It is a clumsy trick from a creature built for straightforward violence, and it costs the beast a full turn to set up, a turn spent not breathing steam and not guarding its throat. Take it.

The dragon turtle's shell alone is the size of a longship's hull, and when it turns broadside in the drowned hall you understand exactly why the old maps marked this chamber as impassable. Barnacles and drowned gold leaf crust its back; underneath, its belly plates are pale and soft, the only place a blade will actually bite. It attacks in a set rhythm you learn fast or not at all: a slamming forelimb, a pause while it gathers breath, then the steam gout that fills the chamber with scalding fog. Time your dodge to that pause. Everyone who waits half a second too long gets cooked instead of merely singed, and half a second is all the warning you get down here. Between gouts of steam, close in low and fast, going for the tendon behind a foreleg or the soft seam beneath the jaw. Every clean hit there staggers it enough to buy the next exchange. It is old, and slow to turn, and that slowness is the only edge you have against something this size.
