Its bite finally lands somewhere in the third exchange, a glancing hit that still nearly takes a shield arm off, proof enough that even a slow ancient thing can be dangerous when it finally connects. Answer immediately. A staggered dragon turtle that has just spent its bite is briefly wide open at the throat, and that opening does not last more than a heartbeat.

Deep beneath the drowned nave, past columns furred green with age, the water goes still in a way that feels deliberate, like something enormous is holding its breath to listen. Then the dragon turtle surfaces from the silt floor, shell first, displacing enough water to rock you back a step before it has even moved to attack. Its eyes are old and yellow and utterly unbothered by your torchlight. It has eaten fools before who mistook its stillness for sleep, and the crushed remains of at least one previous party still rot near the base of the altar it guards. Initiative rolls; the current itself seems to lean in to watch. You open with a feint toward the shell, drawing its attention while a second strike goes for the throat seam instead. It works, barely, a shallow cut that draws no blood but proves the plan sound. Keep drawing its guard wide and keep striking narrow. That is the only shape this fight can take and still end in your favor. The rhythm holds for three full exchanges: steam, cover, close, retreat. By the fourth, the shell is dragging visibly in the silt, and the gouts of scalding water come slower, less certain, more like the last gasps of a furnace running out of fuel than the confident opening breath from before. Push the advantage now, while it still can be pushed.

A wall of scalding water slams into the chamber before you even see the dragon turtle move, steam boiling off its jaw and rolling across the drowned floor like a living thing. Whoever is caught in the open takes damage before they even understand what happened; the smart move was already ducking behind a column two seconds ago. When the fog clears, the turtle has closed half the distance, forelimbs planting into silt with enough force to shake loose stone from the collapsed roof above. Its shell shrugs off a first strike without so much as a scratch, gold leaf and barnacle alike untouched. The message is clear: nothing about that shell is going to give. So go under it instead. The soft plates near the joints, the pale seam beneath its throat, those are the only real targets in this fight. Every hit that lands there staggers the beast; every hit that lands anywhere else just wastes a turn you cannot spare against something this patient and this old. A second gout follows faster than expected, catching someone still recovering from the first, and the burn of it lingers long after the steam clears. Grit through it. The soft plates near the joints are softer now, the whole creature visibly tiring, and every strike that finds that seam counts for more than it did at the fight's opening moments.

The temple's deepest hall is a drowned throne room, broken columns rising into darkness overhead, and at its center sits a dragon turtle so large it has reshaped the silt around itself into a shallow crater. Coins and gold leaf litter that crater like an offering, decades of unlucky visitors who never made it back out. It watches you cross the threshold without moving, patient in the way only a truly ancient thing can be patient, and only when you are close enough for the fight to matter does it finally rise. Water surges outward from the motion, a shove strong enough to stagger anyone standing too near, and its jaw opens on a rumble that promises worse. The steam breath comes first, always first, a gout of boiling water that fills the chamber white. Survive that, then close the distance while it gathers itself for the next one. The rhythm is brutal but learnable: dodge, close, strike the seam, retreat. Four beats, repeated, until the ancient thing finally goes still. Twice the beast tries to pin the party against the crater's rim, forelimbs slamming down hard enough to crack more of the collapsed roof loose above. Twice the trick fails, dodged by inches, and each failed lunge costs the ancient thing a turn it cannot spend guarding the seam beneath its jaw. Every wasted turn on its part is a turn earned on yours.

Somewhere above, sunlight tries and fails to reach the bottom of this drowned nave, and the only real light comes from lichen glowing faint blue along the fallen columns. That is enough to see the dragon turtle when it finally moves, shell grinding free of a thousand years of silt, eyes opening like lamps behind old glass. It has guarded this hoard since the temple first went under, back when the roof was still a roof and the altar still held its offerings upright. None of that matters to it now; only the intrusion matters, and the intrusion is you. It rises with a roar that sends a visible shockwave rippling through the water between you. The fight that follows is a lesson in patience. Its bite is fast, its steam breath faster, but its turns are slow, and slow turns mean you can circle behind it if you are quick and lucky. Go for the tendon at the back of a foreleg first. A staggered dragon turtle is a turtle that finally stops being unkillable. Its roar echoes twice more through the ruined nave as the fight wears on, each one a little less certain than the last, the water shoving outward with a little less force. That fading confidence is the surest sign the plan is working: circle wide, strike narrow, and let the ancient thing's own bulk work against it in these tight collapsed halls.

The current tastes different this deep, mineral and cold, and it carries the first warning of the dragon turtle before your eyes ever find it: a low pulsing thrum, water shoved rhythmically by something breathing far larger than any breath you know. You round the last fallen column and there it is, shell wide as the collapsed roof above it. It does not attack immediately. It studies you instead, ancient reflexes weighing whether you are worth the effort of rising fully from the silt. Then, apparently deciding you are, it does rise, and the shove of displaced water alone is enough to knock a smaller party member clean off their feet. Steam comes next, a boiling exhale that turns the water in front of it opaque and white. Get clear of that cone or take real damage. Once it passes, the turtle's head is briefly lowered and exposed, jaw still steaming, and that half second is the best opening you will get to drive a blade into the soft seam beneath its throat. The half second window after the steam gout becomes the whole rhythm of the fight, repeated again and again until it feels almost familiar: brace, breathe, strike, retreat. By the fourth repetition the throat seam is raw and the ancient creature's guard finally starts dropping wide, its head sagging lower each time it exhales.

Every column in this drowned hall has been felled at least once, snapped clean through and left where they fell, and it takes you a moment standing in that ruin to understand what did the felling. The dragon turtle sleeping at the chamber's heart is large enough to have done it simply by rolling over in its sleep. It is not asleep now. Your torchlight woke it three columns back, and by the time you reach the open floor its golden eyes are already tracking you, jaw working slightly as if tasting the water for your scent. When it finally moves, it moves all at once, a lunge that closes ten feet in a single stroke. The shell takes every early hit without complaint, barnacles and gold leaf both untouched by steel. The soft plates near its joints are the answer, along with the pale seam under its jaw where a well placed strike actually draws blood. Keep circling. Keep striking narrow. The old thing tires slower than you do, but it does tire. By the second lunge its patience is visibly thinning, jaw snapping at empty water where a fighter stood a half second earlier, frustration plain in the force of the miss. Frustrated things make mistakes. The next opening at the throat comes wider and lasts longer than the last, and it is answered with everything the party has left to give.

Bubbles rise past you in a steady silver column, oxygen from somewhere far above leaking down through a crack in the drowned roof, and it is that faint light glinting off gold that first reveals the dragon turtle's hoard scattered across the chamber floor. The turtle itself is harder to see until it moves, shell colored exactly like the silt it rests in. When it does move, the whole floor seems to shift with it, ancient muscle rolling beneath armor thick enough to have turned aside a thousand years of cave ins. It fixes both eyes on you and opens its jaw wide enough to swallow a shield, and the sound that follows is less a roar than a physical shove of water. Get behind cover before the steam breath follows. Once it passes, close in fast; its head lowers to breathe again only a few seconds later, and that lowered angle briefly exposes the soft seam under its jaw. Strike there, retreat, repeat. Patience wins this fight faster than fury ever will. A silver column of bubbles from a lucky sword strike marks the first real wound, a thin dark thread rising from the soft seam beneath its throat. It is not much, not yet, but it is proof the plan works, and proof enough to keep pressing while the ancient guardian's confidence, so absolute a few exchanges ago, visibly starts to crack.

The drowned nave narrows toward its far end into a single collapsed archway, half blocked by rubble from the roof that gave way centuries ago, and it is through that narrow gap that you first glimpse the dragon turtle's eye, huge and golden and entirely too aware of you already. It does not fit through the archway, which is the only reason you have time to plan before the fight starts in earnest. When you finally step past the rubble into the open chamber, it is already turning to meet you, shell scraping stone, gold leaf and old coin sliding off its back in a small avalanche. The steam breath opens the exchange, as it always does with this creature, boiling the water white for a long two count. Behind cover, count those two seconds and move the instant they end. Its head dips low right after venting, jaw still smoking, and that lowered angle is your best and briefest chance to strike the soft seam beneath it. Every strike that lands on gold plated shell rings out through the water like a hammer on an anvil, useless noise that accomplishes nothing but drawing its attention. Ignore that instinct to swing at the loudest target. The seam beneath the jaw, quieter and far less satisfying to hit, is the only place tonight's fight is actually being decided.

Something enormous has been resting against the temple's deepest wall long enough for barnacles to grow across half its shell, and it is only when that shell shifts, slow and grinding, that you realize the wall itself is alive. The dragon turtle peels away from the stone it has slept against for a thousand years, gold leaf and silt sliding off in sheets. Its eyes find you before its body has even fully turned, old and yellow and unblinking, weighing exactly how much trouble you represent. Not enough, apparently, to make it hurry; it rises at its own pace, jaw opening on a low rumble that builds steadily toward the roar that will start this fight in earnest. When the roar comes, so does the shockwave, a physical shove of water that rattles the fallen columns around you. Brace, then close the moment its steam breath passes. The soft seam beneath its jaw is the only honest target on that whole armored bulk, and every strike anywhere else is steel wasted against gold plated stone. Fourteen feet of ancient reptile does not tire quickly, but it does tire, and by the fight's middle stretch its turns come visibly slower, its steam gouts spaced further apart. Keep the rhythm steady: dodge, close, strike the seam, retreat. Patience is the only weapon here that never runs dry, not even against something a thousand years old.

A single shaft of surface light finds its way down through a crack in the drowned roof, illuminating a slow drift of silt across the chamber floor, and it is in that pale column that the dragon turtle first reveals itself, rising from the silt like the temple's own broken foundation come to life. It is old beyond guessing, its shell a patchwork of gold leaf and barnacle and scars from fights long since forgotten by everyone but itself. It studies you the way a landlord studies an uninvited guest, unhurried, faintly annoyed, entirely unafraid. Then it decides you are worth the effort and the fight begins for real. Steam comes first, boiling the water into blinding fog; get clear or take the damage. When the fog thins, its head is lowered from the exhale, jaw still smoking faintly, and that half second of exposure is the whole plan: strike the seam beneath the jaw, retreat before the bite answers, and wait out the next gout of scalding breath. The old thing's jaw finally catches a glancing hit near the fight's midpoint, snapping shut on nothing but water where a party member stood a heartbeat before, and the frustration in that miss is almost audible. Frustration is useful. It means the next opening at the throat will come wider, angrier, and easier to exploit than anything offered so far.

Deep water presses against your ears like a hand as you descend the last flooded stair, and the pressure only grows worse when the dragon turtle finally reveals itself at the bottom, a shape so large it takes a moment to register as a single living creature rather than a fallen section of temple wall. It has claimed this chamber for longer than anyone alive can measure, and the scattered coins and cracked columns around its resting place are proof enough of how many have tried and failed to take it from here. It rises slowly, shell scraping free of silt, eyes opening like twin lanterns behind old glass. The fight opens with its steam breath, a boiling exhale that fills the chamber white and forces everyone behind whatever cover the collapsed columns still offer. Once it passes, close fast; the seam beneath its jaw is soft where the rest of it is stone. Strike there, retreat, and let its own slow patience work against it. Twice the party circles wide to avoid a fresh gout of steam, and twice the maneuver costs the ancient guardian more than it costs them, each wasted breath leaving its throat exposed a beat longer than the last. By the time the shell finally drags low in the silt, worn down rather than broken, the whole rhythm of the fight has become second nature.

The temple's deepest hall smells of salt and old copper, and the current here moves in slow deliberate pulses, almost like breathing. That is because something is breathing: the dragon turtle, half buried in silt at the chamber's center, chest rising and falling in a rhythm large enough to stir the whole floor. It wakes the instant your torchlight crosses its eye, and there is no groggy pause, no slow uncurling. One moment it is still; the next its shell is turning to face you fully, gold leaf sliding free in sheets, and a rumble building in its throat that you have already learned to fear. The steam gout that follows fills the chamber with blinding heat. Get clear, then close while its head is still lowered from the exhale. The seam beneath its jaw is the only soft place on an otherwise impenetrable creature, and every clean strike there buys the party a little more room to survive what comes next. The next gout of steam catches the chamber at an angle, rolling low across the floor instead of straight ahead, and dodging it means diving rather than simply ducking behind stone. It works, barely, and the moment of scrambling recovery afterward almost costs someone a hit from the follow up bite. Stay sharp. This creature adapts as the fight wears on.

Fallen columns litter the drowned hall like felled trees, evidence of a fight that happened here long before you arrived, and it is threading between them that you first catch sight of the dragon turtle's shell, gold leaf dulled by centuries but unmistakably still catching what little light reaches this deep. It is resting, or pretending to, one golden eye cracked open just enough to track your approach. The moment you are close enough to matter, that pretense ends; the shell turns, water shoves outward in a heavy ring, and a jaw wide enough to take a shield whole opens on a rumble that fills the whole ruined nave. Steam comes next, scalding and blinding, forcing the party behind whatever column still stands. When it clears, strike fast and low, aiming for the soft seam beneath the jaw rather than wasting steel on shell that has turned aside worse blows than yours for a thousand years running. By the fight's later stretch the roar comes weaker, more habit than threat, and the shockwave it once sent through the water barely stirs the silt underfoot anymore. That fading power says everything: the ancient guardian is running out of whatever fuels its breath weapon, and every strike still landing on the throat seam brings that end closer.

You feel the dragon turtle before you see it, a slow heavy pulse of displaced water moving through the drowned nave like a second current layered over the first. Then the silt ahead shifts and heaves, and a shape the size of a longship rises out of it, shell crusted gold and barnacle both. It has clearly been resting here a long while; coins and cracked columns lie undisturbed around its resting spot, a small kingdom it has never had to actually defend. Today changes that. It fixes both eyes on the party and rises fully, jaw opening on a rumble that builds toward a roar loud enough to rattle stone. The steam breath that follows the roar is the real danger, boiling the water white for a long count before it fades. Get behind cover, then close the instant it clears; the soft seam under its jaw, briefly exposed while its head stays lowered, is the only place a blade will do real damage tonight. A second wave of steam catches the party mid regroup, forcing an ugly scramble back behind the columns with burns to show for it. Push through the sting anyway. Its head stays lowered a beat longer after this exhale than after the first, tired rather than cautious now, and that extra beat is worth more than the burn cost to earn it.

The archway into the temple's final chamber has partly collapsed, forcing you to duck through a gap barely wide enough for a shield, and on the far side the ceiling opens into a vast drowned nave where the dragon turtle waits, already awake, already watching the gap you just came through. It has clearly heard you coming for some time; its shell is already turned to face the archway, gold leaf and barnacle glinting faintly in the bioluminescent glow from the lichen along the walls. There is no surprise round here, no easy opening strike. Just the two of you, and the water between. It opens with its steam breath rather than waiting for you to close the distance, a boiling gout that fills the narrow chamber fast. Get clear, then push in while its head stays low from the exhale. The seam beneath its jaw is soft; everything else about this ancient creature is unforgiving stone. The party's rhythm holds through a second and third exchange, steam then cover then a fast strike at the throat, and each cycle leaves the ancient creature a little slower to recover than the one before. By the time its jaw finally hangs open, breath ragged, the fight has turned from desperate defense into something almost like a hunt.

Somewhere in the drowned dark ahead, something enormous exhales, and the sound travels through the water as a low physical thrum you feel in your teeth before you hear it. That is your only warning before the dragon turtle surfaces from the silt floor of the temple's last chamber, shell wide as a fallen roof beam. Its eyes are the color of old brass, dulled by a thousand years underwater but sharp enough to track every twitch of your approach. It has guarded this hoard since before the temple's roof gave way, and the coins scattered across the floor beneath it are proof no one has managed to take it from here yet. It rises fully with a roar, water shoving outward hard enough to rattle the nearest column loose from the ceiling above. Steam follows, boiling and blinding; survive that, then close in on the soft seam beneath its jaw while its head is still lowered from the breath. Everything else about it is armor. Its final gout of steam comes late and weak, more habit than real threat, and the shell that once shrugged off every blow now drags visibly low against the silt floor. One last strike to the soft seam beneath the jaw is all it takes to finish what a dozen exchanges of patience already decided. The ancient guardian goes still, and the hoard beneath it is finally, quietly, yours.

11: Fog rolls off the gutters and pools around a crooked lamppost where the road forks. One branch climbs to the bell tower, the other slides into shadow that seems to breathe. A pale shape steps from the mist, cloak dragging like a torn wing. The Nightbringer has picked its hunting ground. Type quick before it picks you.

Rain slicks the rooftops above the first branch, where loose tiles slide and gutters spill into the dark below. Something perches on a crooked chimney, watching with eyes like dying embers. It drops without a sound, cloak snapping open like leather wings. Race the clock before those fangs find your throat.

The lamplighter never reached this corner tonight; his ladder lies broken against the wall. In his place waits a tall shape in a fine coat, gloved hands folded, patient as frost. The Nightbringer bows low, mocking, before its smile splits far too wide. Strike true before the courtesy ends.

Fog thickens at the fork where three narrow streets meet beneath a dead streetlamp. A door creaks somewhere in the dark, though no hand opens it. A pale shape steps from between two shuttered stalls, cloak dragging through spilled rainwater, eyes catching the last light like a coin. Fight now.

A crooked signpost marks the branch in the road, one arm pointing to the guild hall, the other snapped clean off. Mist pools ankle deep and swallows the sound of your own footsteps. Something tall unfolds from a doorway, cloak whispering against brick. The Nightbringer has been waiting all along.

Gutters overflow along the alley where the road splits in two, carrying dead leaves and a single dropped glove past your boots. A shape detaches from the fog, taller than any door frame should allow. Its smile is handsome and wrong all at once. Type the line before it steps any closer.

The streetlamp gutters and dies at the exact spot where the alley forks, plunging the crooked lane into full dark. Somewhere close a cloak drags across wet cobbles, slow and unhurried. A pale hand beckons from the black doorway ahead. The Nightbringer never needs to hurry when its prey is trapped.

Sewer grates rattle underfoot where the tunnel branches beneath the tenements, one way to the docks, the other to nowhere good. A cold wind carries the smell of old blood and older stone. Something with too many teeth smiles from the dark fork ahead. Type fast before the smile finishes forming.

Rooftop tiles clatter loose where the chimneys crowd together above the fork in the street below. A shape crouches there against the fog, cloak pooling like spilled ink around gloved hands. It tips its head, listening for your breath. The Nightbringer has all night, but you do not.

The bell tower creaks above a crooked branch of alleyway where the cobbles turn slick with old rain. A courier's dropped satchel lies split open, papers scattering in the wind. From the dark beyond it steps a tall figure, smiling like it already knows how this ends. Prove it wrong.

Lamplight gutters along the narrow fork where the guild district splits from the slums, one branch lit, the other black as a shut door. Something moves in that dark half, unhurried, patient, gloved fingers trailing along brick. The Nightbringer only ever chooses the darker branch. So did you.

Mist condensing on the cobbles turns the whole crooked street to a mirror of dim light. Where the road splits, a pale shape waits with its back to a shuttered window, cloak hanging still despite the wind. Its eyes open slow, catching the last streetlamp like flame. Type before it blinks again.

A dropped coin rolls to a stop at the fork in the alley, spinning once before it settles heads up. Fog swallows the sound of the second footstep that was never yours. A tall shape leans out from behind a rain barrel, smile widening like a wound. The Nightbringer collects more than coin tonight.

The tenement stairs branch into two crooked landings, one lit by a guttering candle, the other pitch dark and silent. Something in that dark landing exhales slowly, cold enough to fog the nearest window. A gloved hand rests on the broken railing. Climb fast, or let the Nightbringer climb first.

Rain drums on tin roofs where the alley forks past a boarded tavern, gutters roaring with runoff toward the sewer grate. A tall shape steps out unbothered by the storm, cloak dry despite the downpour. Its smile is far too handsome for the teeth behind it. Type quickly before it offers you shelter.

The lamplighter's ladder still leans where he left it, abandoned at the fork where the crooked street splits toward the docks. Fog curls around its rungs like fingers. A voice, quiet and courteous, offers a greeting from the dark beyond. The Nightbringer always sounds kind before it strikes.

Cobblestones glisten at the branch where three alleys meet under a cracked streetlamp, its light flickering weak and gold. A shape unfolds from the deepest shadow, taller than the doorway it stands beside. Gloved fingers curl in invitation. Answer with your own hands and win this fork.

A church bell tolls once as the road forks past the crumbling chapel, and the sound dies fast in the thick fog. Something pale crouches on the low wall, cloak pooling around its boots like spilled ink. It rises slow, smiling with a courtesy that chills more than the wind. Fight now.

The gutter runs black with old rain where the alley splits behind the tanner's shop, carrying a single ribbon downstream. A tall shape steps from the fork's darker branch, smile handsome, eyes hollow. It bows, mocking, gloved hand extended. The Nightbringer always offers the first move.

Fog rolls thick through the fork where the cobbler's row meets a dead end lit by one dying lamp. A shape detaches from the brick, cloak dragging soundless over wet stone. Its smile is quiet, its patience quieter still. Type your line before that patience finally runs out.

The crooked lane splits at an old well, one branch toward the guild hall, the other toward silence. Mist pools thick around the well's cracked stones. A tall figure rises from beside it, gloved hands folded politely, waiting. The Nightbringer offers you first choice of branch. Choose speed.

Loose shutters bang somewhere above the fork in the alley, rattled by a wind that smells faintly of copper. A pale shape steps from behind a rain barrel, cloak whispering against brick, smile widening slow. Its courtesy is a mask worn thin. Strike before the mask finally slips.

The lamplight flickers twice and dies at the branch where the market street narrows to a crooked throat of stone. Something waits just past the dark, patient as a held breath. A gloved hand beckons once from the shadow. The Nightbringer only needs you to take one more step.

Rain pools in the cracked cobbles where the road forks near the old bell tower, reflecting a streetlamp that gutters weak and gold. A tall shape steps into that reflection, cloak dragging soundless. Its smile is handsome enough to be believed. Do not believe it. Type fast.

A courier's cart sits abandoned at the fork, one wheel broken, papers scattered across wet cobbles. Fog swallows the street beyond it whole. Something tall steps from that fog, gloved hands folded, smile courteous and cold. The Nightbringer always finds the ones who linger.

The alley forks past a shuttered bakery, the smell of old bread lost beneath damp stone and colder air. A pale shape crouches on the sill above, cloak pooling like ink around its boots. It drops without a sound, smile already waiting. Race the clock before it lands.

Mist thickens where the crooked street splits near the tannery, carrying the smell of old blood no rain can wash clean. A tall figure steps from the fork's darker half, gloved hand extended in mock welcome. The Nightbringer bows low, smiling wide. Answer its bow with speed.

The gutter overflows at the fork behind the guild hall, carrying a dropped glove past your boots toward the drain. A shape unfolds from the shadow beside the drainpipe, taller than it should be, smile patient. Its eyes catch the last lamp like a struck match. Fight now.

A dead streetlamp marks the branch where the alley splits toward the docks, its glass long since broken by some other night's trouble. Fog pools thick and cold around your ankles. Something pale steps from the dark half of the fork, gloved hands folded, smiling slow. Type fast.

The crooked lane narrows to a single branch behind the chapel, walls pressing close on either side. A tall shape waits there already, cloak dry despite the drizzle, smile courteous and cold. It inclines its head once, patient as stone. The Nightbringer never needs to chase.

Rain hammers the tin roofs above the fork where two alleys meet near a boarded inn, gutters roaring toward the sewer grate below. A shape steps out unbothered by the storm, gloved hand rising in quiet greeting. The Nightbringer's smile is warmer than the weather. Do not trust it.

The lamplighter's route ends at this fork tonight, ladder abandoned against a crooked wall streaked with old rain. Fog curls thick around the dead lamp overhead. A tall figure steps from the dark beyond it, smile patient, gloved hand extended. Answer quickly, before the fog closes in.

The Clay Golem stands at the mouth of Waypoint alley, shoulders wide as a doorway, fists like millstones, clay cracking faintly with every breath it does not take. Fog drips off the guild lamps overhead. You raise your blade and roll initiative, feet braced on wet cobbles, ready.

Rain has packed the golem's clay shin with grit, and every step it takes grinds like a cart wheel dragged over gravel, slow and certain. You watch the crack running up its chest, wondering if that is a flaw or old scarring, while fifteen feet of fog still separate you from the answer.

Lamplight catches the golem's shoulder as it turns, revealing a shelf of dried mud where a shield should be, cracked at the edges like old pottery. Somewhere above, a rooftop guild bell rings the hour. You plant your feet, breath frosting, and wait for the first swing to commit.

The alley narrows to a single crooked lane, gutters running black with rain and old ash. Clay fists the size of barrels crack the air as the golem swings, missing you by inches and splintering a hanging sign. You roll clear and come up already counting seconds until it turns again.

Somebody carved a symbol into the golem's back centuries ago, and it still glows faint copper under all the grime and river mud. You catch a glimpse of it as the creature spins, dust sifting from every joint, slow and heavy, and you know exactly where you mean to strike next.

Fog rolls thick down Waypoint's crooked street, and the golem emerges from it like a chimney come loose from some forgotten roof. Its footprints fill instantly with rainwater, dark little pools. You grip your weapon tighter, feet cold on stone, waiting for that grinding step to close.

The golem's fist connects with a shuttered door, and the whole frame folds inward with a crack like breaking ice on a frozen river. Dust and splinters drift past the lamplight in slow spirals. You use the noise as cover, sliding along the wall toward its flank while it hauls free.

Waypoint's sewers breathe cold air up through an iron grate, and the golem's shin catches on the bars as it turns, grinding loud. You see the crack spreading wider up its leg, feel your pulse in your throat, and press the advantage before the mud has time to knit itself whole again.

A guild lantern gutters out just as the golem raises both fists overhead, clay groaning under its own weight. You count the beat before impact, roll sideways across slick cobbles, and feel the shockwave through your boots as clay meets stone hard enough to crack the street open.

The golem does not chase so much as arrive, closing distance with slow, terrible certainty, mud sloughing from its arms. Rain slicks its shoulders to a dull shine. You circle wide, waiting for the gap between its swings, aware one clean hit from those fists could end this fast.

Somewhere behind the fog, a bell tolls the hour, and the golem answers with a step that shakes loose mortar from a nearby wall. You watch its chest crack wider with every motion, a fault line spreading like frost across old glass, and you aim your next strike straight for the seam.

The alley reeks of river mud and old smoke, and the golem's footprints leave puddles that never quite drain away. You feint left, drawing a fist that crashes into a barrel instead of your ribs, splintering the wood, and use the half second of recovery to close in with everything.

Clay dust hangs in the lamplight like snow that forgot how to fall, drifting slow past crooked rooftops. The golem lumbers forward, indifferent to the cold, indifferent to you, until your blade finally finds the crack running up its side and it staggers, grinding, off balance.

You hear the golem before you see it, a grinding shuffle echoing off brick walls slick with fog and old rain. When it rounds the corner, fists already rising toward the lamplight, you plant your back foot and roll straight into the fight instead of backing away down the alley.

The crack in the golem's chest widens with a sound like a house settling in winter, deep and final. Rain drums on cobblestones as you circle it, watching gray mud flake loose in curls. One more good hit, you think, and the whole crooked wall of clay finally comes down for good.

A guild banner, soaked through and heavy with rain, slaps against the golem's shoulder as it turns, and for a moment you see straight through the crack to something pale and glowing beneath the clay. You do not wait around to ask what that light might actually be made of.

The golem's fist grazes a lamppost, and glass rains down in bright little pieces that catch the fog like scattered coins tossed from a purse. You use the noise, the sudden light, the half second of chaos, to slip past its guard and drive your weapon straight into the crack.

Waypoint's crooked towers lean in close overhead, blotting out what little moon there is tonight. The golem's eyes, twin points of dull ember, track your every step across the wet stone. You breathe out slow, roll your shoulders once, and close the last few feet between you both.

Somebody sealed the golem's chest with a copper plate long ago, back when the guild still kept records, and centuries of rain have worn it thin as parchment. You spot the seam glinting under lamplight and know exactly where your next strike needs to land to do real damage.

The golem drags one leg through standing water, clay sloughing off in gray ribbons that dissolve in the current. It is losing itself a piece at a time, and you intend to help that process along with the point of your blade before it manages to reach you first.

Every guild in this district has a story about the golem at the waypoint, and none of them end well for whoever swung first without a plan. You are about to add your own version tonight, fists up, breath fogging in the cold, feet already moving across the wet stone.

The crack across the golem's shoulder catches lamplight like a river at dusk, gold and shivering and strange. You watch it widen with every step the creature takes, mud flaking away in gray curls, and you press forward before it can seal itself shut again in the cold.

Rain turns the alley to a black mirror, and the golem's reflection arrives a half second before the creature itself, fists raised, shoulders grinding low. You roll the dice in your head before you roll them for real and step forward into range, breath held, blade ready.

The golem's footsteps shake dust from centuries-old shutters overhead, and somewhere a cat yowls and bolts for the rooftops. You do not have that option tonight. You square your stance, plant your feet on the wet cobbles, and wait for the first swing to give you an opening.

A crack in the cobblestones marks where the golem last put its fist down in anger, deep enough to catch a boot heel if you are not careful. You skirt around it, watching the creature reset its stance, and time your approach to match its slowest, heaviest point of recovery.

Fog swallows the tops of the guild towers, leaving only lamplight and the golem's hulking silhouette below on the wet stone. It swings wide, missing you by a hand's width, and you answer with a strike aimed straight at the crack already spreading across its ribs and chest.

The golem's clay shoulder catches on a low awning, tearing the canvas loose in one long strip. Rain pours through the gap onto its head, and mud runs like tears down a face that was never meant to have one, dripping onto the cobblestones below in slow gray streaks.

You count the golem's steps the way you would count a slow drumbeat, each one grinding closer through the fog and lamplight. When it finally swings, you are already moving, already inside its reach, already striking hard for the widening crack along its chest.

A stray dog barks from a doorway as the golem passes, then thinks better of it and vanishes down a side street into the fog. You wish you had that option too, but instead you grip your weapon tighter and step into the lamplight to meet the grinding thing head on, alone.

The golem pauses at the crossing of two crooked streets, clay dust sifting from its joints like flour spilled from a torn sack. You take the pause as an invitation, closing the distance fast on wet stone before it decides, slowly and heavily, to start moving again.

Somewhere under Waypoint, sewer water rushes loud enough to hear even over the golem's grinding steps through the fog. You use the sound to mask your approach, sliding along wet brick until you are close enough to strike before it even manages to turn and notice you.

The golem's fist splits a rain barrel clean in two, water gushing across the cobbles in a sudden flood that soaks your boots through. You wade through the cold spray anyway, and drive your blade toward the crack already spreading fast up its heavy, grinding arm.

The revenant rises from a drain grate, cloak dripping black water, and drifts along the canal path with a sound like a door closing forever somewhere far off. You plant your boots, steady your breath, and wait for the fog to shape it into something you can finally strike.

Lantern light flickers over cracked cobblestones as the revenant glides between crooked towers, its edges dissolving into smoke whenever you blink twice. Grip your weapon tight, watch the words ahead of you, and land your strike before it slips back into the waiting fog.

It does not walk so much as remember walking, boots that leave no print, a coat still buttoned against a cold that stopped mattering to it years and years ago. Somewhere below, water moves through old pipes, patient as the thing drifting slowly toward you right now.

Rooftops lean close over the alley, gutters clogged with ash, and the revenant floats between them like smoke that forgot how to ever rise again. Your candle gutters low. You do not look away. The dice are already turning in your favor if you hold your ground.

Halfway across the causeway the fog thickens, and something pale steps out of it wearing a coat twenty years out of fashion, ribs showing through where the old buttons gape open wide. It says nothing at all. It only waits for you to make the first careless mistake.

The old canal breathes steam even at night, and out of that steam the revenant comes, dragging chains that never quite touch the frozen ground beneath it. You count your breaths, three, two, one, and step forward before your nerve has time to reconsider the whole plan.

A single tear of blue wax runs down the candle in the window across the street, the only light left burning in a whole row of boarded doors. The revenant pauses beneath it, as if it remembers what candles were once for, then turns its hollow face slowly on you.

Twin lanterns swing from a mooring post nobody has tied a boat to in twenty years, and between them the revenant drifts, feet not quite touching the frozen boards below. You grip your weapon, plant your stance, and refuse to let the cold reach your steady resolve.

The gutters run black tonight, thick with old rain, and somewhere beneath the cobbles something clawed drags itself upward one floor at a time. You hear it long before you see it, boots on rooftops, patient as a bell that has not rung in twenty long years, waiting.

It hauls itself out of the sewer grate slick and silent, coat heavy with runoff, and stands there simply remembering how doors used to open for it long ago. You raise your weapon anyway. The fog closes around both of you like a held breath waiting to fall.

Somewhere a bell tolls the wrong hour, and the revenant answers it by drifting closer, unfinished business trailing behind it like smoke off a snuffed candle wick. You do not run from it. Running only ever fed the thing that hunted you down these narrow alleys.

The old golem frozen at the crossroads has rust for eyes, but tonight something moves behind them, cold and patient, waiting for you to step within reach of hands that have not closed on anything living in a very long, forgotten time, and will not miss twice.

Fog pours down the chute between two crooked towers, and out of it steps the revenant, moving with the terrible calm of something that has already died once and found the whole process unremarkable the second time it happened, back when the towers still stood straight.

A jar of tears sits sealed on the windowsill above the alley, closed with blue wax, and the revenant drifts toward it as though it still remembers crying once, long ago. You do not let sentiment slow your hand tonight. The dice favor the quick and the steady.

Steam rises off the canal in heavy sheets, hiding the far bank completely from view, and somewhere in that white nothing the revenant is already moving toward the sound of your boots on the boards, patient as rot working slowly through old timber, unseen and unhurried.

The mourners left the doorway open, candle still burning low, waiting for someone who is never coming home again, and the revenant drifts through that unlatched door like it still has somewhere important it needs to be before dawn finally breaks over the rooftops.

Sixteen floors of a leaning tower stand dark above the street, every window broken except one, where a single candle burns for something that stopped needing light long before the wax finished melting down to absolutely nothing at all, unwatched by anyone living.

The longsword rusting in the fountain has been there since the flood, and tonight the revenant reaches for it with fingers made of fog, remembering a grip it lost the very day the water finally closed over its head for good and all, twenty cold winters back.

Down in the sewer the water runs fast and black, dragging old rafts of driftwood past drowned lantern posts, and something rides that current toward you, patient, cold, and entirely uninterested in hearing your reasons for standing here at all tonight, of all nights.

A blind alley off the canal ends in a locked door, and that is exactly where the revenant wants you, cornered between crooked brick walls with nothing behind you but fog and the sound of its slow, deliberate, approaching feet closing the last of the distance.

The clay jar shatters loudly on the cobblestones, and out of the dust that was once someone's collected grief the revenant gathers itself, tired and patient, reaching toward the only warm thing left in the whole silent street tonight, which happens to be you.

Ten long years the causeway has stood empty, its lanterns unlit, and tonight it finally has a visitor again, gliding across the frozen boards with a hunger that outlasted its own heartbeat by two decades and is still counting slowly, patiently, toward you.

The revenant does not run at you, it simply arrives, the way fog arrives, filling every gap in the alley until there is nowhere left for you to stand except directly in its path with your weapon already raised and your feet set firm against the cobbles.

Rain drums steady on tin roofs above the guild district, and beneath that endless sound something drags itself up from the drowned cellar, clawed hands finding purchase on stone that remembers every single step it ever takes here, slow and sure and coming closer.

The revenant's coat is buttoned wrong, one hole skipped from collar to hem, as though whoever dressed it that last cold morning had been in far more of a hurry than they ever wanted anyone else to notice at the time, and no one ever did, not once, not even now.

Behind the shuttered bakery a chain rattles once, then twice, and then goes still, and you understand that whatever pulled it is done waiting patiently and has decided instead to simply come and find you where you stand, fog trailing behind it like a torn cloak.

Fog curls up from the storm drains thick enough to hide your own boots from sight, and somewhere inside that white the revenant is counting your footsteps, patient, cold, and far closer than the sound would ever suggest, close enough almost to touch you now.

The lamplighter never came back for this street, and the dark has grown teeth since then, something pale drifting between the dead lamps with the unhurried certainty of a debt that finally intends to collect tonight, in full, and with interest owed on every single dark year.

A single point of candlelight marks the window where the old vigil never ended, and the revenant drifts toward it like a moth that forgot it used to fear the flame more than it ever feared staying alone in the dark, night after night, waiting for nothing.

The old ferryman's post stands empty at the mooring, ropes frayed to nothing, and tonight something climbs out of the black water anyway, dragging behind it the shape of a coat it no longer needs to keep dry, water pooling where its footsteps land on the boards.

Between two leaning rooftops the fog gathers into a shape with a coat and empty sleeves, and it turns toward you slowly, the way something turns when it has all the time in the whole world left to spend, and has chosen tonight, finally, to spend every last minute of it right here.

The revenant remembers a door it never got to knock on, and now it drifts the alley outside that same house every single foggy night, patient, cold, and utterly unwilling to let one more visitor simply pass it by, tonight of all nights, unnoticed and untouched.

Fog rolls low along the gutter and something in a gentleman's coat rises off a crate lid. Its hands are turned backward at the wrist, palms out, and when it smiles you count too many teeth. Rooftops lean close overhead. You grip your weapon and step onto the wet cobbles.

The lamplight flickers twice, then dies, and a shape unfolds from the shadow of the archway. Tiger eyes, human mouth, a voice like honey poured over gravel. It offers you a bargain you already know is a lie. Somewhere a gutter drips. You do not answer; you draw steel instead.

Steam hisses up from a grate as you cross the alley, and the fog parts just enough to show two backward hands folded in polite greeting. The face beneath the hood is almost kind, almost human, almost yours. You keep both eyes on the claws and neither on the smile.

A guild banner sags overhead, soaked through, while paw prints the size of dinner plates track across the gravel toward a shuttered door. Something purrs behind it, low and pleased, like it has been expecting company. You test the door. It is not locked. That is the trap.

The rooftops here lean so close that the sky is just a seam of grey between them, and in that seam a tiger's silhouette prowls, easy and unhurried. It has all the time it wants. You do not. Below, the sewer grate rattles once, as if something answered from underneath.

Between one lamppost and the next the fog thickens into a shape with reversed hands and a courtier's bow. It speaks your name wrong on purpose, testing how you flinch. The cobbles are slick with old rain. You plant your feet and refuse to give it the reaction it wants.

A chestnut vendor's cart lies overturned at the mouth of the alley, nuts scattered like teeth across the stone. Whatever tipped it left claw marks deep in the wood and a smell of hot fur hanging in the fog. Somewhere close, something purrs its approval of the mess it made.

The guild doors are barred from outside, which means whatever locked them is already in. Through the keyhole comes a low chestnut-warm chuckle and the click of backward fingers tapping wood. You raise your weapon. The lock gives way before you even touch it.

Rain slicks the rooftop tiles as you climb, and something with a tiger's face and a gentleman's hands watches from the next chimney over, entirely unbothered by the height. It offers a slow, mocking bow. You do not bow back. You close the distance at a dead run instead.

The sewer entrance breathes out warm, wrong air, and painted on the brick above it is a crude sigil someone scratched in a hurry. Claw marks. A door left ajar on purpose. Somewhere below, a voice like honey calls up an invitation you have no intention of accepting.

Fog pools thick around the lamppost, and within it something folds long fingers backward, knuckle to wrist, in a gesture meant to unsettle you. It works. You breathe once, twice, and step through the fog anyway, because the alternative is standing still and losing.

A cracked mirror hangs crooked in an abandoned shopfront, and in it your reflection smiles a half second before you do. Behind you, nothing. In the glass, a tiger's shadow slides past your shoulder. You do not turn around. You watch the mirror and time your strike.

The gravel path behind the guildhall is scattered with coins, too neat to be an accident, each one placed like a lure toward the dark. At the end of the trail a shape waits with backward hands folded, patient as a debt collector counting interest. You step past every coin and go straight for the throat instead.

Somewhere above the rooftops a cat yowls once and cuts off mid cry, and the silence that follows is far worse than the sound. You climb the fire escape two rungs at a time, fog curling cold around your boots, and find a tiger's grin waiting patiently at the top, entirely too pleased that you bothered coming to look for it.

The alley narrows to a single crooked door marked with a symbol scratched fresh into old wood, the paint of it still faintly wet. Behind it something purrs, content, entirely certain of its welcome. You test the handle. It turns without resistance. That ease alone tells you everything you need to know before you even step through.

A decanter of dark liquor sits untouched on an overturned barrel, offered up like a peace treaty nobody signed. Whatever left it there is still close, watching patiently from the fog with eyes that catch the lamplight wrong. You leave the decanter exactly where it sits and raise your weapon instead of a toast.

The rooftop gutter runs red with old rust or maybe worse, and the tiles ahead are scored with four parallel claw marks, deep enough to catch a careless boot heel. You follow them anyway, because turning back now means admitting the fog has already won this whole round of the chase.

A gentleman's hat rolls past your feet, empty, its owner nowhere in sight, and behind it comes a low chuckle from somewhere deep in the wet dark. You do not bother chasing the hat. You watch the alley mouth instead, where a shape with backward hands is already stepping calmly out to meet you.

The guildhall lantern gutters and relights itself, far too deliberate to be wind. Something out there is toying with the light the way a cat toys with a moth before it finally eats it. You keep walking anyway, boots loud on the wet stone, refusing to let the flickering dark decide how fast you move tonight.

A trail of chestnut shells leads from the market stalls into the fog, each one cracked open in turn like a slow countdown someone is running. At the last shell a tiger's tail flicks once from behind a chimney stack, and then the whole rooftop goes perfectly, deliberately still, waiting patiently for you to arrive.

The sewer grate below your boots hums faintly, warm, alive in a way plain iron should never be. A voice drifts up through it, honey smooth, offering safe passage for a price you never agreed to pay. You step off the grate anyway and find the stairs down on your own terms, not its.

Fog thickens at the mouth of a dead end alley, and within it a shape bows low with a courtier's grace, backward hands spread wide in welcome. The bow is theatre, all performance and no substance. The claws underneath are not. You answer the welcome with the flat of your blade, not a handshake.

A cracked shop window shows a tiger's reflection standing just behind where you are, though the street at your back is entirely empty. You do not look back to check, no matter how badly you want to. You watch the glass, count your own heartbeat, and strike the instant the reflection moves first.

The rooftops drip steadily with old rain and something paces the ledge above you, unbothered by the narrow stone, tail flicking with lazy patience. It has cornered prey here plenty of times before. You refuse to be the next one it does not even bother learning the shape of before it strikes.

A guild seal lies snapped clean in half on the cobbles, its wax still warm, whoever broke it barely gone from sight. The fog around the alley mouth smells thick of hot fur and old honey. Somewhere close a voice hums a tune that is almost a lullaby and entirely, unmistakably a threat.

The lamplight throws two shadows from one shape crouched on the gutter's edge, one human, one not, the two of them arguing silently over which face to wear tonight. You do not wait around for it to decide. You close the gap while it is still busy being two things at once, neither one ready.

A backward handprint presses wet into the fogged shopfront glass, five fingers splayed the wrong direction from any wrist you know. Whatever left it there is still close enough to watch you notice, still amused by your reaction. You back slowly toward open street and keep your weapon raised the whole way.

The alley behind the tannery reeks of curing hide and something else entirely, warmer, wilder, far more patient. Claw marks score the doorframe at exactly eye height, as if measuring you for a fitting you never asked for. You step past the marks and refuse to be measured for anything at all.

Rain needles steadily down through a broken skylight above a shuttered guildhall, and in the puddle below, a tiger's face ripples where your own reflection ought to be. You do not flinch, not even a little. You step straight through the puddle and let the fog take the reflection with it instead.

A rope ladder sways from a rooftop that should be entirely empty, and at the top a shape waits with a gentleman's posture and a predator's stillness, watching you climb with obvious, patient amusement. You climb anyway, rung after slow rung. You have never once, in your whole life, liked being watched.

The fog swallows the lamplight whole at the mouth of the sewer stair, and from somewhere down in that dark comes a low, satisfied purr, the sound of something that already believes its hunt is finished before it has even begun. You start down the stairs anyway, ready to prove it thoroughly wrong.

A guild apprentice's dropped satchel lies open on the cobbles, its contents entirely untouched, which is the single wrongest thing about the whole scene. Something out there wanted you to stop and look closer. You do not stop. You keep moving toward the alley where the real trail actually begins.
12: Fog rolls off the gutters in slow grey sheets, pooling around a crooked lamppost at the exact spot where the alley splits in two. One branch climbs toward the bell tower and its promise of open sky. The other slides down into shadow that seems, impossibly, to breathe on its own. Somewhere close a cloak drags across wet cobbles, unhurried, patient, certain. You choose your footing carefully, boots finding the dry stones between puddles, and that is when the shape steps free of the mist. It is taller than any doorway has a right to allow, gloved hands folded, smile far too handsome for what waits behind it. The Nightbringer has already chosen its branch tonight. Now it only needs you to choose wrong.

Rain slicks the rooftops above the first branch of the old quarter, where loose tiles slide underfoot and gutters spill silver rain down into the dark street below. You climb because the ground way is blocked, because a courier's overturned cart has spilled crates across the only door. Somewhere above, a shape crouches on a crooked chimney, watching with eyes like dying embers, waiting for you to look up. It drops without a sound, cloak snapping open like leather wings catching wind. For one long heartbeat you see its whole shape against the fogged moon, elegant and wrong, before it lands soft as falling ash between you and the ladder home. Race the clock now, because the Nightbringer never falls twice.

The lamplighter never reached this corner tonight. His ladder lies broken against the crooked wall, rungs snapped clean through, and the lamp above it gutters weak and dying. In his place waits a tall shape in a fine coat, gloved hands folded with unsettling patience, the kind of stillness that only comes from something that has stopped needing breath. It bows low when it notices you watching, mocking and courteous all at once, before its smile splits far too wide for any handsome face to hold. Somewhere behind it the fog thickens, swallowing the last dim light of the guttering lamp. The Nightbringer offers you the first move only because it already knows how the second one ends.

Fog thickens at the fork where three narrow streets meet beneath a dead streetlamp, its glass cracked from some earlier night's trouble. A door creaks somewhere close in the dark, though no hand ever opens it, and the sound repeats twice more before it stops. You steady your grip and step forward anyway, because the branch behind you is worse. A pale shape detaches itself from between two shuttered market stalls, cloak dragging soundless through spilled rainwater. Its eyes catch the last thread of lamplight like a struck coin, bright and cold. The Nightbringer studies you a long moment, unhurried, before it finally decides you are worth the trouble of a proper fight.

A crooked signpost marks the branch in the road just past the tannery, one arm pointing toward the guild hall's warm lit windows, the other snapped clean off by some older storm. Mist pools ankle deep here and swallows the sound of your own footsteps entirely, so that you cannot tell if you walk alone. Something tall unfolds itself from the black mouth of a doorway, cloak whispering against old brick like a held breath finally released. The Nightbringer has been waiting here a long while, patient as stone, gloved hand rising in a slow and terrible welcome. Type your line before that patience finally runs its course.

Gutters overflow along the tenement row where the alley splits into two uneven halves, carrying dead leaves and a single dropped glove past your boots toward the drain. Somewhere above, a shutter bangs loose in the wind, again and again, like a heartbeat that will not settle. You press on toward the darker branch because it is the only one that leads home. A shape detaches from the fog there, taller than any door frame should allow, cloak pooling around gloved boots like spilled ink. Its smile is handsome and wrong all at once, courteous in a way that makes your skin go cold. The Nightbringer only ever waits this long for prey it considers worth the wait.

The streetlamp gutters twice and dies at the exact spot where the alley forks, plunging the whole crooked lane into a dark thick enough to taste. Somewhere close a cloak drags slow across wet cobbles, and you count the seconds between each footstep, though you know better than to trust the count. A pale gloved hand beckons once from the black doorway ahead, patient and unhurried, certain you will come closer on your own. The Nightbringer never needs to hurry when its prey is already boxed between two dead ends. It only smiles wider the longer you hesitate, waiting for the exact moment you decide to run.

Sewer grates rattle loose underfoot where the tunnel branches beneath the old tenements, one way leading toward the docks and open air, the other toward a silence that presses on your ears like water. A cold wind rises from that second branch, carrying the smell of old blood and older, colder stone. Something with too many teeth for any human smile steps from the dark fork ahead, gloved hands spread in mock welcome. Its cloak drags through standing water without a ripple, as if the tunnel itself refuses to acknowledge it. The Nightbringer speaks softly here, courteous as ever, and the words land colder than the water at your ankles.

Rooftop tiles clatter loose where the chimneys crowd close together above the fork in the street below, and you climb because the ground route is already lost to fog too thick to see through, cold and rising fast. A shape crouches against that fog at the roofline, cloak pooling like spilled ink around gloved, folded hands. It tips its head slowly, listening for your breath the way a cat listens for a mouse behind a wall. The Nightbringer has all the patience the long night can offer, unhurried, certain, smiling faintly at the sound of your heartbeat. You do not have that luxury, and the clock behind your eyes already knows it.

The bell tower creaks above a crooked branch of alleyway where the cobbles turn slick with old standing rain, and somewhere close a courier's dropped satchel lies split open, papers scattering white against the dark stone. You crouch to gather what you can, and that is when the fog beyond it moves on its own. A tall figure steps free of it, smiling like it already knows exactly how this ends, gloved hand extended in an offer of help that is no offer at all. The Nightbringer's courtesy is a blade worn smooth from long use, hidden until the last possible moment. Prove tonight, at least, that its confidence is misplaced.

Lamplight gutters weakly along the narrow fork where the guild district splits from the slums below, one branch bright with candlelit windows, the other black as a shut and rusted door. Something moves in that darker half, unhurried, patient, gloved fingers trailing slow along the damp brick as it walks. The Nightbringer only ever chooses the darker branch of any road, and tonight, so did you, though you told yourself it was the shorter way home. Its smile widens the moment it realizes you understand the trap now, far too late to matter. Type quickly, because understanding alone will not save you here.

Mist condensing thick on the cobbles turns the whole crooked street into a dim and trembling mirror of lamplight, doubling every shadow twice over. Where the road splits near the old well, a pale shape waits with its back against a shuttered window, cloak hanging unnaturally still despite the wind pulling at every loose shutter nearby. Its eyes open slow, catching the last dying streetlamp like caught flame, and for a moment the whole alley seems to hold its breath along with you. The Nightbringer studies you the way a jeweler studies a stone, weighing worth before the first strike. Type before it blinks again and decides you are worth taking.

A dropped coin rolls to a stop at the fork in the alley, spinning once on its edge before settling heads up in a puddle of old rain that has not yet stopped falling. Fog swallows the sound of the second footstep that was never yours to begin with, close behind and softer than it should be for something that size. A tall shape leans out slow from behind a rain barrel, smile widening like a wound finally allowed to open. Gloved fingers curl once in invitation, courteous as ever, patient as the fog itself. The Nightbringer collects far more than coin on nights like this, and it has already decided what it wants from you.

The tenement stairs branch into two crooked landings partway up, one lit by a single guttering candle in a cracked window, the other pitch dark and utterly silent despite the storm still hammering the shutters outside. Something in that dark landing exhales slowly, cold enough that your breath fogs the nearest broken pane. A gloved hand rests on the broken railing there, patient, unhurried, waiting for you to decide which landing is the lesser risk. The Nightbringer already knows which one you will choose, because it always does. Climb fast, or let it climb first and meet you at the top instead.

Rain drums hard on tin roofs where the alley forks past a boarded and abandoned tavern, gutters roaring with runoff toward the black mouth of a sewer grate below. A tall shape steps out into the downpour entirely unbothered, cloak dry despite the storm soaking everything else in sight. Its smile is far too handsome for the teeth waiting behind it, courteous even as it studies you head to boot like a merchant weighing goods. The Nightbringer offers shelter from the rain in a voice smooth as poured wax, patient and warm and utterly false. Type quickly, because its shelter always costs more than the weather ever would.

The lamplighter's ladder still leans exactly where he left it, abandoned at the fork where the crooked street splits toward the fog-choked docks. Fog curls around its rungs like curious fingers testing the wood. A voice, quiet and unnervingly courteous, offers a greeting from the dark just beyond the light. The Nightbringer always sounds kindest right before it strikes, its words smooth and patient, its smile hidden just past where the lamp still reaches. You grip your line tighter and refuse to answer its greeting with anything but speed. The fog waits, patient as its master, for you to falter first.

Cobblestones glisten wet at the branch where three narrow alleys meet under one cracked streetlamp, its light flickering weak and gold across the puddled stone. A shape unfolds slowly from the deepest pool of shadow, taller than the doorway it stands beside, patient as the fog curling at its boots. Gloved fingers curl once in silent invitation, and its smile widens the moment you square your shoulders instead of running. The Nightbringer respects nothing so much as prey that chooses to stand. Answer with your own steady hands now, and win this fork before the lamp above finally dies for good.

A church bell tolls once as the road forks past the crumbling chapel wall, and the sound dies fast, swallowed whole by fog thick enough to hide a second footstep behind your own. Something pale crouches low on the chapel's broken wall, cloak pooling around its boots like spilled ink left to dry. It rises slow, joints unhurried, smiling with a courtesy that chills far more than the wind ever could on its own. The Nightbringer has waited here through worse storms than this one, patient as the stone beneath it. Fight now, before its patience finally runs its course and the courtesy drops away.

The gutter runs black with old standing rain where the alley splits behind the tanner's shuttered shop, carrying a single lost ribbon downstream toward the drain. A tall shape steps from the fork's darker branch, smile handsome, eyes hollow and patient as a held breath waiting to be released. It bows low, mocking, gloved hand extended in an offer that is really no offer at all. The Nightbringer always offers the first move to prey it considers worth the courtesy, and tonight, whether you wanted it or not, that prey is you. Take the offer as a warning instead of a kindness, and answer its bow with speed of your own.

Fog rolls thick through the fork where the cobbler's row meets a dead end lit by one dying, guttering lamp, and the smell of old leather lingers even here where nothing living remains open. A shape detaches from the brick wall itself, cloak dragging soundless over wet stone toward the light. Its smile is quiet, its patience quieter still, the kind of stillness that has had centuries to perfect itself. Type your line before that patience finally runs out and the quiet breaks into something far worse. The Nightbringer has never once needed to raise its voice to be terrifying, and it does not start tonight.

The crooked lane splits at an old covered well, one branch leading toward the guild hall's warm windows, the other toward a silence that presses close and cold. Mist pools thick around the well's cracked stones, rising slow like breath from something buried far beneath. A tall figure rises from beside the well, gloved hands folded politely, waiting with the patience of something that has never once needed to hurry. The Nightbringer offers you first choice of branch tonight, courteous as ever, though both branches lead back to the very same waiting smile in the end. Choose speed over hesitation, always.

Loose shutters bang somewhere above the fork in the alley, rattled again and again by a wind that smells faintly of copper and old rain. A pale shape steps slow from behind a rusted rain barrel, cloak whispering against damp brick, smile widening at the exact pace of your own rising unease. Its courtesy is a mask worn thin from long use, barely hiding the hunger underneath the handsome face. Strike before the mask finally slips entirely and shows what waits behind it. The Nightbringer prefers its prey believing in the mask a moment longer than they should, and you have already given it that moment.

The lamplight flickers twice and finally dies at the branch where the market street narrows down to a crooked throat of old stone. Something waits just past that dark, patient as a held breath, close enough now that you can hear the faint whisper of a cloak settling against the wall. A gloved hand beckons once from deep in the shadow, unhurried, certain you will take one more step regardless of better judgment. The Nightbringer only ever needs that single extra step, no more, and it has learned exactly how to make prey offer it freely, without ever once raising a hand of its own to force the matter.

Rain pools deep in the cracked cobbles where the road forks near the old bell tower, reflecting a streetlamp that gutters weak and gold across the wet stone below, unbroken until now. A tall shape steps directly into that trembling reflection, cloak dragging soundless, as if the puddle itself refuses to ripple beneath it. Its smile is handsome enough to be believed by anyone who has not already learned better. Do not believe it, no matter how warm its voice sounds through the rain. The Nightbringer has drowned better judgment than yours in reflections just like this one, and it is patient enough to try again tonight.

A courier's cart sits abandoned at the fork in the road, one wheel shattered clean through, papers scattered pale across the wet cobbles like fallen leaves. Fog swallows the street beyond it whole, thick enough that the far lamp is only a smear of gold through the grey. Something tall steps slow from that fog, gloved hands folded in mock welcome, smile courteous and colder than the night around it. The Nightbringer always finds the ones who linger too long over scattered papers and broken wheels, and it has found you exactly on schedule. Move fast, before the fog closes the gap for good.

The alley forks past a shuttered bakery, the smell of old bread long lost beneath damp stone and colder, sharper air rising from the sewer grate somewhere below. A pale shape crouches on the sill above the door, cloak pooling like ink around its boots, patient as a spider waiting out a fly. It drops without a sound, landing soft between you and the only lit window left on the street, smile already waiting before its boots even touch the ground. Race the clock now, before it finishes the smile it has clearly been saving. The Nightbringer never wastes a good entrance on prey too slow to appreciate it.

Mist thickens where the crooked street splits near the old tannery, carrying the smell of old blood that no amount of rain has ever managed to wash clean from the stones. A tall figure steps from the fork's darker half, gloved hand extended in mock welcome, smile patient and courteous. The Nightbringer bows low at the fork, smiling wide enough to show exactly what waits behind the handsome face. Answer its bow with speed instead of courtesy of your own, because every second spent bowing back is a second it spends closing the distance between you both, unhurried and certain of the outcome.

The gutter overflows at the fork behind the guild hall, carrying a dropped glove past your boots toward the black mouth of the drain below. A shape unfolds from the shadow beside the drainpipe, taller than it has any right to be, smile patient, eyes catching the last lamp like a struck match. It watches you a long moment before moving, unhurried, certain of its own patience the way only something ancient can be. The Nightbringer has stood in worse fog than this waiting for lesser prey to make the first mistake, and it will happily wait a while longer. Fight now, and refuse to be the one who makes it tonight.

A dead streetlamp marks the branch where the alley splits toward the fog-choked docks, its glass long since broken by some other night's forgotten trouble. Fog pools thick and cold around your ankles, rising slow the way water rises in a filling tub, patient and unhurried. Something pale steps from the dark half of the fork, gloved hands folded, smiling slow enough that you can watch the smile grow the whole way. The Nightbringer likes an audience for its patience, even when that audience is only the prey it intends to take. Type fast, and deny it the satisfaction of watching you hesitate.

The crooked lane narrows to a single branch behind the old chapel, walls pressing close on either side until there is barely room to raise a weapon properly. A tall shape waits there already, cloak dry despite the drizzle soaking everything else, smile courteous and colder than the stone around it. It inclines its head once in greeting, patient as the chapel wall itself, before straightening to its full unnatural height. The Nightbringer never needs to chase prey that has already walked itself into a corner this narrow. Make the corner work for you instead, and answer its greeting with your fastest hands.

Rain hammers hard on the tin roofs above the fork where two alleys meet near a boarded and forgotten inn, gutters roaring toward the black sewer grate below like a river finally finding its bed. A shape steps out unbothered by the storm entirely, gloved hand rising in a quiet, courteous greeting. The Nightbringer's smile is warmer than the weather around it, and that warmth alone should be warning enough not to trust a single word it offers. Do not trust it regardless, no matter how cold the rain runs down your collar. Type through the storm, and let speed answer where trust never should.

The lamplighter's route ends at this fork tonight, his ladder abandoned against a crooked wall streaked dark with old standing rain and older neglect. Fog curls thick around the dead lamp overhead, swallowing what little light remains of the evening. A tall figure steps slow from the dark just beyond it. Its smile is patient, gloved hand extended in an offer wrapped in false courtesy. The Nightbringer answers hesitation with an invitation every single time, smooth and unhurried, certain the invitation will be enough on its own. Answer quickly instead, before the fog closes in around you both for good.

You find the golem waiting at the waypoint the way old debts wait, patient and unhurried, standing in a puddle of its own dripping clay. Fog hangs thick between the crooked towers, and the guild lamps burn low and orange, throwing long shadows across wet cobblestones. Somewhere a shutter bangs in the wind. You circle once, testing the ground for loose stone, and the golem turns with you, slow as a millstone finding its groove. Its chest already carries a crack, hairline thin, and you wonder how many fights it survived to earn that scar. When it finally swings, the fist passes close enough to fog your collar, and you answer with a strike aimed straight for the seam, praying the clay gives before your arm does.

The rain started an hour before you reached the waypoint, and it has not let up since, turning every gutter into a small black river. The golem stands beneath a broken guild sign, motionless until you step within range, and then it moves all at once, shoulders grinding, fists rising like a drawbridge. You dodge the first swing wide, boots slipping on wet stone, and feel the wind of the second pass close enough to chill your neck. The crack along its ribs catches lamplight for just a moment, gold through gray mud, and you commit everything to that single narrow window before the light moves on and the opening closes for good.

Waypoint smells like river mud and old smoke, the kind that clings to your clothes long after you leave. The golem waits at the crossing of two crooked streets, dust sifting from its joints in slow gray curls, patient as a locked door. You have fought smaller things that hit harder, and bigger things that hit slower, but nothing quite like this. When it finally moves, the whole street seems to shake with it, mortar rattling loose from nearby windows. You slide beneath the first heavy swing, feel the crack in its chest catch your eye again, wider now than it was a moment ago, and drive your blade toward it before the mud has any chance to close back over the gap.

Somebody sealed this golem's chest with a copper plate centuries ago, back when the guild still kept careful records of things like that, and the plate has worn thin as old parchment under all that rain. You spot the seam the moment lamplight catches it, a hairline glow beneath layers of packed clay and grit. The golem swings twice before you find your opening, each fist heavy enough to crack cobblestone, each miss shaking dust loose from the crooked buildings around you. On the third pass you slip inside its reach entirely, blade angled straight for that copper seam, and feel the whole fight tip, for just a moment, in your favor.

Fog rolls down Waypoint's crooked lane thick enough to swallow lamplight whole, and the golem steps out of it like a chimney that decided to walk. Its footprints fill with rainwater almost before it lifts its next foot, small dark pools scattered behind it like a trail someone forgot to hide.
