You circle wide, watching the crack in its shoulder catch what little light survives the fog, gold and shivering, and time your approach to its slowest recovery. When the fist finally comes, you are already moving past it, already inside its guard, already driving your weapon home before the golem even finishes its swing.

The alley narrows just past the old guild hall, gutters running black with rain and centuries of grime, and that is where the golem chooses to make its stand. Clay fists the size of barrels crack the air as it swings, missing you by inches and splintering a hanging sign into kindling. You use the wreckage as cover, sliding along the wall toward its flank while it hauls its arm free from a shattered awning. The crack along its ribs has widened since you first saw it, a fault line spreading like frost across old glass, and you know this fight will not last much longer either way. One more good strike and the whole wall of clay comes down.

A guild lantern gutters out just as the golem raises both fists overhead, clay groaning under its own considerable weight. You count the beat before impact and roll sideways across slick cobbles, feeling the shockwave through your boots as clay meets stone hard enough to crack the street wide open beneath it. Dust drifts down from the impact like slow snow, catching what lamplight remains. The golem turns, unbothered, already resetting its stance for another swing, but you have already spotted the crack running fresh up its forearm, pale beneath the grime. You press the advantage before the mud has time to knit itself whole again.

You hear the golem long before you see it, a grinding shuffle echoing off brick walls slick with fog and old rain, patient and unhurried the way only something without lungs can afford to be. When it finally rounds the corner into lamplight, fists already rising, you plant your back foot and roll straight into the fight instead of retreating down the alley. The first exchange leaves your shoulder aching and the golem's chest freshly cracked, a pale line spreading through packed clay like a river finding a new course. Rain drums steady on the cobblestones as you circle, waiting for the next opening, aware that one more clean hit from those fists could end this before you get the chance to land yours.

Somewhere under Waypoint, sewer water rushes loud enough to hear even over the golem's grinding footsteps, and you use the sound to mask your approach along wet brick. Guild lamps flicker overhead, throwing crooked shadows across the narrow lane where the creature finally turns to face you. Its fist splits a nearby rain barrel clean in two, water gushing across the cobbles in a sudden flood that soaks your boots through the leather. You wade through the cold spray anyway, driving your blade toward the crack already spreading fast up its heavy grinding arm, and feel the clay finally give beneath the pressure of a well placed strike.

The golem pauses at the crossing of two crooked streets, dust sifting from its joints like flour spilled from a torn sack, and for a moment you almost believe it might just walk away. It does not. Instead it turns, slow and deliberate, fists already curling closed at its sides. You close the distance fast while it resets, feet slipping on wet stone slick with fog, and land the first real hit of the fight square against the crack running up its chest. The impact shivers up your arm, but the golem barely staggers, mud flaking loose in gray curls that the rain immediately washes toward the gutter and the dark grate beyond.

Rain turns the alley into a black mirror, and the golem's reflection arrives a half second before the creature itself, fists raised, shoulders grinding low against the fog. You roll the dice in your head before you roll them for real, feet braced, breath held tight in your chest. The first swing goes wide, cracking a lamppost instead of your ribs, and glass rains down in bright little pieces that scatter across the cobblestones like coins spilled from a torn purse. You use the chaos, the noise, the sudden light, to slip past its guard entirely and drive your weapon straight into the widening crack along its side.

The crack in the golem's chest widens with a sound like a house settling deep into winter ground, slow and final. Rain drums steady on cobblestones as you circle it, watching gray mud flake loose in long curls that drift toward the gutter and vanish into the dark water below. One more good hit, you think, and the whole crooked wall of clay finally comes down for good. The golem does not seem to notice its own damage, resetting its stance with the same patient certainty it started the fight with, but you have fought enough of these things now to know exactly where the weakness lives, and you aim everything you have straight for it.

A guild banner, soaked through and heavy with days of rain, slaps against the golem's shoulder as it turns toward you, and for one strange moment you see straight through the crack in its chest to something pale and faintly glowing beneath all that packed clay. You do not wait around to ask what that light is actually made of. Instead you close the distance while the banner still hangs tangled across its arm, slowing its next swing just enough to matter. Your blade finds the crack dead center, and the golem lets out a low grinding groan, more mortar than voice, as pale light spills briefly into the fog before the clay closes back over it.

Waypoint's crooked towers lean in close overhead tonight, blotting out what little moon manages to break through the clouds. The golem's eyes, twin points of dull ember, track your every step across the wet stone, patient in a way that makes your skin crawl more than any snarl ever could. You breathe out slow, roll your shoulders once, and close the last few feet between you before it can commit to a swing. The first strike lands solid against its ribs, and the crack there widens with a sound like ice giving way on a frozen river, pale light flickering faint beneath the surface where the clay has finally begun to fail.

The golem drags one leg through standing water at the mouth of the alley, clay sloughing off in gray ribbons that dissolve slowly in the current running toward the sewer grate. It is losing itself a piece at a time, and you intend to help that process along with the sharp end of your blade before it manages to close the distance first. Lamplight flickers overhead as the creature finally lifts its head, ember eyes fixing on you through the fog. You circle wide, watching the crack along its shin catch the light, gold and uneven, and time your next approach to land right as it shifts its weight fully onto the damaged leg beneath it.

Every guild in this crooked district has a story about the golem at the waypoint, and none of them end well for whoever swings first without a plan already in mind. You are about to add your own version to that pile of stories tonight, fists up, breath fogging in the cold night air. The golem meets your first strike with a fist that nearly takes your head off, close enough that you feel the wind of it against your cheek. You answer low, driving your blade beneath its guard toward the crack you spotted earlier, and feel the clay finally shudder under the impact, mud raining down onto the wet cobblestones between you both.

The golem's clay shoulder catches on a low awning as it turns, tearing the canvas loose in one long ragged strip that flutters into a puddle. Rain pours straight through the gap onto its head, and mud runs like tears down a face that was never really meant to have one at all. You use the moment of distraction to close in fast, boots splashing through cold water, and land a solid strike against the crack already spreading across its chest. The golem staggers for the first time all fight, grinding low, and you press the advantage immediately, unwilling to give it even a second to steady itself again.

You count the golem's steps the way you would count a slow, heavy drumbeat, each one grinding closer through the fog and scattered lamplight of the waypoint. When it finally swings, you are already moving, already inside its reach, already committed to the strike before the fist even finishes its arc. The impact against its ribs sends a shiver up your arm and a satisfying crack through the cold night air. Mud flakes loose in curls, catching lamplight as it falls, and the golem groans low, more grinding stone than voice, resetting its stance for another slow attempt at ending this fight in its favor instead of yours.

A stray dog barks once from a doorway as the golem passes through the fog, then thinks better of it and vanishes down a side street without looking back. You wish you had that option too, but instead you grip your weapon tighter and step fully into the lamplight to meet the grinding thing head on, alone in the crooked street. The first swing misses you by a hand's width, cracking cobblestone instead of bone, and you answer immediately with a strike aimed straight at the crack you have been tracking since it first turned to face you. Clay shivers, dust lifts, and for a moment the whole fight seems to hang on that single narrow gap between you.

Somewhere behind the fog a bell tolls the hour, slow and heavy, and the golem answers with a step that shakes loose mortar from a nearby wall. You watch its chest crack wider with every motion it makes, a fault line spreading like frost across old glass in the cold night air, deep and unhurried. You aim your next strike straight for that seam, feet braced against the wet stone, breath fogging in front of you. The blade connects, clay giving way in a shower of gray dust, and the golem lets out a low grinding sound that might, if you squint, almost pass for surprise at how much damage a person so small could do.

Clay dust hangs in the lamplight like snow that forgot how to fall, drifting slow past crooked rooftops and shuttered windows. The golem lumbers forward through it, indifferent to the cold, indifferent to you, fists rising with the same patient certainty it has carried since the fight began at the waypoint, unhurried and grim. Your blade finally finds the crack running up its side, and it staggers, grinding, off balance for the first time tonight. You do not let up, pressing the advantage while dust still falls around you both, aiming every following strike straight at the widening gap before the clay has any real chance to close it.

The alley reeks of river mud and old smoke, the kind that settles into stone and never quite leaves, and the golem's footprints leave puddles that refuse to properly drain away. You feint left, drawing a fist that crashes into a barrel instead of your ribs, splintering the wood into a dozen pieces across the cobblestones. You use the half second of recovery to close in with everything you have got, blade angled for the crack already spreading across its chest. The golem groans, low and grinding, mud sloughing from its shoulder in gray curls that the rain immediately carries toward the gutter and the dark water waiting below.

The golem's fist grazes a lamppost as it swings wide, and glass rains down in bright little pieces that catch the fog like scattered coins tossed carelessly from a torn purse near the guild gate. You use the noise, the sudden light, the half second of chaos, to slip past its guard entirely and close the distance fast. Your blade drives straight into the crack along its ribs, and the clay gives with a sound like ice breaking on a frozen river somewhere far from this crooked street. The golem staggers back a step, grinding low, dust and mud raining down onto wet cobblestones already slick with rain and broken glass.

Fog swallows the tops of the guild towers, leaving only scattered lamplight and the golem's hulking silhouette below on the wet stone. It swings wide, missing you by a hand's width, cracking the cobblestones instead of your ribs, and you answer immediately with a strike of your own aimed low. The blade catches the crack already spreading across its ribs and chest, and clay shivers loose in gray curls that drift down slow through the fog. The golem groans, grinding low, resetting its stance for another attempt, but you have already spotted exactly where the next strike needs to land to finish this properly.

Waypoint's sewers breathe cold air up through an iron grate set into the cobblestones, and the golem's shin catches on the bars as it turns, grinding loud enough to echo off the crooked buildings around you, dust sifting free with every motion it makes tonight in the fog. Your pulse climbs in your throat as you press the advantage, blade aimed straight for the damaged shin before the mud has any real chance to knit itself whole again. The golem staggers, off balance for just a moment, and you use every bit of that moment before the heavy, grinding fight can tip back in its favor once more, mud already sloughing free.

Lamplight catches the golem's shoulder as it turns, revealing a shelf of dried mud where a shield should be, cracked at the edges like old pottery left out too long in the rain and cold. Somewhere above, a rooftop guild bell rings the hour, slow and deep across the fog and crooked chimneys. You plant your feet, breath frosting in the cold night air, and wait for the first swing to fully commit before you answer it. The fist passes wide, cracking cobblestone instead of your ribs, and you drive your blade straight into the widening seam along its chest, feeling clay finally begin to give beneath the pressure.

You find the golem standing motionless at the waypoint when the fog finally thins enough to see it clearly, dust settled thick across its shoulders like old snow that never properly melted in the cold. It does not move until you step within range, and then everything happens at once, fast and heavy. Fists rise, shoulders grind, and you dodge the first swing wide, feeling the wind of it chill your collar. The crack along its ribs catches lamplight for just a moment, pale gold through gray mud, and you commit everything to that single narrow window before it closes and the fight resets to square one.

The rain has not let up since you reached the crooked lane leading to the waypoint, turning every gutter into its own small black river rushing toward the sewer grates. The golem waits beneath a broken guild sign, patient as packed clay, until you finally step close enough to matter to it. It moves all at once, fists rising like a drawbridge slamming shut, and you slip beneath the first heavy swing on instinct alone. The crack in its chest is wider now than when you first spotted it, and you drive your blade home before the gap has any chance to close, mud spraying across the wet stone.

Somebody carved a symbol into the golem's back centuries ago, faint copper glowing beneath layers of river mud and grime, and you only catch a glimpse of it as the creature spins slow through the fog. Dust sifts from every joint, heavy and unhurried, marking exactly where you mean to strike next. The first exchange goes poorly for you, a fist grazing your shoulder hard enough to spin you half around. You recover fast, driving your blade toward the crack along its chest, and feel the clay finally shudder and give, mud raining down onto cobblestones already slick with rain and old dust.

A crack in the cobblestones marks where the golem last put its fist down in anger, deep enough to catch a careless boot heel, and you skirt around it while the creature resets its stance for another slow, heavy swing. Fog curls thick between the crooked towers overhead, muffling every sound of your own breathing. You time your approach to match its slowest recovery, closing the distance fast while it is still grinding back into position. The strike lands clean against the crack in its chest, and the golem groans low, mud sloughing free in gray curls that vanish into the rain-slick gutter beside you.

The golem's footsteps shake dust loose from centuries-old shutters overhead, and somewhere nearby a cat yowls once and bolts hard for the rooftops, vanishing into the fog without looking back. You do not have that particular option tonight, not with the waypoint blocked behind this grinding thing. You square your stance, feet braced on the wet cobblestones, and wait for the first swing to fully commit before you answer it with everything you have got. The blade finds the crack along its ribs dead center, and clay gives way in a shower of gray dust that drifts slow through the lamplight between you.

The golem's back bears a symbol worn smooth by centuries of rain, faint copper glinting under lamplight whenever it turns just right through the fog. You circle it slow, watching dust sift from every crooked joint, patient as the creature itself while you wait for a real opening to appear. When it finally swings, you slip inside the arc and drive your blade toward the crack spreading up its side, feeling clay shudder and give beneath the strike. Mud rains down onto the wet cobblestones, and the golem groans low, grinding stone answering the damage in the only voice it has left to use in this whole crooked, fog-bound street.

The canal fog rolls in thick tonight, swallowing the lamplight one post at a time until the causeway is a corridor of white. Somewhere in it the revenant is already moving, dragging chains that never touch the frozen boards, patient as rot working through old timber. You hear it before you see it, a sound like a door closing over and over, in some house that burned down years ago. When it finally steps clear of the fog its coat hangs open over ribs showing through in the lamplight, buttoned wrong from collar to hem. It does not run at you. It simply arrives, the way fog arrives, filling every gap until there is nowhere left to stand except directly in its path. You plant your feet, steady your breath, and let the dice decide who walks away.

Down in the sewer the water runs fast and black, carrying old rafts of driftwood past drowned lantern posts that have not held a flame in years. Something rides that current toward you, patient, cold, uninterested in your reasons for being here at all. You count your breaths in the dark, three, two, one, and grip your weapon as the sound of dragging chains grows louder against the tunnel stone. It hauls itself out of the black water slick and silent, a coat heavy with runoff clinging to shoulders that remember standing somewhere warmer, once. For a moment it simply stands there, remembering how doors used to open for it, how streets used to have names it recognized. Then it turns its hollow face toward you, and the fog closes around you both like a held breath deciding to fall.

Sixteen floors of a leaning tower rise dark above the street, every window broken except one, where a single candle burns behind cracked glass. Nobody has trimmed that wick in years, yet it never drowns in its own wax, as if something climbs the stairs each night just to relight it. Tonight you are the one climbing, listening for the sound that means it already knows you are here. The revenant meets you on the ninth landing, coat dragging across dust nobody has disturbed in a decade, edges dissolving into smoke whenever candlelight flickers through the floor below. It does not speak. It only reaches for the banister the way it must have the night it fell, or was pushed, or forgot how stairs worked. You raise your weapon and refuse to let sentiment slow your hand.

The old ferryman's post stands empty at the mooring, ropes frayed to threads, and no boat has tied up since the flood took the last one downstream. Tonight something climbs out of the black water anyway, dragging behind it a coat it no longer needs to keep dry, water pooling black wherever its footsteps land. You watch from the causeway, weapon ready, breath held. It crosses the mooring slowly, the way something crosses when it has already died once and found the process unremarkable the second time around. Twin lanterns swing overhead though no wind blows, and between their light and its own shadow the revenant flickers in and out of being solid. You do not wait for it to decide which version of itself to commit to. You strike first, and hope the dice favor the quick.

A jar of tears sits sealed on a windowsill above the alley, closed with a single tear of blue wax that never finished dripping down the glass. The revenant drifts toward it as though it still remembers crying once, before the coat it wears stopped needing warmth or anything the living ask for. You watch it pause beneath the window, hollow face tilted up, almost gentle for one moment. Then the moment breaks, the way moments always break around it, and it turns on you with the patience of something that has nothing left to lose by waiting one more year. Chains it never needed drag behind it across the cobblestones, and the fog thickens around your boots like it wants to hold you still. You do not let sentiment slow your hand. The dice favor whoever strikes first tonight.

Steam rises off the canal in heavy sheets tonight, hiding the far bank and turning every lamp along the causeway into a smear of orange behind white. Somewhere in that steam the revenant is already moving, tracking your boots on the boards with the patience of rot working through old timber, unseen and unhurried. You grip your weapon and try not to let the fog convince you you are alone. It comes out of the steam without warning, coat dripping, ribs showing pale where the buttons gape open across a chest that stopped needing breath decades ago. For one long moment neither of you moves, the causeway silent except for water lapping below the boards. Then it lunges, and you meet it, and the only sound left in the fogbound street is steel against something that was never quite steel at all.

The mourners left the doorway open on the house at the end of the lane, candle burning low on the sill, waiting for someone who stopped coming home years ago. The revenant drifts through that unlatched door most nights like it still has somewhere to be before dawn breaks. Tonight you are waiting inside, weapon drawn, watching the flame bend as something colder than wind slips through. It does not notice you at first, moving instead toward the single chair still set at the table, reaching for a cup emptied and washed a decade ago. You let it have that half second of forgetting before you strike, because even monsters deserve one breath of peace before the fight begins. Then the chains rattle, the candle gutters, and the small house fills with fog and the sound of something remembering what it lost.

Behind the shuttered bakery a chain rattles once, then twice, and goes utterly still, the silence somehow louder than the sound before it. You already know what that means. Whatever pulled that chain is done waiting patiently in the dark and has decided instead to come find you where you stand, fog trailing behind it like a torn cloak dragged through the gutters. It rounds the corner slower than expected, almost unhurried, boots that leave no print landing soundless on cobblestones slick with old rain. Twenty years of standing frozen at some crossroads have not dulled whatever patience it was built with, and it studies you now the way a bell studies an hour it has not rung in decades. You raise your weapon first. Out here, hesitation is the only mistake that matters.

The lamplighter never came back for this street after the fever took half the guild, and the dark has grown teeth since, patient and slow and unbothered by how long collecting takes. Something pale drifts between the dead lamps tonight with the certainty of a debt finally deciding to call itself due, in full, with interest owed on every unlit year. You feel it before you see it, that particular cold that has nothing to do with the season, and then it is simply there, closer than the fog should have allowed unnoticed. Its coat is buttoned wrong, one hole skipped from collar to hem, as though whoever dressed it that morning had been in far more of a hurry than they wanted anyone to notice. You do not give it time to finish remembering. You strike, and the street goes quiet again.

A single point of candlelight marks the window where the old vigil never ended, burning through fog thick enough to swallow every other light on the street. The revenant drifts toward it like a moth that forgot it used to fear the flame more than the dark, circling closer with each pass, never quite touching the glass. You watch from the shadows, weapon ready, waiting to see which way it turns. It turns toward you instead, the way something turns when it has all the time in the world left to spend and has finally chosen tonight to spend every last minute of it right here. Chains drag across cobblestones slick with old rain, and the candle flickers once, as if startled by the sudden violence of its attention. You meet it head on, because retreating now would only teach it that running works.

Between two leaning rooftops the fog gathers slowly into a shape with a coat and empty sleeves, edges dissolving and reforming as a gust cuts through the alley below. It turns toward you at its own unhurried pace, the way something turns when it has nowhere left to be and nothing left to lose by taking its time. You steady your grip and watch the gap for whatever shape it settles on. When it drops into the alley its boots make no sound at all against the cobblestones, twenty years of habit worn smooth into something closer to memory than motion. The chains it drags were never attached to anything, just habit again, just weight it carries because letting go would mean admitting it is finished. You do not let it finish remembering. You strike first, and the fog scatters like startled birds.

The revenant remembers a door it never got to knock on, and now it drifts the alley outside that same house every single foggy night, patient, cold, and utterly unwilling to let one more visitor simply pass it by unnoticed. Tonight you are the visitor, and it meets you three steps from the stoop with chains dragging soundless across cobblestones slick from a rain that stopped hours ago. Up close its coat is buttoned wrong, one hole skipped from collar to hem, the way a hand shaking too hard to count properly might leave it. You do not have time to feel sorry for whatever it used to be before the dice decide the fight for both of you. It lunges, you meet it, and for one long moment the whole alley is nothing but fog and steel and the sound of something finally getting the answer it came for.

Ten long years the causeway has stood empty, its lanterns unlit, boards frozen solid under a crust of old frost that never quite melts even in summer. Tonight it finally has a visitor again, gliding across those frozen boards with a hunger that outlasted its own heartbeat by two decades and is still counting, patiently, toward whatever crosses next. That happens to be you. You feel the cold reach for you before the shape does, curling up through your boots like the causeway remembers what walked here last. The revenant closes the distance without hurry, coat dragging, chains dragging, everything about it built for patience rather than speed. You do not match its pace. You close the gap yourself, weapon first, because waiting only ever favors the thing that has already died once.

Rain drums steady on tin roofs above the guild district tonight, and beneath that endless sound something drags itself slowly up from a drowned cellar nobody has opened in years. Clawed fingers find purchase on stone that remembers every step it has taken, patient, cold, indifferent to the storm rattling shutters three floors above. You wait at the top of the stairs, listening to it climb. It reaches the doorway soaked through, coat heavy with cellar water, ribs showing pale where the buttons gape open across a chest that gave up breathing decades ago. For one moment it stands in the frame, remembering doors, remembering thresholds, remembering all the houses it used to be welcome inside. Then it steps through anyway, uninvited, and you meet it there because some fights only end at the door.

The old golem frozen at the crossroads has rust for eyes, but tonight something moves behind them, cold and patient, waiting for you to step within reach of hands that have not closed on anything living in a very long, forgotten time. You keep your distance at first, circling wide across cobblestones slick with fog, trying to read which way it means to lunge. It does not lunge so much as unfold, joints grinding loose after twenty years locked at that same crossroads, chains dragging from somewhere beneath the rust like they grew there instead of being carried. The revenant riding it, or wearing it, or simply borrowing its shape for the night, turns a hollow face toward you and waits, patient as stone, for you to make the first careless mistake of the whole encounter.

The clay jar shatters loudly on the cobblestones outside the shuttered chandlery, and out of the dust that was once someone's collected grief the revenant gathers itself, tired and patient, reaching toward the only warm thing left in the silent street tonight, which happens to be you standing three steps back, weapon already drawn. It does not rush the gathering, taking its time the way only something with nothing left to lose can afford to. Chains trail from its sleeves though nothing binds them there, dragging across broken clay and old wax with a sound like a bell that gave up ringing years ago. You do not wait for it to finish assembling itself into whatever shape it means to fight in. You strike while it is still deciding, and the dice favor that nerve.

Fog curls up thick from the storm drains tonight, hiding your own boots from sight and turning the whole alley into a corridor of white you can only navigate by sound. Somewhere inside that white the revenant is counting your footsteps, patient, cold, and far closer than the sound would ever suggest, close enough almost to touch you before you know it has moved at all. You stop walking, forcing it to close the last of the distance on its own, and the fog finally parts enough to show a coat dragging across cobblestones and ribs showing pale beneath open buttons. It does not hesitate once it sees you have stopped running. Chains rattle, cold rolls off it in a wave, and you raise your weapon and meet whatever comes next standing exactly where you chose to stand.

The gutters run black tonight, thick with old rain that never quite drained away, and somewhere beneath the cobbles something clawed drags itself upward one floor at a time, patient as a bell that has not rung in twenty long years. You hear it long before you see it, boots on rooftops overhead, working steadily closer to whichever window it means to climb through first. It surfaces at last through a broken grate three doors down, coat soaked black, hollow face tilting up toward the one lit window on the whole street as though it remembers exactly which house used to be its own. You step between it and that window before it gets the chance to decide anything. The fight that follows is short, cold, and settled entirely by whoever lands the first honest hit.

Twin lanterns swing from a mooring post nobody has tied a boat to in twenty years, creaking against a wind that never seems to actually blow, and between them the revenant drifts, feet not quite touching the frozen boards below. You watch it circle the empty mooring twice, as if still searching for whatever boat it once expected to find waiting there. On the third pass it notices you instead, and the searching stops all at once, replaced by something colder and far more focused. Chains it never needed drag across the frost, and its coat, buttoned wrong from collar to hem, snaps once in wind you cannot feel yourself. You grip your weapon, plant your stance, and refuse to let its cold reach any further than the tip of your blade allows.

Halfway across the causeway the fog thickens all at once, swallowing the far railing completely, and something pale steps out of that whiteness wearing a coat twenty years out of fashion, ribs showing through where the old buttons gape open wide across a chest gone hollow long ago. It says nothing at all. It only waits, patient, for you to make the first careless mistake of the crossing. You do not oblige it. You hold your ground at the causeway's midpoint, weapon steady, and let the silence stretch until it decides on its own to close the distance instead. When it finally moves, it moves all at once, chains dragging soundless across frozen boards, and the whole fight condenses into three fast exchanges before the fog swallows the losing side completely.

Somewhere a bell tolls the wrong hour from a tower nobody has climbed in years, and the revenant answers it by drifting closer down the lane, unfinished business trailing behind it like smoke off a snuffed candle wick. You do not run from the sound of it. Running only ever fed the thing that has hunted these narrow alleys for two decades running. It closes the distance at its own unhurried pace, coat dragging, chains dragging, every part of it built for patience instead of speed. When it finally reaches striking range the bell tolls again, wrong hour still, and you use that half second of its attention drifting toward the sound to land the first honest hit of the whole encounter. The fight that follows never quite escapes the bell's strange rhythm.

The longsword rusting in the fountain at the plaza's center has sat there since the flood took the square, green with age and half sunk in silt nobody has bothered to clear. Tonight the revenant reaches for it anyway with fingers made of fog, remembering a grip it lost the very day the water finally closed over its head for good, twenty cold winters back. Its hand passes through the hilt twice before frustration, or something close enough to it, turns its hollow face toward you instead. You do not give it time to decide you make an easier target than an old sword it cannot even lift anymore. You strike first, weapon steady, and let the fountain's still water finally reflect something other than fog and old rust.

A blind alley off the canal ends in a locked door, and that is exactly where the revenant wants you, cornered between crooked brick walls with nothing behind you but fog and the sound of its slow, deliberate, approaching feet closing the last of the distance. You test the door once, quick, and find it exactly as locked as you feared it would be. There is no way out but through, and you turn to face that fact squarely as the fog thickens at the alley's mouth. Chains drag across wet brick, and the cold that precedes it settles into the dead end like water finding its level. You plant your feet against the locked door at your back and meet the revenant on ground it chose, because sometimes the only choice left is how hard you fight for it.

The old canal breathes steam even at night, warm water meeting cold air in slow ribbons that hide the towpath completely from view. Out of that steam the revenant comes, dragging chains that never quite touch the frozen ground beneath it, moving with the terrible patience of something that already knows exactly how this crossing usually ends. You count your breaths in the dark, three, two, one, and step forward before your nerve has time to reconsider the whole plan. It meets you halfway down the towpath, coat dripping steam, hollow face turning slow to track your approach. The fight that follows is fast and cold and settled, in the end, by whichever one of you refused to hesitate first.

Rooftops lean close together over the alley here, gutters clogged with years of ash, and the revenant floats between them like smoke that forgot how to ever rise again properly. Your candle gutters low in the wind funneling down between the buildings, and still you do not look away from the shape drifting closer through the dark above the street. It drops down between two chimneys without a sound, boots leaving no print in the ash coating every rooftop tile. For a moment it simply hovers there, studying you the way something studies a door it has not yet decided whether to knock on. Then the chains rattle and the decision is made for both of you, and the fight spills out fast across cobblestones slick with soot and old rain.

The old canal district goes quiet after midnight, every shop shuttered and every window dark except the one candle nobody ever remembers to blow out on the corner of the chandlery. The revenant favors that particular corner most nights, drifting past the flame like it half remembers what warmth used to mean before the cold became permanent and total. Tonight you are waiting there instead of walking past, weapon drawn, watching the candlelight throw strange shadows across a coat buttoned wrong from collar to hem. It notices you the moment the shadows shift unnaturally, and closes the distance without hesitation, chains dragging soundless over cobblestones slick from a rain that stopped an hour before you arrived.

A chain drags somewhere below the old drawbridge, slow and rhythmic, the sound carrying strange and hollow across water that has not seen a boat in years. You lean over the rail once, quick, and see nothing but black canal and drifting fog reflected back at you from below the ironwork. The revenant comes up over the railing instead of through the water, coat streaming, hollow face rising level with yours before you have fully registered the motion. For one frozen second neither of you moves, the whole bridge holding its breath around you both. Then it lunges, chains rattling loose against the ironwork, and you meet the attack exactly where you are standing, no room left to retreat toward.

The chandlery on the corner has sat empty since the owner vanished one foggy autumn years ago, candles still lined up unsold in the dusty window behind cracked glass nobody bothered to replace. The revenant pauses there most nights, hollow face tilted toward the candles like it remembers exactly what they were meant for, once, before the cold took over entirely. Tonight you catch it mid pause, weapon already drawn, and it turns from the window slow and deliberate, chains dragging across a threshold worn smooth by two decades of this same nightly ritual. It does not rush you even now. It simply closes the distance at its own unhurried pace, and you meet it there, right at the door it can never quite bring itself to open.

Beneath the old drawbridge the water runs black and slow, carrying driftwood rafts past a mooring nobody has used since the flood took the last real boat downstream. Something rides that current toward the bank tonight, patient, cold, dragging chains that catch on nothing but habit and old rust. It climbs onto the bank soaked through, coat heavy, boots leaving no print in the mud despite the weight it clearly still carries somehow. For a moment it simply stands there dripping, remembering currents, remembering crossings, remembering every single trip it made across this canal before the last one went wrong. Then it turns toward you, and the fight that follows is fast, cold, and entirely unforgiving of hesitation.

Fog pours down the chute between two crooked towers tonight, thick enough to swallow the lamplight at street level completely, and out of it steps the revenant, moving with the terrible calm of something that has already died once and found the whole process unremarkable the second time it happened, back when the towers still stood straight and true. You watch it settle into the alley below, chains dragging soundless, coat buttoned wrong from collar to hem exactly as it always is. There is no surprise left in this encounter, only the familiar cold and the familiar patience and the familiar sound of a door closing somewhere that burned down long ago. You raise your weapon anyway, because familiar does not mean safe, and meet it standing your ground.

The revenant's coat is buttoned wrong, one hole skipped from collar to hem, as though whoever dressed it that last cold morning had been in far more of a hurry than they ever wanted anyone else to notice at the time, and no one ever did, not once, not even now, twenty years and counting into whatever this has become. You notice the small detail before anything else about it, before the chains, before the cold, before the hollow patient face turning slowly to track your approach down the alley. It is almost funny, in a bleak sort of way, that something so far past caring still wears its old mistakes so plainly. You do not let the almost-humor slow your hand. You strike, and the buttoned wrong coat finally, mercifully, comes undone.

Behind the guild hall the old well has been dry for decades, and yet something climbs out of it tonight anyway, dragging a chain that catches on the bucket rope still coiled at the rim from whoever used this well last. Cold rolls off it in a slow wave that fogs the stones around the wellhead completely. You back away from the well's edge, giving yourself room to swing, and watch it haul itself fully upright with the unhurried patience of something that has nowhere left to be in any hurry. Its coat drips well water that should have run dry with everything else down there years ago. You meet its first lunge standing well clear of the wellhead, because falling in is not a mistake you intend to make twice.

The fog over the tannery district never quite lifts, and tonight it carries a warmth that has no business coming off cold stone. You track the smell of hot fur down a gutter that runs uphill, past shopfronts shuttered so long the paint has peeled to bare wood. Somewhere a chestnut vendor's abandoned cart still smolders under a fresh dusting of ash. At the end of the lane a gentleman in a very good coat waits with his hands folded behind his back, and when he turns to greet you, you see why. His fingers bend the wrong way at the knuckle, backward toward the wrist, and his smile shows one row of teeth too many. He offers a bow so perfect it could only be practiced. You do not return it. You draw your weapon, and somewhere close, something that is not quite a man begins, very softly, to purr.

Rooftops crowd so close over this quarter that daylight rarely reaches the cobbles, and by lamplight the gap between buildings looks like a single unbroken ceiling, fog pooling along its seams like smoke trapped indoors. You climb a rickety fire escape because the street below has gone strangely quiet. At the top, a shape crouches on the ledge with a tiger's face and the poise of a courtier attending a garden party. It does not startle at your arrival; it seems to have been expecting you for some time, patient as rent coming due. Its tail flicks once, twice, gauging the width of the gap between your ledge and its own. You test your grip on the rail and refuse to look down, because down is exactly where it wants your attention, and up here you can still see its hands.

The sewer stair behind the guildhall breathes warm air up into the cold night, wrong in a way you feel before you understand it. You go down anyway, one hand on the slick brick, boots finding the worn centers of stairs other feet have used for a very long time. Water drips in a rhythm too even to be natural. At the bottom a lantern burns behind a grate, and behind it a shape sits with knees drawn up and hands folded backward at the wrist, watching you arrive with the patience of something that has never had to chase its dinner. It speaks first, voice honey smooth, offering safe passage if you simply turn around. You do not answer. You raise your weapon, and the lantern gutters as if the thing behind the grate finally exhaled.

Fog rolls off the river and pools thick in the alley behind the fishmonger's stalls, carrying the smell of brine and something else underneath it, warm and wrong and close. A cracked mirror leans against a crate where someone left it to be collected, and in its glass your reflection stands a half second behind where you actually are, as if it is still catching up. You do not trust the mirror, and you are right not to. A tiger's shadow slides across the glass behind your reflection, though the alley behind you shows nothing when you finally risk a glance. Somewhere close, a low chuckle rides the fog, delighted that you noticed. You turn your back on the mirror entirely and face the alley instead, weapon raised, because whatever this is, it wants you watching the wrong thing.

The guild seal on the door has been snapped clean in half, wax still faintly warm, and beyond the broken lock the hall sits dark except for one guttering candle someone left burning on purpose. Coins are scattered across the floor in a trail too neat to be an accident, each one placed like a lure toward the back room where the candle's light does not quite reach. You follow the coins because the alternative is standing in the dark waiting for whatever scattered them to come find you first. At the end of the trail a shape rises from behind the guildmaster's overturned chair, backward hands folded, a tiger's face arranged into something almost like an apology. It is not sorry. You can tell by the way it is still smiling when you step past every last coin and refuse the invitation.

Rain slicks the rooftop tiles until every step is a gamble, and you take the gamble anyway because the alternative is the alley below, where something with a gentleman's coat and a predator's patience has been pacing for the better part of an hour. From up here you can see the whole crooked skyline, chimneys leaning together like old men gossiping in the wet dark. A shape detaches from the shadow of the nearest chimney stack, unbothered by the rain, unbothered by the height, unbothered by anything at all as far as you can tell. Its tail flicks once in greeting, or maybe warning; with a tiger it is hard to say which. You plant your feet on the wet tile and refuse to give ground, because one wrong step here means a very long fall, and this thing knows it better than you do.

The alley behind the tannery reeks of curing hide and hot fur in equal measure, the two smells tangled so thoroughly you cannot tell where one ends and the other begins. Claw marks score the doorframe at exactly eye height, four parallel gouges deep enough to catch a careless boot heel, as if something paused here to measure you for a fitting. You step past the marks anyway, because turning back means admitting the fog has already decided how this goes. Behind the door, low and pleased, something purrs at the sound of your approach, entirely certain of its welcome. When the door finally opens on its own, unlatched from the inside, a gentleman's silhouette stands framed in lamplight, hands folded backward at the wrist, offering you a bow you have no intention of returning.

A decanter of dark liquor sits untouched on an overturned barrel in the middle of the guild courtyard, offered up like a peace treaty nobody signed and nobody trusts. The fog around it pools strangely thick, curling close to the ground the way smoke does when there is no wind to carry it anywhere else. Whatever left the decanter is still close, watching from just beyond the lamplight with eyes that catch the glow wrong, too bright, too golden, too patient. You leave the offering exactly where it sits and turn slowly to face the dark instead, weapon raised, because whatever wants you distracted by a drink is exactly the thing you need to be watching. A low chuckle answers your refusal, warm as honey and twice as false.

The lamplight along this stretch of the gutter flickers in a pattern too deliberate to be a dying wick, on and off, on and off, like something is testing how the shadows fall. You count the flickers without meaning to, and somewhere around the seventh one a shape steps fully into view where a moment ago there was only empty cobblestone. It wears a gentleman's coat over a tiger's face, an arrangement that should look absurd and instead belongs perfectly to this rotten, dangerous street. Its hands are folded behind its back, bent backward at the wrist in a way that turns your stomach even from a distance. It offers a bargain in a voice like honey poured over gravel. You draw your weapon, and the lamplight steadies at last, as if it too was waiting to see what you would choose.

A trail of cracked chestnut shells leads away from the overturned market cart, each shell split open in turn like a countdown someone patient is running just for you. You follow it past shuttered stalls and a guild banner sagging heavy with rain, the fog thickening with every step until the lamplight behind you is only a smear of gold. At the last shell, a tiger's tail flicks once from behind a chimney stack high above, and the whole rooftop goes still in a way that feels rehearsed, waiting for its cue. You look up just as the shape drops soundlessly to the cobbles ahead, landing in a crouch far too graceful for something so large. It rises into a gentleman's posture, brushing dust from its coat, and smiles with far too many teeth for the moment to feel like anything but a trap.

Water drips through a broken skylight above the shuttered guildhall, each drop landing in a puddle that has slowly grown wide enough to reflect the whole ruined ceiling. You cross the room carefully, boots silent on old floorboards, and try not to look down at the puddle more than you have to. You look anyway, and the reflection staring back is wrong: a tiger's face where your own should be, patient and amused and entirely unbothered that you have noticed. You do not flinch, though every instinct screams to run. Instead you step straight through the puddle, scattering the reflection into a hundred rippling pieces, and somewhere behind you in the real dark, a low, satisfied purr confirms that the thing wearing your face was never really trapped in the water at all.

The rope ladder hanging from the rooftop should not be there, not on a building this abandoned, not swaying gently when there is no wind to move it. You test the first rung anyway, because the alley behind you has gone too quiet for comfort and the roof at least offers a clear line of sight. At the top a shape waits with a gentleman's posture and a predator's patience, watching you climb with obvious, unhurried amusement, as though your effort is the evening's entertainment. It offers a hand to help you over the ledge, fingers bent backward at the wrist in a way no human hand should bend. You ignore the hand and haul yourself up on your own, weapon ready, because whatever help this thing is offering always, always comes with a price you have not agreed to pay.

The guild apprentice's satchel lies open on the wet cobbles, its contents perfectly undisturbed, which is the single wrongest detail in an alley full of wrong details. Coins, a stub of chalk, a half eaten chestnut, all arranged as if someone wanted you to stop and take inventory before continuing on. You do not stop for long. Something wanted your attention here, and that alone is reason enough to keep moving toward the true trail, the one marked by claw gouges in the brick and a smell of hot fur growing steadily stronger. At the alley's end a gentleman's silhouette waits beneath a dead lamppost, hands folded backward, smiling the exact patient smile of someone who left the satchel there on purpose and is delighted that you noticed but did not linger.

Fog swallows the mouth of the sewer stair whole, thick enough that the lamplight behind you barely reaches past your own shoulder. From somewhere down in that dark comes a low, satisfied purr, unhurried and confident, the sound of something that already believes tonight's hunt is as good as finished before it has properly begun. You start down anyway, one careful step at a time, letting your eyes adjust to the dark. At the bottom, a shape rises from a puddle, tiger faced, gentleman dressed, backward hands spread in a mocking bow of welcome. It compliments your nerve in a voice like honey over gravel, expecting the words to unsettle you more than the claws do. You raise your weapon instead of trembling, and for just a moment, its confident smile flickers.

The guild banner above the courtyard hangs sodden and heavy with rain, dripping steadily onto cobbles already slick from the storm that passed through an hour ago. Paw prints track across the gravel toward a shuttered side door, each print the size of a dinner plate and pressed deep enough to hold standing water. You follow the prints because the alternative is waiting here in the open, and open ground favors whatever left tracks that large. Behind the shuttered door something purrs, low and pleased, entirely certain of its welcome, the sound of a predator that has never once had to work very hard for its supper. You test the door and find it unlocked, swinging open on oiled hinges that someone bothered to maintain, which tells you this welcome has been prepared for far longer than tonight.

Between one guttering lamppost and the next, the fog thickens into something with a shape, reversed hands folding into a courtier's bow so smooth it could only have been practiced for centuries. It speaks your name wrong on purpose, testing the shape of your flinch the way a tailor tests a hem. The cobbles underfoot are slick with old rain and older grime, and you plant your feet carefully before answering with steel instead of words. It does not seem surprised by your silence; if anything, the tiger's mouth curls a little wider, as though silence was the answer it wanted all along. Somewhere behind it, a second lamppost gutters out on its own, plunging half the alley into a dark that feels suddenly, deliberately, much larger than it was a moment ago.

A cracked shop window along the tanners' row shows a reflection standing just behind where you are, though the street at your back is entirely, verifiably empty when your nerve finally breaks and you glance. You do not look back a second time. You watch the glass instead, counting your own heartbeat against whatever rhythm the reflection is keeping. The tiger's face in the glass smiles a half second before your own reflection does, patient as a debt collector who already knows you cannot pay. You strike the moment the reflection's hand moves first, shattering the window in a spray of glass that sounds far too satisfying for how much your own hand is shaking. Behind you, in the real dark, something laughs low and warm, delighted that you finally decided to stop watching and start fighting.

The alley narrows until your shoulders nearly brush both walls, funneling you toward a single crooked door marked with a symbol scratched fresh into old wood, the paint of it still faintly wet to the touch. Behind the door something purrs, content and unhurried, utterly certain that whatever comes through will be worth the wait. You test the handle and it turns without the slightest resistance, oiled and ready, which alone tells you everything you need to know before you ever step through. Inside, lamplight throws a gentleman's shadow twice as tall as the room should allow, backward hands already folded in greeting. It thanks you for coming so promptly, as though you had any real choice in the matter, and you answer by raising your weapon instead of returning the courtesy.

Steam hisses continuously from a broken grate as you cross the narrow alley, fog parting just long enough to show two hands folded in polite greeting, fingers bent backward at every knuckle in a way that makes the greeting feel like a threat dressed up in manners. The face beneath the hood is almost kind, almost human, almost, disturbingly, almost yours. You keep both eyes fixed on the claws and neither on the smile, because the smile is clearly meant to be the distraction. It speaks in a voice like honey poured slow over gravel, offering terms you have no intention of considering. Somewhere behind it a second shape flickers into being and out again, a trick of fog and lamplight, or perhaps not a trick at all. You do not wait to find out which. You close the distance instead.

Rain hammers the tiles of a rooftop three stories above the guildhall, and you climb toward it because the street below has gone unnervingly still, the specific stillness that settles right before something large decides to move. Chimneys lean together against the wind like old friends sharing a secret they would rather not tell you. At the top, a shape crouches easily on the narrow ledge, entirely unbothered by height or weather, a tiger's face turned toward you with an expression that might be curiosity or might simply be hunger wearing a patient mask. It rises slowly into a gentleman's posture, brushing rain from a coat that somehow stays perfectly dry. You grip the wet rail and refuse to look away first, because looking away, on a ledge like this, is the only mistake that actually matters.

The guild courtyard gate hangs open on one hinge, swinging slowly in a wind you cannot otherwise feel, and beyond it the cobbles are scattered with coins arranged in a line too deliberate to be anything but bait. Fog curls low around each coin, pooling thick enough to hide whatever waits at the trail's end. You follow the coins step by careful step, counting them without meaning to, until the trail ends at an overturned chair and a shape rising slowly from behind it. Backward hands fold in greeting, a tiger's mouth curls into something almost like an apology it clearly does not feel. It calls the coins a gift, a welcome, an honest offer of hospitality. You step past every last one and answer with your weapon instead, because honest is the last word this thing has earned.

Fog pools thick around a lamppost at the alley's mouth, and within that fog something folds its long fingers backward, knuckle bending toward the wrist in a slow, deliberate gesture clearly meant to unsettle you before a single word is spoken. It works, at least a little; you feel your grip tighten on your weapon without quite deciding to. You breathe once, twice, and step through the fog anyway, because the alternative is standing still while it decides how this goes, and standing still has never once won a fight in this city. The shape resolves fully as you approach, gentleman's coat over a tiger's face, hands already folding into a mocking little bow. It speaks first, voice warm as honey, but you are already moving before it finishes the sentence.

A guild lantern above the alley gutters and relights itself twice in a row, far too deliberate to be wind, the flame flickering in a rhythm that feels like a signal from something that wants very badly to be noticed. You keep walking anyway, boots loud and deliberate on the wet stone, refusing to let flickering light dictate your pace. At the alley's end the lantern finally steadies, and in its restored glow a shape waits with a gentleman's posture, a tiger's face, and hands folded backward at the wrist in patient, practiced welcome. It compliments your composure, clearly disappointed that the flickering game did not rattle you more. You do not answer the compliment. You close the distance instead, weapon raised, while the lantern behind you burns steady at last.

The gravel path behind the guildhall glitters with scattered coins in the lamplight, each one placed with the precision of a lure rather than the carelessness of an accident, leading straight toward a shadowed archway where the fog pools thickest. Somewhere within that shadow, something waits with the patience of a debt collector counting interest. You step past every coin without slowing, because stopping to collect them is exactly what the trail wants you to do. At the archway a shape steps forward, backward hands folded, tiger's face arranged into a courtier's polite smile. It calls the coins a small hospitality, a gesture of goodwill before the real business begins. You answer by drawing your weapon, and the polite smile widens into something with entirely too many teeth for goodwill.

The tannery alley reeks of curing hide, hot fur, and old rain in a combination that makes your stomach turn before you have even seen the source. Claw marks score the doorframe ahead at precisely eye height, four parallel gouges deep enough to catch a careless boot heel, as though something paused here once just to measure a previous visitor for a fitting. You step past the marks anyway, refusing to be measured for anything tonight. Behind the door, low and pleased, something purrs at the sound of your footsteps, utterly certain of its welcome and entirely unbothered by your weapon. When the door swings open on its own, unlatched from within, a gentleman's silhouette stands waiting in lamplight, offering a bow you have absolutely no intention of returning in kind.

A cat yowls once above the rooftops and cuts off mid cry, and the silence that follows presses down heavier than the sound ever did, thick enough that you can hear your own pulse in your ears. You climb the fire escape two rungs at a time anyway, fog curling cold around your boots with every step upward. At the top, a tiger's grin waits patiently in the lamplight, entirely too pleased that you bothered coming to look for whatever made that sound. It sits with a gentleman's posture, backward hands folded neatly in its lap, as though it has all the time in the world and knows you do not share that luxury. You raise your weapon and refuse to let the silence, or the grin, decide how this next moment unfolds.

The sewer entrance behind the fish market breathes out warm, wrong air that has no business rising from anything as cold as sewage, and painted crudely on the brick above it is a sigil someone scratched in a visible hurry, more warning than welcome. The door beside it hangs ajar, deliberately, invitingly. Somewhere below, a voice like honey poured over gravel calls up an invitation you have absolutely no intention of accepting on its terms. You go down anyway, weapon first, boots finding the worn centers of steps that countless other feet have used before yours. At the bottom, a shape waits with a tiger's face and a gentleman's folded hands, delighted that you came, disappointed that you came armed and unwilling to talk.

Rain slicks the cobbles outside the guildhall until every surface gleams with borrowed lamplight, and a gentleman's hat rolls past your feet, empty, its owner nowhere within sight along the whole rain slicked street. Behind it comes a low chuckle from somewhere deep in the wet dark, patient and entirely unhurried. You do not bother chasing the hat, because chasing it is clearly the point of leaving it there in the first place. You watch the alley mouth instead, where a shape with backward hands is already stepping calmly out to meet you, tiger's face arranged into an expression of polite, practiced surprise at your restraint. It compliments you on not taking the bait. You answer by raising your weapon instead of accepting the compliment gracefully.

A guild seal lies snapped clean in half on the wet cobbles, its wax still faintly warm, whoever broke it barely gone from sight around the nearest corner. The fog around the alley mouth smells thick of hot fur and old honey, a combination that has come to mean exactly one thing in this part of the city. Somewhere close a voice hums a tune that is almost a lullaby and entirely, unmistakably a threat dressed up in something gentler. You follow the hum past shuttered stalls and a sagging guild banner until it resolves into a shape with a tiger's face and a courtier's folded hands, waiting beneath a lamppost as though it had all the patience in the world to spare on you tonight. You do not return the tune. You draw your weapon and let the humming stop on its own.

The lamplight throws two shadows from a single shape crouched on the gutter's edge, one distinctly human, one very much not, the two of them seeming to argue silently over which face ought to be worn for the occasion of your arrival. Fog curls thick around the disagreement, muffling whatever quiet sounds it makes. You do not wait around for it to decide. You close the gap while it is still busy being two things at once, weapon raised before either shadow has settled into something solid enough to fight back properly. It notices you a beat too late, backward hands only halfway folded into a greeting, tiger's face still half formed beneath the human one. Whatever advantage indecision cost it, you intend to spend before it gets the chance to recover.

A backward handprint presses wet into the fogged glass of a shopfront window, five fingers splayed the wrong direction from any human wrist, pressed there recently enough that condensation is still creeping back in around its edges. Whatever left it is still close enough to watch you notice, patient and clearly entertained by your reaction. You back slowly toward open street, keeping your weapon raised the entire way, refusing to turn your back on the handprint or whatever left it behind. A chuckle drifts out of the fog, warm as honey, complimenting your caution in a voice that makes the compliment feel like an insult. You do not answer. You simply wait for the shape to finally step fully into the open, where you can meet it on your own terms instead of its.

Fog rolls thick down the crooked lane behind the guild district, pooling around lampposts already half drowned in mist, and somewhere within it a cat yowls once before the sound cuts off far too abruptly to be natural. You slow your pace, weapon half raised, listening for whatever silenced it so cleanly. A shape steps out from between two shuttered stalls, wearing a gentleman's coat over a face that shifts, tiger one moment, something almost human the next, as though it has not quite decided which mask suits tonight's meeting. Its hands fold backward at the wrist in a gesture of welcome that chills more than it charms. It offers pleasant words, honey smooth and entirely hollow. You answer with steel, and the fog around you seems, just slightly, to draw back to watch.
13: Fog rolls off the gutters in slow grey sheets, pooling around a crooked lamppost at the exact spot where the alley splits in two. One branch climbs toward the bell tower and its promise of open sky. The other slides down into shadow that seems, impossibly, to breathe on its own, and the streetlamp above it gutters weak and gold, refusing to fully die. You choose your footing carefully, boots finding the dry stones between puddles, and that is when the shape steps free of the mist. It is taller than any doorway has a right to allow, gloved hands folded, smile far too handsome for what waits behind it. A cloak drags soundless across the wet cobbles, leaving no trail, no ripple, nothing but silence in its wake. The Nightbringer has already chosen its branch tonight, patient as the fog itself, and it studies you the way a jeweler studies a stone before the first cut. Somewhere close a shutter bangs loose in the wind, again and again, like a second heartbeat keeping time with your own. Now it only needs you to choose wrong, to hesitate one beat too long at the fork. Steady your grip. The lamplight will not hold much longer, and neither will the quiet. Type your line clean and fast, because the Nightbringer has all the patience the long night can spare, and you do not.

Rain slicks the rooftops above the first branch of the old quarter, where loose tiles slide underfoot and gutters spill silver rain down into the dark street below. You climb because the ground way is blocked, because a courier's overturned cart has spilled crates across the only door, and because the fog down there is thick enough to hide a second footstep behind your own. Somewhere above, a shape crouches on a crooked chimney, watching with eyes like dying embers, waiting for you to look up before it moves at all. It drops without a sound, cloak snapping open like leather wings catching wind, and for one long heartbeat you see its whole shape against the fogged moon, elegant and wrong, before it lands soft as falling ash between you and the ladder home. Its smile is patient, courteous even, gloved hands folded as if this were a proper introduction rather than an ambush timed to the second. The Nightbringer never wastes a good entrance on prey too slow to appreciate it, and it clearly expects you to appreciate this one fully before the fight begins in earnest. Race the clock now, because the Nightbringer never falls twice from the same chimney, and the rain shows no sign of easing before this is settled one way or the other.

The lamplighter never reached this corner tonight. His ladder lies broken against the crooked wall, rungs snapped clean through, and the lamp above it gutters weak and dying, throwing long trembling shadows across the wet stone. In his place waits a tall shape in a fine coat, gloved hands folded with unsettling patience, the kind of stillness that only comes from something that has stopped needing breath entirely. It bows low when it notices you watching, mocking and courteous all at once, before its smile splits far too wide for any handsome face to hold steady. Somewhere behind it the fog thickens, swallowing the last dim light of the guttering lamp, and the whole alley seems to lean in closer to listen. The Nightbringer offers you the first move only because it already knows how the second one ends, and its confidence sits on it like a well tailored coat, unhurried, certain, patient as the crooked wall it leans against. A dropped glove lies forgotten near the broken ladder, the lamplighter's, and you wonder briefly where the man himself has gone. There is no time left to wonder further. The fog is closing, the lamp is dying, and the Nightbringer's bow is already straightening back into something far more dangerous. Type now, before the courtesy runs out entirely and the real fight begins.

Fog thickens at the fork where three narrow streets meet beneath a dead streetlamp, its glass cracked from some earlier night's trouble long before you ever arrived. A door creaks somewhere close in the dark, though no hand ever opens it, and the sound repeats twice more before it finally stops, leaving only the drip of rain from a broken gutter overhead. You steady your grip and step forward anyway, because the branch behind you is worse, choked with old rubble and a smell you would rather not identify. A pale shape detaches itself from between two shuttered market stalls, cloak dragging soundless through spilled rainwater, its boots never quite seeming to touch the ground beneath them. Its eyes catch the last thread of lamplight like a struck coin, bright and cold and entirely too aware. The Nightbringer studies you a long moment, unhurried, before it finally decides you are worth the trouble of a proper fight rather than a quick and quiet end in the dark. Somewhere close a shutter bangs once more, as if the street itself is applauding the decision. Grip your line and hold steady, because whatever patience the Nightbringer has shown so far is about to run out, and the fork behind you offers no better shelter than the one ahead.

A crooked signpost marks the branch in the road just past the tannery, one arm pointing toward the guild hall's warm lit windows, the other snapped clean off by some older storm and never repaired. Mist pools ankle deep here and swallows the sound of your own footsteps entirely, so that you cannot tell if you walk alone or if something keeps perfect pace just behind your shoulder. Something tall unfolds itself from the black mouth of a doorway, cloak whispering against old brick like a held breath finally released after a long and patient wait. The Nightbringer has been waiting here a long while, patient as stone, gloved hand rising in a slow and terrible welcome that somehow still manages to look courteous. You remember, too late, that the signpost's broken arm always seemed to point straight at this very doorway, as if someone long ago had tried to warn every traveler who passed and failed to make the warning stick. The mist thickens further, curling around your boots like fingers testing whether you intend to run. Type your line before that patience finally runs its course, because the Nightbringer's smile has already stopped pretending to be anything but hunger, and the broken signpost offers no direction now that matters half as much as speed.

Gutters overflow along the tenement row where the alley splits into two uneven halves, carrying dead leaves and a single dropped glove past your boots toward the drain, spinning slow in the black water. Somewhere above, a shutter bangs loose in the wind, again and again, like a heartbeat that will not settle no matter how long you wait for it to still. You press on toward the darker branch because it is the only one that leads home, past shuttered windows and a crooked chimney leaning further than any chimney should. A shape detaches from the fog there, taller than any door frame should allow, cloak pooling around gloved boots like spilled ink left to dry on old stone. Its smile is handsome and wrong all at once, courteous in a way that makes your skin go cold despite the mild night air. The Nightbringer only ever waits this long for prey it considers worth the wait, and it studies you now with the patient attention of a collector eyeing a particularly fine addition. The overflowing gutter roars on behind you, indifferent to everything unfolding at the fork. Grip your line, because whatever courtesy the Nightbringer offers next will not last, and the darker branch has already proven itself the wrong choice to have made alone.

The streetlamp gutters twice and dies at the exact spot where the alley forks, plunging the whole crooked lane into a dark thick enough to taste, heavy with old rain and colder stone. Somewhere close a cloak drags slow across wet cobbles, and you count the seconds between each footstep, though you know better than to trust the count when nothing about this street behaves as it should. A pale gloved hand beckons once from the black doorway ahead, patient and unhurried, certain you will come closer on your own without needing to be asked twice. The Nightbringer never needs to hurry when its prey is already boxed between two dead ends, one choked with rubble, the other guarded by its own smiling patience. It only smiles wider the longer you hesitate, waiting for the exact moment you decide to run, as if the running itself were half the entertainment it came here for tonight. Somewhere behind the dead lamp, water drips steady from a broken gutter, marking time neither of you seem willing to acknowledge openly. Whatever choice you make now, make it fast. The Nightbringer's patience is a well it has never once run dry, but yours is considerably shallower, and the fork offers no third branch to escape down.

Sewer grates rattle loose underfoot where the tunnel branches beneath the old tenements, one way leading toward the docks and open air, the other toward a silence that presses on your ears like water pushing in from every side at once. A cold wind rises from that second branch, carrying the smell of old blood and older, colder stone that no amount of rain has ever managed to wash clean. Something with too many teeth for any human smile steps from the dark fork ahead, gloved hands spread in mock welcome, cloak dragging through standing water without a single ripple, as if the tunnel itself refuses to acknowledge its passing. The Nightbringer speaks softly here, courteous as ever, and the words land colder than the water already lapping at your ankles. You check the branch behind you once, quickly, and find it just as choked with shadow as the one ahead, offering no comfort and no easy retreat. The grates rattle again somewhere close, though nothing visible moves to explain the sound, and the silence that follows feels far too deliberate to be accidental. The Nightbringer waits, patient as the tunnel itself, gloved hand still extended in its mockery of welcome. Answer speed with speed now, because down here the fog cannot follow to warn you, and the only clock left running is the one in your own chest.

Rooftop tiles clatter loose where the chimneys crowd close together above the fork in the street below, and you climb because the ground route is already lost to fog too thick to see through, cold and rising fast enough to swallow the whole lane in minutes. A shape crouches against that fog at the roofline, cloak pooling like spilled ink around gloved, folded hands that have clearly done this before. It tips its head slowly, listening for your breath the way a cat listens for a mouse behind a wall, patient enough to wait out an entire winter if the hunt demanded it. The Nightbringer has all the patience the long night can offer, unhurried, certain, smiling faintly at the sound of your heartbeat carrying clear across the narrow gap between chimneys. You do not have that luxury, and the clock behind your eyes already knows it, ticking louder with every tile that shifts loose beneath your boots. Below, the fog continues to thicken, swallowing the last visible lamp along the street, leaving the rooftop as the only lit ground left in the whole crooked quarter. Steady yourself against the chimney's crumbling brick, and do not look down. The Nightbringer is close enough now to smell the rain on your coat, and it has stopped pretending patience is anything but a courtesy it can withdraw at will.

The bell tower creaks above a crooked branch of alleyway where the cobbles turn slick with old standing rain, and somewhere close a courier's dropped satchel lies split open, papers scattering white against the dark stone like fallen snow that refuses to melt. You crouch to gather what you can, and that is when the fog beyond it moves on its own, curling in a way no ordinary wind explains. A tall figure steps free of it, smiling like it already knows exactly how this ends, gloved hand extended in an offer of help that is no offer at all, courteous in the exact way a trap is courteous right up until it closes. The Nightbringer's courtesy is a blade worn smooth from long use, hidden until the last possible moment when it finally matters. The bell above tolls once, unprompted, as if the tower itself has grown weary of watching this particular scene repeat. You leave the scattered papers where they lie and grip your line instead, because whatever the courier once carried matters far less now than what stands between you and the way home. Prove tonight, at least, that its confidence is misplaced. The fog has already closed the gap behind you, and the bell tower offers no shelter, only a witness to whatever happens next in its shadow.

Lamplight gutters weakly along the narrow fork where the guild district splits from the slums below, one branch bright with candlelit windows and low laughter drifting faint through shuttered glass, the other black as a shut and rusted door that has not opened in years. Something moves in that darker half, unhurried, patient, gloved fingers trailing slow along the damp brick as it walks. The Nightbringer only ever chooses the darker branch of any road, and tonight, so did you, though you told yourself it was the shorter way home and not simple exhaustion making the choice for you. Its smile widens the moment it realizes you understand the trap now, far too late to matter, the candlelit windows already well out of reach behind you.
