A shutter somewhere in the bright branch bangs once, distant and unconcerned, as if that whole half of the street has no idea what unfolds so close by in the dark. You grip your line tighter, aware now that understanding alone will not save you here, not against something this patient and this pleased with itself. Type quickly. The Nightbringer has already closed half the distance while you were busy noticing the difference between the two branches, and it does not intend to let you notice much else before this ends.

Mist condensing thick on the cobbles turns the whole crooked street into a dim and trembling mirror of lamplight, doubling every shadow twice over until you can no longer tell which belongs to you. Where the road splits near the old well, a pale shape waits with its back against a shuttered window, cloak hanging unnaturally still despite the wind pulling at every loose shutter nearby. Its eyes open slow, catching the last dying streetlamp like caught flame, and for a moment the whole alley seems to hold its breath along with you, the mist itself pausing mid curl. The Nightbringer studies you the way a jeweler studies a stone, weighing worth before the first strike, unhurried despite the storm clearly gathering overhead. You glance once at the old well, its cracked stones slick with the same condensing mist, and find no comfort there either, only more reflected lamplight bending strange across wet rock. The shape at the window has not moved again, though its smile has widened by some small and deliberate degree. Type before it blinks again and decides you are worth taking. The mirrored street offers no second path home, only the one fork, and the Nightbringer clearly intends to be the one who decides how you cross it.

A dropped coin rolls to a stop at the fork in the alley, spinning once on its edge before settling heads up in a puddle of old rain that has not yet stopped falling from a gutter somewhere above. Fog swallows the sound of the second footstep that was never yours to begin with, close behind and softer than it should be for something that size to manage so quietly. A tall shape leans out slow from behind a rain barrel, smile widening like a wound finally allowed to open after being held shut too long. Gloved fingers curl once in invitation, courteous as ever, patient as the fog itself pooling thick around the barrel's rotted staves. The Nightbringer collects far more than coin on nights like this, and it has already decided what it wants from you, weighing the decision with the same idle interest it might give any dropped trinket in the street. You leave the coin where it lies, because bending to retrieve it now would mean turning your back on the one thing in this alley that matters. Grip your line instead, and hold it steady. Whatever game the coin was meant to start, the Nightbringer has already moved past it, closing the last of the distance while your attention was elsewhere.

The tenement stairs branch into two crooked landings partway up, one lit by a single guttering candle in a cracked window, the other pitch dark and utterly silent despite the storm still hammering the shutters outside. Something in that dark landing exhales slowly, cold enough that your breath fogs the nearest broken pane before you even reach the turn in the stairs. A gloved hand rests on the broken railing there, patient, unhurried, waiting for you to decide which landing is the lesser risk between two choices that both feel equally wrong. The Nightbringer already knows which one you will choose, because it always does, and its patience carries the particular weight of something utterly certain of the outcome. The candle in the lit landing gutters once, threatening to go out entirely, as if even the flame senses which way this is truly headed. You test the railing with one hand and find it colder than the storm outside should allow, colder than wood left out in rain has any right to be. Climb fast, or let it climb first and meet you at the top instead. Either way the stairs end at the same place, and the Nightbringer has never once minded waiting at the top for prey to arrive on its own.

Rain drums hard on tin roofs where the alley forks past a boarded and abandoned tavern, gutters roaring with runoff toward the black mouth of a sewer grate below, loud enough to swallow most other sound in the street. A tall shape steps out into the downpour entirely unbothered, cloak dry despite the storm soaking everything else in sight, not a single drop clinging to the fabric. Its smile is far too handsome for the teeth waiting behind it, courteous even as it studies you head to boot like a merchant weighing goods before naming a price neither of you will actually get to negotiate. The Nightbringer offers shelter from the rain in a voice smooth as poured wax, patient and warm and utterly false in every syllable. You glance once at the tavern's boarded windows, hoping for some gap wide enough to slip through, and find none, only warped wood nailed tight years ago against exactly this kind of night. The rain shows no sign of slowing, and neither does the smile waiting patiently just a few steps closer than before. Type quickly, because its shelter always costs more than the weather ever would, and the tavern behind you offers no door that still opens for the living.

A church bell tolls once as the road forks past the crumbling chapel wall, and the sound dies fast, swallowed whole by fog thick enough to hide a second footstep behind your own without any real effort. Something pale crouches low on the chapel's broken wall, cloak pooling around its boots like spilled ink left to dry undisturbed on ancient, crumbling stone. It rises slow, joints unhurried, smiling with a courtesy that chills far more than the wind ever could on its own, the kind of smile that has clearly practiced patience for longer than the chapel itself has stood. The Nightbringer has waited here through worse storms than this one, patient as the stone beneath it, untroubled by rain or wind or the tolling bell above. You glance once at the chapel's broken door, half hoping for some sanctuary the old stone might still offer, and find only more darkness waiting past the threshold, no different from the fog already pressing close on every side. The bell does not toll again, as if it too has decided this is not its business to interrupt further. Fight now, before its patience finally runs its course and the courtesy drops away entirely, because whatever waited quietly on that broken wall has clearly finished waiting for tonight.

The gutter runs black with old standing rain where the alley splits behind the tanner's shuttered shop, carrying a single lost ribbon downstream toward the drain, spinning slow in water that smells faintly of old leather and older rot. A tall shape steps from the fork's darker branch, smile handsome, eyes hollow and patient as a held breath waiting to be released after a very long wait. It bows low, mocking, gloved hand extended in an offer that is really no offer at all, the gesture so practiced it might have been rehearsed across a hundred identical forks on a hundred identical nights. The Nightbringer always offers the first move to prey it considers worth the courtesy, and tonight, whether you wanted it or not, that prey is you standing at this exact fork. The ribbon catches briefly on a loose stone before the current frees it again, carrying it on toward the drain and whatever lies beneath the street. You do not look away from the bow long enough to watch it disappear, because looking away now would be exactly the mistake this courtesy is designed to invite. Take the offer as a warning instead of a kindness, and answer its bow with speed of your own, because the tanner's shop offers no door that opens from this side of the fork tonight.

Fog rolls thick through the fork where the cobbler's row meets a dead end lit by one dying, guttering lamp, and the smell of old leather lingers even here where nothing living remains open past dark, the shuttered shopfronts long since gone quiet for the night. A shape detaches from the brick wall itself, cloak dragging soundless over wet stone toward the light, as if drawn there by the last warmth it offers. Its smile is quiet, its patience quieter still, the kind of stillness that has had centuries to perfect itself into something almost peaceful, right up until the moment it isn't. The Nightbringer has never once needed to raise its voice to be terrifying, and it does not start tonight, letting the silence between you do most of the work instead. You check the dead end behind the lamp once more, uselessly, already knowing there is no other way through this fork, no side alley, no back door left unlocked by some careless cobbler. The lamp gutters lower still, as if agreeing with your assessment of the situation. Type your line before that patience finally runs out and the quiet breaks into something far worse than silence. The cobbler's row will still be standing come morning; whether you are standing with it depends entirely on the next few seconds.

The crooked lane splits at an old covered well, one branch leading toward the guild hall's warm windows and the murmur of a late crowd still awake inside, the other toward a silence that presses close and cold like a hand over the mouth. Mist pools thick around the well's cracked stones, rising slow like breath from something buried far beneath that has only just begun to stir. A tall figure rises from beside the well, gloved hands folded politely, waiting with the patience of something that has never once needed to hurry toward anything it truly wanted. The Nightbringer offers you first choice of branch tonight, courteous as ever, though both branches lead back to the very same waiting smile in the end, a fact it seems entirely content to let you discover on your own. You lean once toward the well, half convinced the stirring beneath it deserves attention too, before deciding the shape standing beside it is by far the more immediate concern. The guild hall's distant warmth flickers faint through fogged glass, tempting and entirely too far to reach without passing this fork first. Choose speed over hesitation, always, because the Nightbringer has already made its choice clear, and the well's mist shows no sign of settling before this is decided one way or the other.

The lamplight flickers twice and finally dies at the branch where the market street narrows down to a crooked throat of old stone, the abandoned stalls to either side offering nothing but empty crates and torn awnings flapping loose. Something waits just past that dark, patient as a held breath, close enough now that you can hear the faint whisper of a cloak settling against the wall beside you. A gloved hand beckons once from deep in the shadow, unhurried, certain you will take one more step regardless of better judgment, the same certainty a well set trap carries long before the prey ever notices the wire. The Nightbringer only ever needs that single extra step, no more, and it has learned exactly how to make prey offer it freely, without ever once raising a hand of its own to force the matter. You test the narrowed street with one careful boot, and the stone underfoot feels no different from any other night, ordinary and cold and entirely unhelpful in warning you of anything at all. The torn awnings flap once more in a wind you cannot otherwise feel, as if the street itself is holding its breath alongside you both. Refuse the step it wants. The market stalls offer no better shelter than the throat of stone ahead, but at least here you choose the ground yourself, rather than letting the Nightbringer's patient hand choose it for you.

The gutter overflows at the fork behind the guild hall, carrying a dropped glove past your boots toward the black mouth of the drain below, spinning once before it vanishes into the dark water rushing beneath the street. A shape unfolds from the shadow beside the drainpipe, taller than it has any right to be, smile patient, eyes catching the last lamp like a struck match flaring bright before it gutters. It watches you a long moment before moving, unhurried, certain of its own patience the way only something ancient can be, entirely untroubled by the overflowing gutter soaking its boots without so much as a ripple disturbing its cloak. The Nightbringer has stood in worse fog than this waiting for lesser prey to make the first mistake, and it will happily wait a while longer if tonight requires it. The guild hall's back wall looms close behind you, its windows dark at this late hour, offering no help and no door left unlocked for a stranger fleeing down this particular fork. The glove disappears fully into the drain with a small, final splash, swallowed the way the whole alley seems intent on swallowing everything else tonight. Fight now, and refuse to be the one who makes it tonight. The Nightbringer's patience is a well without a bottom, but the fork behind the guild hall offers no second chance once its smile finally decides to move.

The fog over Waypoint has a texture to it tonight, thick and gray-gold under the guild lamps, and the golem waits at the center of the crooked crossing like it has always stood there, like the street grew up around it instead of the other way around. Rain drips steady off its shoulders, pooling in the seams of its clay, and you can hear the faint grinding of stone against stone even when it is not moving, a low internal shifting that never fully stops. You circle once, boots careful on wet cobblestones slick with old moss, checking the ground for the loose stone you noticed on your way in. The golem tracks you with dull ember eyes, patient in a way that unsettles you more than teeth would, and you roll initiative in your head before you ever raise your weapon, counting the beats until it finally decides to move. When it does move, it commits fully, both fists rising together like a drawbridge slamming shut, and you throw yourself sideways across the slick stone. The fist crashes into the spot where you stood a heartbeat earlier, cracking the cobblestone deep enough to swallow a boot heel. Dust and grit spray up in a gray fan, and through it you finally spot the crack running the length of its chest, hairline thin but glowing faintly at the edges, and you know exactly where the fight is actually going to be won.

Somebody sealed this golem's chest with a copper plate centuries ago, back when the guild that built it still kept careful records of such things, ledgers and pamphlets nobody living has read in a hundred years. The rain has worn that plate thin as old parchment, and tonight, under the scattered lamplight of the waypoint, you can just make out its seam glowing faint gold beneath layers of packed clay and river grit. The golem does not seem to know its own weakness, or does not care, swinging with the same patient certainty it has probably used for centuries against anyone foolish enough to block its path. Its first fist crashes wide, missing you by a hand's width and cracking a lamppost instead, glass raining down in bright little pieces that scatter across the wet stone like coins spilled from a torn purse. You use the chaos, the light, the half second before it resets its stance, to close the distance fast, feet slipping slightly on cobblestones slick with rain and broken glass. Your blade finds the copper seam dead center, and the whole fight seems to tip in that one motion, clay shuddering, mud sloughing free in long gray ribbons that vanish into the gutter running black beside you. The golem groans, low and grinding, more mortar settling than any real voice, and for the first time since you arrived at this waypoint, it takes a full step back instead of forward.

You hear the golem long before you see it, a slow grinding shuffle echoing off brick walls slick with fog and old rain, patient in the particular way that only something without lungs can afford to be. Waypoint's crooked lane narrows just ahead, gutters running black with river mud, and that is exactly where the creature rounds the corner into view, fists already curling closed at its sides. The first exchange goes badly for you, a fist grazing your shoulder hard enough to spin you half around and knock the breath clean out of your chest. You recover fast though, driving your blade toward the crack you spotted earlier along its ribs, feeling clay finally shudder and begin to give. Mud rains down onto cobblestones already slick with rain and old dust, and the golem staggers, grinding low, resetting its stance. Rain drums steady overhead as you circle, watching gray mud flake loose from the widening crack in long curls that drift toward the nearest gutter and disappear. One more good hit, you think, feet braced against slick stone, breath fogging in the cold night air, and the whole crooked wall of clay finally comes down for good. The golem does not seem to notice its own damage, resetting itself with the same patient certainty it started with, but you know precisely where the true weakness lives, and you aim everything you have left straight for it.

Fog rolls thick down Waypoint's crooked main street, swallowing the guild towers whole and leaving only scattered lamplight and the golem's hulking silhouette visible against the wet stone. It has been standing at this crossing for longer than anyone in the district can remember, dust settled thick across its shoulders like old snow that never properly melted even in the warmest months. You approach carefully, testing the cobblestones for loose or cracked sections, and the creature turns to track you with dull ember eyes the moment you step within range. It swings wide first, missing you by a hand's width and cracking the stone instead of your ribs, and you answer immediately with a strike of your own aimed low, catching the crack already spreading fast across its ribs and chest. Clay shivers loose in gray curls that drift down slow through the fog, catching what little lamplight survives the mist. The golem groans, grinding low, unbothered by the damage in any way you would recognize as pain, and resets its stance for another attempt at ending this fight in its favor. You have already spotted exactly where the next strike needs to land though, a hairline seam running straight through the center of the crack, and you press forward before it can fully recover, boots splashing through cold puddles that never quite drain from this crooked, waterlogged street.

The alley reeks of river mud and old smoke, the kind that settles permanently into stone and never really leaves no matter how much rain falls through the guild district. Waypoint sits at the heart of it, a crossing of two crooked streets where the golem has apparently chosen to make its stand tonight, patient and unmoving until you finally step close enough to matter. It moves all at once when you do, fists rising together like a drawbridge slamming fully shut, and you feint left on instinct, drawing a heavy fist that crashes into a nearby barrel instead of your ribs, splintering the wood into a dozen scattered pieces across the wet cobblestones. You use the half second of recovery that follows to close in with everything you have got, blade angled straight for the crack already spreading visibly across its chest. The golem groans, low and grinding, mud sloughing from its shoulder in gray curls that the steady rain immediately carries toward the nearest gutter and the dark water waiting below. It staggers, off balance for the first time since the fight began, and you press the advantage hard, unwilling to give it even a single second to properly reset. Somewhere above, a rooftop guild bell tolls the hour, slow and heavy, and the golem answers with one final grinding step that shakes loose mortar from a nearby wall before the whole crooked wall of clay finally starts to come apart for good.

A guild lantern gutters out just as the golem raises both fists overhead, clay groaning audibly under its own considerable weight in the thick fog of Waypoint's central crossing. You count the beat before impact and roll sideways fast across slick cobblestones, feeling the shockwave travel clean through your boots as clay meets stone hard enough to crack the street itself wide open beneath the blow. Dust drifts down slow from the impact site like a strange kind of snow, catching what little lamplight manages to survive the fog and mist. The golem turns, unbothered by its own miss, already resetting its stance for another attempt, but you have spotted something it apparently has not: a crack running fresh up its forearm, pale and glowing faintly beneath all that packed grime and river mud. You press the advantage immediately, before the mud has any real time to knit itself whole again the way it seems to want to. Your blade catches the fresh crack dead center, and the golem staggers back a full step, grinding low, more damaged now than at any point since this whole fight first began at the crooked waypoint crossing. Rain keeps falling steady through it all, indifferent to the outcome, pooling in the footprints the creature leaves scattered behind it across the wet stone.

Waypoint's sewers breathe cold, damp air up through an iron grate set directly into the crooked cobblestones, and the golem's shin catches hard on the bars as it turns to face you, grinding loud enough to echo off every brick wall in the narrow lane. You see the crack along its leg spreading visibly wider with each motion the creature makes, a hairline fault deepening fast. Your pulse climbs steadily in your throat as you press the advantage, blade aimed straight and true for the damaged shin before the mud has any real chance to knit itself whole again in the cold night air. The golem staggers hard, off balance for just a moment, dust and grit sifting loose from every joint like flour spilled from some ancient torn sack left forgotten in a guild cellar. You use that single moment before the fight can possibly tip back in its favor, closing the last few feet of distance while it struggles to find solid footing on the wet stone. The blade connects clean against the widening crack, and clay finally gives way in a shower of gray dust that drifts slow through the scattered lamplight surrounding you both. Somewhere below, sewer water rushes on indifferent to the fight happening just above it, cold and constant, carrying away whatever mud and debris the golem sheds with every staggering step it takes across the crooked street.

You find the golem waiting at the mouth of Waypoint's crossing the way an old, unpaid debt waits, patient and entirely unhurried, standing motionless in a puddle formed from its own dripping clay. Fog hangs thick and heavy between the leaning, crooked towers overhead, and the scattered guild lamps burn low and orange, throwing long shadows across cobblestones slicked black with rain. You circle once, testing the wet ground carefully for any loose or cracked stone, and the golem turns to follow you, slow as a millstone finally finding its groove after sitting idle. Its chest already carries an old crack, hairline thin and pale, and you find yourself wondering exactly how many fights across how many decades it survived in order to earn a scar like that one. When it finally swings, the fist passes close enough to fog your collar with displaced, cold air, and you answer immediately with a strike aimed straight and true for that old seam, praying quietly that the ancient clay gives before your own arm does under the strain. It connects. Clay shudders, cracks further, and a thin curl of pale light escapes briefly into the fog before the whole wound seals back over, patient as ever, waiting for you to try again in this long crooked street.

The rain has not let up once since you first reached the crooked lane leading toward Waypoint, turning every gutter along the way into its own small black river rushing steadily toward the sewer grates below. The golem waits beneath a broken, weathered guild sign at the crossing, patient as packed clay always is, until you finally step close enough within the fog to properly matter to it. It moves all at once the instant you do, fists rising together like a drawbridge slamming suddenly shut, and you slip beneath the first heavy swing purely on instinct, boots skidding slightly across the slick and uneven stone. The crack in its chest looks wider now than it did when you first spotted it from a distance, and you drive your blade straight home before that gap has any real chance to close back over, mud spraying wide across the wet cobblestones between you both. The golem staggers, grinding low, and for one strange moment you can see straight through the widening crack to something pale and faintly glowing deep beneath all that ancient packed clay. You do not particularly want to know what that glow is actually made of, not tonight, not with the fight still very much undecided. Instead you press forward hard, driving everything you have left toward that exposed seam before the fog-soaked creature can properly reset itself for another slow, heavy swing of its own.

Every guild in this crooked, waterlogged district has its own story about the golem that guards the waypoint crossing, and none of those stories end particularly well for whoever swings first without some kind of actual plan already firmly in mind. Tonight, fists up and breath fogging steadily in the cold night air, you are about to add your own version to that long and growing pile of local stories. The golem meets your very first strike with a fist that nearly takes your head clean off, passing close enough that you feel the displaced wind of it brush hard against your cheek. You answer low and fast, driving your blade in beneath its guard toward the crack you had already spotted earlier along its ribs, and feel the ancient clay finally shudder hard under the impact, mud raining down heavily onto the wet cobblestones scattered between you both. It staggers, off balance for the first real time since this whole fight began at the crooked crossing, and you press the sudden advantage without any hesitation, unwilling to give the creature even a single second to properly steady itself again. Rain keeps falling steady through the fog overhead, indifferent as always to the outcome, while somewhere above a rooftop guild bell finally tolls the hour, slow and deep, almost like it already somehow knows exactly how this particular fight is going to end tonight.

Clay dust hangs thick in the scattered lamplight like a strange kind of snow that simply forgot how to properly fall, drifting slow past crooked rooftops and shuttered windows all along Waypoint's central crossing. The golem lumbers forward steadily through all of it, indifferent to the cold, indifferent to you, fists rising with that same patient certainty it has apparently carried since this whole fight first began near the old guild hall. Your blade finally finds the crack running the full length up its side, and it staggers hard, grinding loud, off balance for what feels like the first genuine time all night. You do not let up even slightly, pressing the sudden advantage while dust still falls steadily all around you both, aiming every single following strike straight at the widening gap before the ancient clay has any real chance to close it back over on its own. Somewhere behind the thick fog a bell tolls the hour, slow and heavy, and the golem answers with one final grinding step that shakes loose mortar from a nearby crooked wall. You watch its whole chest crack wider with every motion it makes now, a fault line spreading fast like frost across old glass in the cold night air, and you know without a doubt that the fight is finally, mercifully, drawing to its close.

A guild banner, soaked entirely through and heavy with days of steady rain, slaps hard against the golem's shoulder as it turns to face you, and for one strange and lingering moment you see straight through the crack in its chest to something pale and faintly glowing deep beneath all that ancient, packed clay. You do not particularly want to wait around and ask what that light is actually made of, not with the creature already resetting its stance. Instead you close the remaining distance fast while the heavy banner still hangs tangled loosely across its arm, slowing its next intended swing just enough to genuinely matter in the fight. Your blade finds the exposed crack dead center, and the golem lets out a low, grinding groan, more settling mortar than any real voice, as pale light spills briefly out into the surrounding fog before the ancient clay closes back fully over it once more. Rain keeps falling steady through the whole exchange, indifferent as ever to how this particular fight ends, pooling in the deep footprints the creature leaves scattered behind it across the wet and crooked stone. You press forward one final time, unwilling to let the golem properly reset itself again, driving everything you have left straight toward that same widening seam before it can seal shut for good in the cold, foggy night air surrounding you both.

Waypoint's crooked towers lean in unusually close overhead tonight, blotting out what little pale moonlight manages to actually break through the thick, rolling clouds above the district. The golem's eyes, twin points of dull, steady ember, track your every careful step across the wet stone, patient in a way that genuinely makes your skin crawl more than any snarl or growl ever really could. You breathe out slow, roll your shoulders once to loosen them, and close the last few remaining feet between you both before it can properly commit to another heavy swing. The first strike lands solid against its ribs, and the crack already there widens further with a sound like thick ice finally giving way on a frozen river somewhere far from this crooked, fog-bound street. Pale light flickers faint beneath the surface of the widening crack, visible now even through the packed grime and river mud, right where the ancient clay has finally begun to properly fail after however many centuries this thing has stood guard here. You press the sudden advantage hard, driving your blade back toward the same weakening seam before the golem can fully reset its stance, mud sloughing away in long gray ribbons that the steady rain immediately carries off toward the nearest waiting gutter.

The golem drags one heavy leg through standing water pooled at the mouth of the alley, ancient clay sloughing off in long gray ribbons that dissolve slowly in the current running steadily toward the nearest sewer grate. It is losing itself a small piece at a time with every step, and you fully intend to help that whole process along with the sharp point of your blade before it manages to close the remaining distance first. Lamplight flickers unsteadily overhead as the creature finally lifts its heavy head, dull ember eyes fixing squarely on you through the drifting fog. You circle wide across the slick stone, watching the crack along its damaged shin catch what light there is, gold and uneven, and time your next careful approach to land right as it shifts its considerable weight fully onto that same weakened leg. The strike connects hard, and the golem staggers, grinding loud enough to echo off the crooked buildings lining the narrow street. Mud rains down in heavy curls, and for a moment the whole fight seems to hang on that single narrow opening you just created. You press forward without hesitation, unwilling to let the ancient creature find its footing again, driving your blade back toward the same widening crack before the standing water can wash away any trace of the damage you have already done tonight.

The crack in the golem's broad chest widens slowly with a low sound like an old house finally settling deep into winter ground, patient and final all at once. Rain drums steady on the cobblestones as you circle it carefully, watching gray mud flake loose in long curls that drift toward the nearest gutter and vanish completely into the dark water running below the crooked street. One more good hit, you think to yourself, and the whole ancient wall of clay finally comes down for good tonight. The golem does not seem to notice its own mounting damage the way any living thing would, resetting its stance with that same patient certainty it apparently started this entire fight with, but you have fought enough of these creatures by now to know exactly where the true weakness genuinely lives. You aim absolutely everything you have left straight for that widening seam, feet braced hard against the slick and uneven stone, breath fogging steadily in front of you in the cold night air. The blade connects clean, and clay finally gives way in a heavy shower of gray dust that drifts slow through what scattered lamplight survives the thick fog. The golem lets out one final low, grinding groan, more settling stone than any real voice, as it staggers back a full step, more damaged now than at any point since this whole fight first began near the crooked waypoint crossing.

Somebody carved a strange symbol into the golem's broad back many centuries ago, faint copper still glowing dimly beneath layers of thick river mud and accumulated grime, and you only catch a brief glimpse of it as the creature spins slow through the drifting fog. Dust sifts steadily from every joint, heavy and entirely unhurried, marking exactly where you fully intend to strike next. The first exchange goes rather poorly for you, a heavy fist grazing your shoulder hard enough to spin you half around and knock the wind clean from your chest. You recover fast though, driving your blade back toward the crack along its chest, and feel the ancient clay finally shudder and properly give, mud raining down heavily onto cobblestones already slick with steady rain and old accumulated dust. The golem staggers, grinding low, and you press the sudden advantage without hesitation, unwilling to let the creature find its footing again on the wet and crooked stone. Somewhere above, a rooftop guild bell finally tolls the hour, slow and deep across the fog, almost as if marking the actual moment this long fight finally starts to properly turn in your favor instead of the golem's, mud and dust scattering across the ground between you both.

A crack in the old cobblestones marks the exact spot where the golem last put its heavy fist down in genuine anger, deep enough to easily catch a careless boot heel if you are not paying close attention. You skirt carefully around it while the creature resets its stance for another slow, heavy swing, thick fog curling between the crooked towers overhead and muffling every sound of your own careful breathing. You time your next approach to match its notably slowest point of recovery, closing the remaining distance fast while it is still grinding steadily back into proper position. The strike lands clean against the crack already spreading across its chest, and the golem groans low, mud sloughing free in long gray curls that vanish quietly into the rain-slick gutter running just beside you. The fight has gone on long enough now that your arms genuinely ache with the effort, but you press forward anyway, unwilling to let this ancient creature properly recover after everything it has already cost you tonight. Rain keeps falling steady through it all, indifferent as ever to how things eventually turn out, pooling in the deep footprints the golem leaves scattered behind it across the crooked, waterlogged street stretching out toward the waypoint crossing.

The golem's footsteps shake loose dust from centuries-old shutters overhead, and somewhere nearby a stray cat yowls once sharply and bolts hard for the safety of the rooftops, vanishing completely into the thick fog without ever looking back. You do not have that particular option available tonight, not with the waypoint crossing blocked entirely behind this slow, grinding thing. You square your stance carefully, feet braced firm on the wet and slick cobblestones, and wait patiently for the first heavy swing to fully commit before you answer it with absolutely everything you have got left. The blade finds the crack along its ribs dead center, and ancient clay gives way in a heavy shower of gray dust that drifts slow through the scattered lamplight stretching between you both. The golem staggers back hard, grinding low, more damaged now than at any single point since this whole fight first began near the crooked guild hall. You press the sudden advantage without any hesitation at all, driving your blade back toward that same widening crack before the creature can possibly find its footing again on the rain-slicked stone. Somewhere above, the rooftop guild bell tolls once more, slow and heavy, marking another hour passed in this long and grinding fight at the waypoint.

Fog swallows the tops of every guild tower along the crooked street, leaving only scattered lamplight and the golem's hulking silhouette visible below against the wet and uneven stone. It swings wide first, missing you by only a hand's width and cracking the old cobblestones instead of your ribs, and you answer immediately with a strike of your own aimed low and true. The blade catches the crack already spreading fast across its ribs and broad chest, and ancient clay shivers loose in gray curls that drift down slow through the thick, rolling fog. The golem groans, grinding low, resetting its heavy stance for another attempt at ending this whole fight in its own favor, but you have already spotted exactly where the very next strike genuinely needs to land in order to finish this properly tonight. You press forward without any hesitation, boots splashing through cold puddles that never seem to fully drain from this crooked, waterlogged street stretching out toward the waypoint. The final strike connects clean against the widening seam, and the golem staggers back hard, grinding loud enough to echo off every crooked building lining the narrow alley, mud and dust scattering wide across the wet stone between you as the ancient creature finally begins to properly come apart.

The alley narrows sharply just past the old, weathered guild hall, gutters running black with steady rain and centuries of accumulated grime, and that is precisely where the golem has apparently chosen to make its final stand tonight. Clay fists the size of full barrels crack hard through the cold air as it swings, missing you by mere inches and splintering a hanging wooden sign clean into kindling. You use the sudden wreckage as cover, sliding fast along the wet wall toward its exposed flank while it hauls its heavy arm free from a shattered awning overhead. The crack along its ribs has widened considerably since you first spotted it earlier tonight, a fault line spreading now like frost across old windowpane glass, and you know without any doubt that this fight will not last very much longer either way it goes. One more good strike, you think, bracing your feet hard against the slick and uneven stone, and the whole ancient wall of clay finally comes down for good right here at the crooked waypoint crossing. The golem swings once more, heavy and slow, and you slip past its guard entirely, driving your blade home straight into the widening seam. Clay shudders, cracks fully open, and the creature finally staggers back hard, grinding low, more damaged now than at any point since this whole long fight first began.

Rain turns the crooked alley into a strange black mirror, and the golem's reflection arrives a full half second before the actual creature itself, fists raised high, shoulders grinding low against the thick, rolling fog. You roll the dice in your head before you ever roll them for real, feet braced firm, breath held tight and steady in your chest. The first heavy swing goes wide, cracking a nearby lamppost instead of your ribs, and broken glass rains down in bright little pieces that scatter across the wet cobblestones like coins spilled from some torn purse. You use the sudden chaos, the noise, the flash of unexpected light, to slip past its guard entirely and drive your weapon straight into the widening crack along its exposed side. The golem staggers back hard, grinding low, mud sloughing away in long gray ribbons that the steady rain immediately carries off toward the nearest waiting gutter. You press the sudden advantage without hesitation, unwilling to let the ancient creature properly recover after everything this long fight has already cost you tonight. Somewhere above, a rooftop guild bell finally tolls the hour once more, slow and deep across the fog, as the whole crooked wall of clay begins at last to properly come apart for good.

The golem's clay shoulder catches on a low awning as it turns through the fog, tearing the canvas loose in one long ragged strip that flutters slow into a nearby puddle. Rain pours straight through the gap onto its heavy head, and mud runs like tears down a face that was never really meant to have one at all, dripping steady onto the crooked cobblestones below. You use the moment of distraction to close in fast, boots splashing hard through cold standing water, and land a solid strike against the crack already spreading wide across its broad chest. The golem staggers for what feels like the first real time all fight, grinding low and heavy, and you press the sudden advantage immediately, unwilling to give it even a single second to properly steady itself again on the wet stone. Somewhere above, a rooftop guild bell finally tolls the hour, slow and deep across the drifting fog, and the golem answers with one last grinding step that shakes loose mortar from a nearby crooked wall. You watch the crack widen further with a sound like old ice giving way on some frozen river far from this waterlogged street, and you know without any doubt that the ancient creature is finally, mercifully, starting to come apart for good tonight at the waypoint crossing.

The canal district after midnight belongs to fog and to whatever the fog decides to carry. You have walked this stretch of the causeway twice already tonight, boots echoing off boards that remember every crossing anyone has ever made across them, and both times the far bank stayed stubbornly out of sight behind white. The third time you do not walk. You wait, weapon steady, and let the thing that has been pacing you finally close the distance on its own terms. It comes up out of the steam near the old mooring post, coat dragging, chains trailing from sleeves that were never sewn to hold anything so heavy. Twenty years it has haunted this exact stretch of water, ever since the flood took the last real ferry downstream and left the post standing over nothing but ropes and rust. It does not remember the flood specifically. It remembers only the shape of waiting, the patience of something that outlived its own reasons for staying. You do not give it the satisfaction of another slow crossing. You step forward first, weapon raised, and the fog around you both seems to hold its breath the way it always does right before the first honest hit lands somewhere it matters. The revenant answers in kind, chains rattling loose against the frozen boards, hollow face turning to track your approach with the terrible calm of something that has already died once and found the whole process entirely unremarkable the second time it happened.

Sixteen floors of a leaning tower rise above the street, and every window in it has been dark for years except the one at the very top, where a single candle burns steady behind glass nobody has cleaned since the fire that hollowed the place out. You have heard the stories about that candle from half the guild district, how it never gutters, how the wax never finishes melting, how something climbs those rotted stairs each night just to relight what should have gone out decades ago. Tonight you climb those same stairs yourself, boots finding purchase on wood soft enough to worry you with every step. The dust on the ninth landing has not been disturbed in a decade, except for one clear drag mark leading toward the banister, the kind a coat makes when its wearer no longer bothers lifting its own hem. You find the revenant there waiting, exactly where the mark ends, hollow face already turned toward the stairwell as though it heard you from the first step. It does not speak, and it does not lunge immediately either. It simply reaches for the banister the way it must have the night everything went wrong, fingers passing through wood that remembers the weight of a body it once held up. You do not let that memory slow your hand. You raise your weapon, plant your feet against the rotted boards, and meet it there on the landing where the candlelight barely reaches, letting the dice decide who finishes the climb.

The old canal breathes steam even on the coldest nights, warm water meeting frozen air in ribbons that hide the towpath completely from anyone foolish enough to walk it after dark. You are exactly that foolish tonight, tracking a hunt that has led you past three shuttered chandleries and one drowned mooring post already, following a trail of dragged chain marks that never quite touch the ground the way real chains would. The steam thickens as you near the old drawbridge, and somewhere inside that white the revenant is already moving, patient as rot working slowly through old timber, tracking the sound of your boots with a certainty that has nothing left to prove and all the time in the world to prove it anyway. You count your breaths in the dark, three, two, one, the way you always do before stepping into ground you cannot fully see. It meets you at the towpath's narrowest point, where the water runs closest to the boards and the fog is thickest of all, coat dripping steam, ribs showing pale where the buttons gape open across a chest that gave up breathing decades ago. For one long moment neither of you moves, the whole canal holding its breath around you. Then it lunges, chains dragging soundless across wet stone, and you meet the attack exactly where you stand, weapon first, because retreating into deeper fog was never really an option worth taking.

Behind the shuttered bakery on the corner, a chain rattles once, then twice, then falls utterly silent in a way that somehow carries louder than the sound before it. You already know what that silence means, having heard it three times now this month alone, always in this same stretch of the guild district where the fever took half the bakers and nobody ever quite finished mourning them properly. You round the corner slow, weapon ready, and find the alley empty except for fog pooling thick around a drain grate that has not been cleared in years. The revenant does not announce itself the way lesser hauntings might. It simply arrives, the patience of twenty years standing frozen at some forgotten crossroads compressed into a single unhurried step that somehow covers half the alley's length at once. Its coat is buttoned wrong from collar to hem, one hole skipped entirely, the mark of hands that were shaking too hard on some final morning to count properly. You do not have time to feel sorry for whatever it used to be before the fight demands your full attention. It studies you the way a bell studies an hour it has not rung in decades, patient, unbothered, certain that hesitation is the only real threat left in this narrow little corner of the city. You raise your weapon first, and refuse to give it that particular satisfaction tonight.

A jar sealed with a single tear of blue wax sits on a windowsill above the alley, closed forever against grief nobody living remembers collecting in the first place. The revenant favors this particular corner most nights, drifting toward the jar as though it still remembers crying once, long before the coat it wears now stopped needing warmth or company or anything else the living still ask each other for. You have watched it pause here twice before without striking, curious more than cautious, wanting to understand what exactly draws it back to this one window out of every window in the whole fogbound street. Tonight you finally understand. The jar was its own, sealed by hands that are not attached to the coat anymore, grief bottled up and left behind the way everything else about it got left behind eventually. The moment breaks the way moments always break around it, sentiment giving way to the older, colder patience of something that has nothing left to lose by waiting one more year, or one more century. Chains it never needed drag across the cobblestones, and the fog thickens around your boots like it wants to hold you exactly where you are standing. You do not let the sadness of the jar slow your hand. The dice favor whoever strikes first and steadiest, and tonight that has to be you, whatever the jar once held.

Steam pours off the storm grates near the old mill until the whole street blurs into a single gray smear against a darker sky above, and somewhere inside that haze something moves with the unhurried certainty of a thing that has already died once and found the second time entirely unremarkable. You have tracked it here from three streets over, following chain drags that never quite touch the ground the way real iron would. You back toward the mill wall, giving yourself something solid at your spine before the steam finally gives up whatever shape it has been hiding. The revenant emerges slow, coat dripping, ribs showing pale beneath buttons that gape open across a chest that stopped needing breath decades before either of you were born into this particular fight. It does not rush the reveal. It never rushes anything anymore. You meet it there against the mill wall, weapon raised, letting that solid stone be the only retreat you allow yourself tonight. Chains rattle loose against the cobblestones as it closes the last of the distance, patient as ever, cold rolling off it in a wave that fogs the air between you completely. The fight that follows is fast despite all that patience, settled in the end by whoever refuses to hesitate first, and tonight you have already decided that hesitation is a luxury this particular street cannot afford you.

The old ferryman's post has stood empty at the mooring for twenty years, ropes frayed down to threads, and no boat has tied up there since the flood carried the last real ferry downstream along with half the district's memory of what the canal used to look like before the water rose. Twin lanterns still swing from posts nobody bothers to light anymore, creaking against a wind you cannot actually feel yourself. Tonight something climbs out of the black water anyway, dragging behind it the shape of a coat it no longer needs to keep dry, water pooling black wherever its footsteps land on boards that have not held real weight in decades. You watch from the causeway, weapon ready, breath held tight against a cold that has nothing to do with the season or the hour or anything the calendar could actually explain to you. It crosses the mooring slowly, the way something crosses when it already knows exactly how a crossing usually ends for whoever waits at the other side. Between the lantern light and its own shadow, the revenant seems to flicker in and out of being solid at all, as though even it has not fully decided which version of itself intends to finish this particular night. You do not wait for it to decide. You strike first, weapon steady, and hope the dice favor the quick over the patient for once.

Rain drums steady on tin roofs above the guild district tonight, loud enough to cover most sounds except the one you are specifically listening for, the slow scrape of something dragging itself up from a drowned cellar nobody has opened willingly in years. You wait at the top of the cellar stairs, lantern shuttered, letting your ears do the work your eyes cannot manage in this particular dark. Clawed fingers find purchase on stone that remembers every single step ever taken across it, patient, cold, entirely indifferent to the storm rattling shutters three floors above where the guild still meets on nights when nobody remembers what waits below their own floorboards. The climbing takes longer than you expect, unhurried in the way only something with nowhere else to be can afford to be unhurried. It reaches the doorway soaked completely through, coat heavy with cellar water, ribs showing pale where the buttons gape open across a chest that gave up breathing decades before the guild even built this particular hall. For one moment it simply stands in the frame, remembering doors, remembering thresholds, remembering every house it used to be welcome inside before the welcome ran out entirely. Then it steps through anyway, uninvited, and you meet it there because some fights only ever end at the doorway itself, storm or no storm.

The old golem frozen at the crossroads has worn rust for eyes since before anyone currently living in the guild district was born, standing sentinel over cobblestones slick with twenty years of fog nobody bothered clearing. Tonight something moves behind those rusted eyes, cold and patient, and you understand immediately that whatever rides inside that stone shape did not choose it by accident. You keep your distance at first, circling wide, trying to read which way the whole assembly means to lunge once it finally commits to moving. It does not lunge so much as unfold, joints grinding loose after two decades locked in the same position, chains dragging from somewhere beneath the rust as though they grew there naturally instead of being carried in by whatever haunts the stone from the inside out. The revenant riding it, or wearing it, or simply borrowing its shape for one convenient night, turns a hollow face toward you at last and waits, patient as the stone itself, for you to make the first careless mistake of the whole encounter. You do not oblige it. You close the distance yourself, weapon first, and let the golem's rusted joints discover exactly how much unremembered pain a body can still register even after twenty years of standing perfectly, patiently still.

Fog curls up thick from the storm drains along this stretch of the canal district, hiding your own boots from sight and turning the whole alley into a corridor of white you can only navigate by sound and stubbornness. Somewhere inside that white the revenant has been counting your footsteps for the better part of an hour now, patient, cold, closer than the sound alone would ever suggest to anyone unfamiliar with how it hunts. You have learned, over three prior encounters in three different alleys, that stopping is the only move it does not expect from you. Running only ever fed the thing that has haunted these narrow streets for two decades running, and walking simply gives it more time to close whatever distance remains between its patience and your nerve. So you stop, forcing it to close the last of that distance entirely on its own, and the fog finally parts enough to show a coat dragging across cobblestones, ribs showing pale beneath buttons gaping open in a way that has stopped surprising you by now. It does not hesitate once it sees you have stopped running. Chains rattle, cold rolls off it in a slow deliberate wave, and you raise your weapon and meet whatever comes next standing exactly where you chose to make your stand, fog or no fog, patience or no patience.

The gutters have run black for three nights running now, thick with old rain that never properly drained through pipes nobody has cleared since the flood, and somewhere beneath the cobbles something clawed has been dragging itself upward one floor at a time, patient as a bell that has not rung in twenty long years of standing silent in its tower. You hear it long before you see it tonight, boots on rooftops overhead working steadily closer to whichever window it finally means to climb through first, and you have chosen your position accordingly, standing watch beneath the one window on this whole street that still holds a lit candle behind cracked and unwashed glass. It surfaces at last through a broken grate three doors down, coat soaked black with runoff, hollow face tilting up toward that single lit window as though it remembers exactly which house used to be its own before everything about ownership stopped mattering to it entirely. You step between it and the window before it gets the chance to decide anything at all. The fight that follows is short, cold, and settled entirely by whoever lands the first honest hit, which tonight, finally, turns out to be you standing your ground.

Twin lanterns swing from a mooring post nobody has tied a boat to in twenty years, creaking against wind that never seems to actually blow through this particular stretch of the canal district, and between those lanterns the revenant drifts most nights, feet not quite touching the frozen boards it has haunted for longer than most of the guild has been alive to remember. You have watched it circle that empty mooring three times now from a safe distance, always searching, always patient, as if some boat it once expected might still eventually arrive despite every reasonable indication that no boat is coming tonight or any other night for that matter. Tonight you decide to stop watching and start closing the distance yourself. On your approach it notices you at last, and the searching stops all at once, replaced by something colder and far more focused than the aimless circling you observed before. Chains it never needed drag across the frost coating the mooring boards, and its coat, buttoned wrong from collar to hem exactly as it always has been, snaps once in wind you still cannot feel yourself. You grip your weapon, plant your stance, and refuse to let its cold reach any further than the very tip of your blade allows it to reach tonight.

Halfway across the causeway the fog thickens all at once, swallowing the far railing completely and turning what should be a simple crossing into something closer to a corridor with no visible end. Something pale steps out of that whiteness wearing a coat twenty years out of fashion, ribs showing through where the old buttons gape open wide across a chest gone hollow long before you were ever assigned to hunt it. It says nothing at all, the way it never says anything, patient for you to make the first careless mistake of what should have been an ordinary crossing. You have crossed this causeway four times already tonight without incident, and you suspect that is exactly why it finally chose to appear on the fifth, having learned your rhythm well enough to know precisely when your guard would start to slip. You do not oblige it the mistake it is waiting for. You hold your ground at the causeway's midpoint, weapon steady, and let the silence stretch until it decides on its own to close the remaining distance instead of waiting for you to close it first. When it finally moves, it moves all at once, chains dragging soundless across the frozen boards, and the whole fight condenses into three fast exchanges before the fog swallows whichever side loses this particular crossing completely.

Somewhere in the guild district a bell tolls the wrong hour from a tower nobody has climbed in years, its rope long since rotted through and its mechanism frozen by rust nobody bothered repairing after the fire. The revenant answers that wrong hour by drifting closer down the lane, unfinished business trailing behind it like smoke off a snuffed candle wick nobody ever relit properly. You do not run from the sound of it, having learned the hard way three encounters ago that running only ever fed the thing that has hunted these narrow alleys for two decades running now, growing patient rather than reckless with every failed pursuit it has ever attempted against faster prey than you intend to be tonight. It closes the distance at its own unhurried pace, coat dragging, chains dragging, every single part of it built for patience instead of speed the way most things that have already died once eventually become. When it finally reaches striking range the bell tolls again, wrong hour still, and you use that half second of its attention drifting toward the sound to land the first honest hit of the whole encounter. The fight that follows never quite escapes the bell's strange broken rhythm, each exchange landing slightly out of step with where you expect it to fall.

The longsword rusting in the fountain at the plaza's center has sat there since the flood took the whole square under six feet of water, green with age now and half sunk in silt nobody has bothered clearing in all the years since the water finally receded back to where it belonged. Tonight the revenant reaches for that longsword anyway, fingers made of fog closing around a hilt they can no longer actually grip. Its hand passes through the rusted metal twice before something close enough to frustration turns its hollow face toward you instead, as if you have suddenly become the easier target compared to a blade it cannot lift no matter how many decades it spends trying. You recognize the moment for what it is, an opening it did not mean to give you at all. You do not give it time to decide you make a worthier substitute for the sword it just failed to claim. You strike first, weapon steady, closing the distance across the plaza's cracked stones while it is still caught somewhere between frustration and whatever passes for memory in something that stopped needing living hands decades ago. The fountain's still water finally reflects something other than fog and old rust, if only for the length of this one decisive fight.

A blind alley off the canal ends in a locked door, and that is exactly where the revenant has been herding you for the better part of the last three streets, cornering you between crooked brick walls with nothing behind you now but fog and the sound of its slow, deliberate, entirely unhurried approaching feet closing the last of the distance between you both. You test the door once, quick, shoulder against old wood, and find it exactly as locked as you feared it would be given how carefully this whole chase seemed engineered from the very first turn. There is no way out now but through, and you turn to face that fact squarely as the fog thickens further at the alley's only mouth. Chains drag across wet brick behind you, and the particular cold that precedes it settles into the dead end like water finding its own level in a basin too small to hold it properly. You plant your feet against the locked door at your back and meet the revenant on ground it clearly chose for you, because sometimes the only choice left in a fight like this one is simply how hard you intend to make them work for it.

The chandlery on the corner has sat empty since its owner vanished one foggy autumn years before you were ever assigned to this stretch of the guild district, candles still lined up unsold in the dusty window behind cracked glass nobody ever bothered to replace after the owner failed to come back and explain what happened to them. The revenant pauses there most nights, hollow face tilted toward those unsold candles as though it remembers exactly what they were meant for once, long before the cold took over entirely and made warmth an irrelevant concept to whatever it has become since. You have watched this particular pause three times now, always from the same doorway across the street, always wondering what exactly it is trying to remember. Tonight you catch it mid pause, weapon already drawn, and it turns from the window slow and deliberate, chains dragging across a threshold worn smooth by two decades of this same nightly ritual repeated without fail regardless of weather or season. It does not rush you even now, confronted directly for the first time. It simply closes the distance at its own unhurried pace, and you meet it there, right at the door it can apparently never quite bring itself to open on its own.

Beneath the old drawbridge the water runs black and slow, carrying driftwood rafts past a mooring nobody has used since the flood took the last real boat downstream along with half the causeway's original lanterns. Something rides that current toward the bank tonight, patient, cold, dragging chains that catch on nothing but old habit and older rust accumulated over two full decades of exactly this same crossing. You have waited at this exact bank for three nights running, having tracked the pattern of its crossings closely enough to know roughly when the current tends to bring it home. Tonight the pattern holds, and you watch it climb onto the muddy bank soaked completely through, coat heavy, boots leaving no print despite the obvious weight it somehow still carries after all this time. For a moment it simply stands there dripping, remembering currents, remembering crossings, remembering every single trip it made across this exact canal before the last one finally went wrong for good. Then it turns toward you, having noticed your presence at last, and the fight that follows is fast, cold, and entirely unforgiving of hesitation on either side of the muddy bank you have both chosen to make your stand upon tonight.

Fog pours down the chute between two crooked towers tonight, thick enough to swallow the lamplight at street level completely and turn the whole intersection into a corridor of white nobody sane would willingly enter after dark. Out of it steps the revenant, moving with the terrible calm of something that has already died once and found the whole process entirely unremarkable the second time it happened, back when these same towers still stood straight and true against a sky that has not looked the same since. You watch it settle into the alley below from your position at the mouth of the chute, chains dragging soundless across cobblestones slick with decades of accumulated fog, coat buttoned wrong from collar to hem exactly as it always has been in every encounter tracked so far this month. There is no surprise left in this meeting, only the familiar cold and the familiar patience and the sound of a door closing somewhere in a house that burned down long before either of you arrived here. You raise your weapon anyway, because familiar does not mean safe, whatever comfort the routine might suggest to someone less careful than you have learned to be. It meets your approach the way it meets everything now, patient, cold, certain of an outcome it has rehearsed more times than you care to count, and the two of you settle into the fight standing exactly where the fog first brought you together.

The revenant's coat has been buttoned wrong for as long as anyone has tracked it, one hole skipped from collar to hem, as though whoever dressed it on that last cold morning had been in far more of a hurry than they ever wanted anyone else to notice at the time, and no one ever did, not once, not even now, twenty years and counting into whatever this particular haunting has become over that span. You notice the small detail before anything else about it every time you face it, before the chains, before the cold, before the hollow patient face turning slowly to track your approach down whichever alley it has chosen for tonight's encounter. It is almost funny, in a bleak sort of way, that something so far past caring about anything still wears its old mistakes so plainly stitched into the fabric it never bothered fixing. You do not let the almost humor slow your hand this time, however tempting the small absurdity of it might be in the middle of an otherwise deadly serious fight. You strike, weapon finding the gap where the buttons never quite lined up correctly, and the coat finally, mercifully, comes undone along with whatever held the whole haunting together for two decades running through this fogbound stretch of the city.

Behind the guild hall the old well has been dry for decades, capped over by planks nobody thought carefully enough to secure, and yet something climbs out of it tonight anyway, dragging a chain that catches on the bucket rope still coiled uselessly at the rim from whoever drew water here last before the well finally gave up producing anything but echo and cold air. Cold rolls off it in a slow wave that fogs the stones around the wellhead completely, thick enough that you lose sight of your own boots standing barely three steps back from the rim itself. You back further away still, giving yourself proper room to swing without risking a stumble into the very hole this thing just climbed out of. It hauls itself fully upright at last with the unhurried patience of something that has nowhere left to be in any particular hurry, coat dripping well water that should have run dry with everything else down there years and years ago. You meet its first lunge standing well clear of the wellhead itself, weapon steady, because falling into that same dark hole is a mistake you have absolutely no intention of making twice in one single night.

The old canal district goes fully quiet after midnight, every shop shuttered and every window dark except for the one candle nobody ever remembers to blow out on the corner of the abandoned chandlery, burning steady behind glass that has not been cleaned since its owner disappeared one foggy autumn years before you ever walked this particular beat. The revenant favors that exact corner most nights, drifting past the flame like it half remembers what warmth used to mean before the cold became permanent and total, a habit worn into its patrol the way water eventually wears a groove into stone given enough patient, repeated years to accomplish the task properly. Tonight you are waiting there instead of simply walking past on your usual round, weapon drawn, watching the candlelight throw strange shadows across a coat buttoned wrong from collar to hem in the way you have come to expect from every encounter with this particular haunting. It notices you the moment the shadows shift unnaturally against the chandlery wall, and closes the distance without hesitation, chains dragging soundless over cobblestones slick from a rain that stopped falling an hour before you ever arrived to wait for it.

The fog over this quarter of the city never fully lifts, not even at noon, and by lamplight it turns every alley into a corridor with no visible end. You have been following the smell of hot fur and old honey for the better part of an hour, past shuttered guild stalls and a chestnut cart abandoned mid sale, its coals still faintly glowing under a fresh dusting of ash as though whoever tipped it walked away only moments ago. The trail leads you to a courtyard behind the tanners' guild, where a decanter of dark liquor sits untouched on an overturned barrel, offered up like a peace treaty nobody actually signed. You do not touch it. Somewhere close, eyes catch the lamplight wrong, too bright and too golden, watching from just beyond the reach of the light with the patience of something that has never once had to work for its dinner. When the shape finally steps forward, it wears a gentleman's coat over a tiger's face, an arrangement that should look absurd and instead belongs perfectly here, on this rotten and beautiful street. Its hands fold backward at the wrist in a courtier's bow, and it offers you terms in a voice like honey poured slow over gravel. You do not accept the bow, and you do not accept the terms. You draw your weapon instead, and somewhere in the fog behind you, something that has been circling this whole time finally, softly, begins to purr.

Rooftops crowd so close together over this stretch of the city that the sky between them narrows to a single grey seam, fog pooling along its edges like smoke with nowhere else to go. You climb because the street below has gone unnervingly quiet, the particular quiet that settles right before something very large decides to move, and because a fire escape at least offers you a clear line of sight in every direction that matters. At the top, a shape crouches easily on the narrow ledge, entirely unbothered by the height or the rain, a tiger's face turned toward you with an expression that might be curiosity and might simply be hunger wearing a very patient mask. It rises slowly into a gentleman's posture, brushing invisible dust from a coat that somehow stays perfectly dry despite the weather, and offers a bow so practiced it could only have been performed a thousand times before, for a thousand other guests who never left this rooftop the same way they arrived. You grip the wet rail and refuse to look away first, because on a ledge this narrow, looking away is the only mistake that actually matters. It seems to appreciate your composure, tilting its head with something almost like respect, before its tail flicks once, testing the distance between your footing and its own. You do not wait to see what the flick decides. You close the gap yourself, weapon raised, before the choice is made for you by claws instead of conversation.

The sewer stair behind the guildhall breathes warm, wrong air up into the cold night, the kind of warmth that has no business rising from anything as foul as sewage. You go down anyway, one hand trailing the slick brick, boots finding the worn centers of steps countless other feet have used before yours, some of them, you suspect, never coming back up to use them again. At the bottom, a lantern burns behind an iron grate, and behind that grate a shape sits with its knees drawn up, hands folded backward at the wrist in an attitude of patient, courtly waiting. It watches you arrive without surprise, as though your arrival were simply the next item on an evening's itinerary it had already planned in careful detail. It speaks first, voice honey smooth, offering safe passage back to the surface if you simply turn around now and forget this entire visit ever happened. You do not answer, and you do not turn around. The lantern gutters at your silence, as if the thing behind the grate had genuinely hoped you would take the offer and spare it the trouble of a fight. When you raise your weapon instead, its tiger's mouth curls into something that might be disappointment and might be delight, the two expressions apparently sharing the same shape on a face like that. Either way, the grate begins, very slowly, to bend.

Fog rolls off the river and pools thick in the alley behind the fishmongers' stalls, carrying brine and something else beneath it, warm and wrong and steadily closer. A cracked mirror leans against a crate someone left out for collection, and in its glass your reflection stands a half second behind where you actually are, as though it is still catching up to your movements one beat too late for comfort. You do not trust the mirror, and you are right not to. A tiger's shadow slides across the glass behind your reflection, slow and unhurried, though the alley at your back shows nothing at all when your nerve finally breaks and you risk a glance over your shoulder. Somewhere close a low chuckle rides the fog, delighted, entirely unbothered that you noticed the trick. It seems, if anything, pleased that you are paying attention at last. You turn your back on the mirror entirely and face the alley instead, weapon raised, because whatever this is clearly wants your eyes fixed on the wrong thing while the real danger closes from elsewhere. The shape resolves out of the fog a moment later, gentleman's coat, tiger's face, backward hands folded in a bow that manages to look both gracious and mocking at once. You do not return the bow. You answer with steel, and the mirror behind you finally, quietly, cracks the rest of the way through.

The guild seal on the courtyard door has been snapped clean in half, its wax still faintly warm, and beyond the broken lock the hall sits dark except for a single guttering candle someone left burning on purpose, as if to make certain you would find your way in rather than turn back at the threshold. Coins are scattered across the floor in a trail too neat to be an accident, each one placed with the precision of a lure rather than the carelessness of a robbery. You follow the coins because standing in the dark waiting for whatever scattered them to come find you first is a worse plan by every measure you can think of. The trail leads past the guildmaster's overturned chair to a shape rising slowly from behind it, backward hands folding into a courtly greeting, a tiger's face arranging itself into something almost like an apology. It is not sorry, not remotely, and you can tell by the way it keeps smiling even as it speaks the words. It calls the coins a small hospitality, an honest gesture of goodwill before the real business begins. Honest is the last word this thing has earned tonight, and you tell it so with your weapon rather than your voice, stepping past every coin without slowing. The candle gutters as you move, throwing the shape's shadow twice as tall across the guildhall wall, backward hands stretching wide before you close the distance yourself.

Rain slicks the cobbles until every surface in the tanners' quarter gleams with borrowed lamplight, and the alley behind the tannery reeks of curing hide and hot fur in a combination thick enough to turn your stomach before you have even glimpsed the source. Claw marks score the doorframe ahead at eye height, four parallel gouges deep enough to catch a careless boot heel, as though something paused to measure a previous visitor for a fitting it never finished. You step past the marks anyway, because turning back means admitting the fog has already decided how tonight goes. Behind the door, low and pleased, something purrs at the sound of your footsteps, utterly certain of its welcome. When the door swings open on its own, unlatched from within, a gentleman's silhouette stands waiting in the lamplight, backward hands already folded, offering a bow you have no intention of returning in kind. It thanks you for coming so promptly, as though you ever had any real choice in the matter once the claw marks pointed the way. You answer by raising your weapon instead of a greeting, and the purring behind it deepens into something closer to a growl, low and delighted, the sound of a very patient predator finally getting to skip the pleasantries and move straight to the part of the evening it actually came for.

Between one guttering lamppost and the next, the fog thickens into something with a shape, reversed hands folding into a courtier's bow so smooth it could only have been practiced for centuries of evenings exactly like this one. It speaks your name wrong on purpose, testing the exact shape of your flinch the way a tailor tests the fit of a hem before committing to the final stitch. The cobbles underfoot are slick with old rain and older grime, and you plant your feet carefully before answering with steel instead of words, because words are clearly the game it wants to play and steel is the only language you intend to speak tonight. It does not seem surprised by your silence; if anything, the tiger's mouth curls a little wider, as though silence was the exact answer it had been hoping for all along, better than anything you could have said. Somewhere behind it, a second lamppost gutters out entirely on its own, plunging half the alley into a dark that feels suddenly, deliberately, much larger than it was only a moment before.
